curious_fact: BKEngine was one of the first engines to support real-time physics simulations directly within the engine, setting a new standard for indie developers.

name: BLISS
description: BLISS is a high-level programming language developed at MIT in the 1970s. It was designed for systems programming and is known for its efficiency and flexibility. BLISS is a compiled language that targets various architectures, including PDP-10 and VAX.
connectionsExplanation: BLISS evolved from the earlier MACRO-10 assembly language, which was used for system programming on the PDP-10. It was designed to provide a higher level of abstraction while maintaining the performance and control needed for systems programming.
curious_fact: BLISS was one of the first languages to support nested procedures and block structure.

name: BNR Prolog
description: BNR Prolog is a Prolog implementation developed at the BNR (Bergen University of Technology and Economics) in the Netherlands. It is known for its efficient execution and support for concurrent programming.
connectionsExplanation: BNR Prolog evolved from traditional Prolog systems, inheriting the declarative paradigm and logical programming features. It was designed to address the limitations of earlier Prolog implementations, particularly in terms of performance and concurrency support.
curious_fact: BNR Prolog was one of the first Prolog systems to support concurrent execution, making it a pioneering system in its time.

name: BPELscript
description: BPELscript is a scripting language designed to simplify the creation of Business Process Execution Language (BPEL) processes. It aims to provide a more intuitive and accessible way to define business processes compared to the XML-based BPEL.
connectionsExplanation: BPELscript was developed to enhance the usability of BPEL by offering a scripting interface. It is not a direct descendant of any traditional programming language but is inspired by the need to streamline the creation of complex business processes.
curious_fact: BPELscript was one of the early attempts to make BPEL more accessible to developers without extensive XML knowledge.

name: BQN
description: BQN is a modern array programming language designed by Myles McDonnell. It aims to be concise and expressive, drawing inspiration from APL and J. BQN is known for its unique syntax and powerful array manipulation capabilities.
connectionsExplanation: BQN is inspired by APL and J, both of which are influential array programming languages. While it shares some conceptual similarities with these predecessors, BQN introduces its own unique syntax and features, making it a distinct language in the array programming paradigm.
curious_fact: BQN's syntax is designed to be minimalistic and expressive, allowing for complex operations to be written in a few characters.

name: BSASPEC
description: BSASPEC is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It is a hypothetical language that showcases a unique syntax and structure, intended to illustrate concepts in computer science and programming theory.
connectionsExplanation: BSASPEC is a fictional language created for educational and illustrative purposes. It does not have a direct historical connection to any real programming language. Its design is inspired by various paradigms to demonstrate different programming concepts.
curious_fact: BSASPEC was invented to help students understand the fundamentals of programming by providing a simple yet distinct syntax.

name: Babbage
description: Babbage is a theoretical programming language inspired by Charles Babbage's designs for the Analytical Engine, a mechanical general-purpose computer. It is a fictional language used to explore the concepts of early computing.
connectionsExplanation: Babbage is a conceptual language based on Charles Babbage's designs for the Analytical Engine, which predates modern computers by over a century. It serves as a bridge between the mechanical computing concepts of the 19th century and modern programming languages.
curious_fact: Babbage's Analytical Engine was designed to perform any computation that could be expressed in an algorithm, making it a precursor to modern computers.

name: Babymodula-3
description: Babymodula-3 is a fictional esoteric programming language designed for educational purposes. It features a simple syntax and is often used in programming language theory courses to illustrate basic concepts.
connectionsExplanation: Babymodula-3 is inspired by the Modula-2 language, which was developed in the 1970s. It shares some structural similarities but is simplified for educational use, making it an interesting subject for studying language design and syntax.
curious_fact: Babymodula-3 was created to demonstrate the evolution of programming languages from complex to simple.

name: Baltie
description: Baltie is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It was created to introduce beginners to programming concepts without overwhelming them with complex syntax.
connectionsExplanation: Baltie was inspired by the simplicity of BASIC and the structured approach of Pascal, aiming to provide a gentle learning curve for new programmers.
curious_fact: Baltie was created by a group of Finnish educators to make programming accessible to children.

name: BasicForQt
description: BasicForQt is a dialect of BASIC designed to work seamlessly with the Qt framework, allowing developers to leverage the simplicity of BASIC while building cross-platform applications.
connectionsExplanation: BasicForQt was developed to provide a more accessible entry point for developers familiar with BASIC to transition into Qt application development. It inherits the simplicity and ease of use from BASIC and integrates with the powerful features of the Qt framework, making it a unique tool in the developer's toolkit.

moons.name: Qt Creator Integration
moons.description: BasicForQt includes built-in support for Qt Creator, providing a seamless development environment for Qt applications.

curious_fact: BasicForQt was one of the first BASIC dialects to support modern GUI development, bridging the gap between simplicity and power.

name: BasicSoundExtension
description: BasicSoundExtension is an esoteric programming language designed to extend the capabilities of the BASIC programming language with sound synthesis. It allows users to generate and manipulate sound directly within BASIC programs, making it unique among BASIC dialects.
connectionsExplanation: BasicSoundExtension was created as an extension to BASIC, a widely-used programming language in the 1980s and early 1990s. It aims to integrate sound synthesis capabilities, which were not natively supported by standard BASIC implementations. This extension was inspired by the desire to enhance multimedia applications and educational tools with audio capabilities.
curious_fact: BasicSoundExtension uses a unique syntax for sound commands, allowing for complex audio manipulations within BASIC programs.

name: Basic4GL
description: Basic4GL is a dialect of BASIC designed for game development. It extends the capabilities of traditional BASIC by adding support for graphics, sound, and game-specific functions, making it easier to create interactive games.
connectionsExplanation: Basic4GL evolved from the BASIC programming language, specifically targeting game development. It builds upon the simplicity and ease of use of BASIC while incorporating features necessary for game programming, such as graphics and sound support.
curious_fact: Basic4GL was developed by Glenn Howes, who also created the BASIC-256 language.

name: Basic4android
description: Basic4android is a programming language designed for developing Android applications. It is based on the BASIC programming language and is known for its simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible to beginners.
connectionsExplanation: Basic4android evolved from the BASIC programming language, which was one of the first high-level programming languages. It was designed to be user-friendly and accessible, which aligns with the principles of BASIC. The language's focus on Android development makes it a specialized tool in the mobile app development ecosystem.

moons.name: Basic4iOS
moons.description: A version of Basic4android for iOS development.

curious_fact: Basic4android was developed by Anywhere Software, which also created Basic4ppc for Windows Mobile.
example:
Sub Process_Globals
    'These global variables will be declared once when the application starts.
    'These variables can be accessed from all modules.
End Sub

Sub Globals
    'These global variables will be redeclared each time the activity is created.
    'These variables can only be accessed from this module.
End Sub

Sub Activity_Create(FirstTime As Boolean)
    'Do not forget to load the layout file created with the visual designer. For example:
    Activity.LoadLayout("Main")
End Sub

Sub Activity_Resume

End Sub

Sub Activity_Pause (UserClosed As Boolean)

End Sub

name: Basic4ppc
description: Basic4ppc is a BASIC dialect designed for programming Pocket PC and Windows CE devices. It offers a simple syntax and is known for its ease of use in developing applications for mobile platforms.
connectionsExplanation: Basic4ppc evolved from earlier BASIC dialects, particularly those used in the early days of personal computing. It is closely related to Visual Basic, as it shares many syntax similarities and is designed for a similar purpose, but targets mobile devices instead of desktops.
curious_fact: Basic4ppc was one of the first languages to popularize BASIC on mobile devices, making it easier for developers to create applications for Pocket PCs.
example:
Sub App_Start
    Msgbox("Hello World")
End Sub

name: BasicX
description: BasicX is a dialect of the BASIC programming language designed for educational purposes, particularly for beginners. It was developed in the early 1980s to introduce programming concepts in a simple and accessible manner.
connectionsExplanation: BasicX evolved from the original BASIC language, which was developed in the late 1950s and early 1960s. It shares many similarities with other BASIC dialects like QuickBASIC and Visual Basic, but with additional features tailored for educational use.
curious_fact: BasicX was one of the first programming languages to introduce graphical user interfaces for beginners.

name: BeanShell
description: BeanShell is a small, embeddable Java source interpreter with object scripting language features. It is designed to be a lightweight alternative to Java for scripting tasks.
connectionsExplanation: BeanShell was developed as a scripting language for Java applications, providing a way to execute Java code dynamically. It is inspired by the simplicity of scripting languages like JavaScript and Perl, but it is tightly integrated with the Java ecosystem.
curious_fact: BeanShell was one of the first scripting languages to allow Java code to be executed dynamically within a Java application.

name: Beatnik
description: Beatnik is an esoteric programming language created in 2000 by David Morgan-Mar. It is designed to be difficult to read and write, with syntax inspired by the beat poetry of the 1950s. The language is not widely used and is primarily known for its unique approach to programming.
connectionsExplanation: Beatnik was created as a tribute to the Beat Generation poets of the 1950s. It draws inspiration from the free-form and unconventional nature of their work, translating that into a programming language that challenges traditional syntax and readability.
curious_fact: Beatnik uses a stack-based architecture and its syntax is based on the rhythm and flow of spoken word poetry.

name: Beef
description: Beef is a programming language designed to be a modern, high-level alternative to C++. It aims to provide a more intuitive syntax and better safety features while maintaining performance. Despite its innovative features, Beef has not gained widespread adoption.
connectionsExplanation: Beef was created as an attempt to modernize C++ by introducing a more concise syntax and improved safety features. It draws inspiration from C++ but aims to simplify the language for easier development and maintenance.
curious_fact: Beef introduced the concept of 'beefified' syntax, which aims to make C++ code more readable and less error-prone.

name: Befunge
description: Befunge is a two-dimensional, stack-based esoteric programming language. Unlike most programming languages, Befunge's code is laid out on a two-dimensional grid, and the instruction pointer can move in four directions: up, down, left, and right.
connectionsExplanation: Befunge was created by Chris Pressey in 1993. It is an esoteric language that does not have a direct predecessor but is inspired by the concept of two-dimensional programming. Its unique approach to code layout and execution has influenced other esoteric languages like 2Pak and ><>.
curious_fact: Befunge's instruction pointer can move in four directions, making it unique among programming languages.

name: Behavioral Description Language
description: Behavioral Description Language (BDL) is a specialized programming language designed for the specification and simulation of digital systems. It focuses on describing the behavior of hardware components rather than their implementation details.
connectionsExplanation: BDL was developed as part of the Hardware Description Language (HDL) family, which includes VHDL and Verilog. It was influenced by the need for a language that could accurately model the behavior of digital circuits, making it easier for designers to simulate and verify their designs before physical implementation.
curious_fact: BDL was one of the first languages to introduce behavioral modeling, allowing designers to focus on the functionality of circuits rather than their physical implementation.
example:
PROCESS example_process IS
BEGIN
    -- Behavioral description of the process
END PROCESS;

name: Bertrand
description: Bertrand is a fictional programming language created for educational purposes. It is designed to be simple and easy to understand, making it suitable for beginners. Despite its simplicity, Bertrand incorporates some advanced concepts to challenge more experienced programmers.
connectionsExplanation: Bertrand was created as an educational tool and does not have a direct historical lineage. It is inspired by the simplicity of BASIC and the structured approach of Pascal, but it introduces unique features to encourage deeper understanding of programming concepts.
curious_fact: Bertrand was named after the mathematician Bertrand Russell, known for his work in logic and philosophy.

name: Bidule
description: Bidule is a visual programming environment for creating audio software. It allows users to connect modules to process audio signals, making it unique in the field of digital audio workstations.
connectionsExplanation: Bidule was developed by Robert Poor, a French software developer. It is not directly derived from any traditional programming language but is inspired by the concept of modular synthesis and visual programming paradigms.
curious_fact: Bidule supports real-time audio processing and can be used to create complex audio effects and synthesizers.
example: Create a simple audio delay effect by connecting a Delay module to an Input and Output.

name: Biological Expression Language
description: Biological Expression Language (BEL) is a domain-specific language designed to represent biological knowledge in a structured and machine-readable format. It is used primarily in bioinformatics to encode relationships between biological entities such as genes, proteins, and diseases.
connectionsExplanation: BEL was developed to address the need for a standardized language to represent complex biological relationships. It draws inspiration from logic programming and is used in conjunction with tools like PyBEL for network analysis.
curious_fact: BEL uses a syntax that resembles natural language, making it easier for biologists to express complex biological concepts.
example: SET protein AKT1 is orthologous to protein AKT1_HUMAN

name: BirkbeckAssembler
description: BirkbeckAssembler is a fictional assembly language created for educational purposes at Birkbeck College, London. It is designed to teach the fundamentals of computer architecture and assembly language programming.
connectionsExplanation: BirkbeckAssembler was developed as a teaching tool at Birkbeck College in the early 1960s. It is not based on any real-world assembly language but is inspired by the principles of early assembly languages like IBM 360 Assembler and Motorola 68000 Assembler.
curious_fact: BirkbeckAssembler was created to simplify the teaching of assembly language concepts to students.

name: Bistro
description: Bistro is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It was created to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of more advanced languages.
connectionsExplanation: Bistro was inspired by the simplicity and elegance of Python, aiming to provide a more accessible entry point for those new to programming. It shares some syntactical similarities with Python but is designed to be even more beginner-friendly.
curious_fact: Bistro was created by a group of educators in France to make programming more approachable for students.

name: Bla
description: Bla is a fictional programming language created for illustrative purposes. It is designed to demonstrate the structure of a JSON object for a programming language entry.
connectionsExplanation: Bla is a fictional language created for this example. It does not have any historical connections or parent languages.
curious_fact: Bla was invented to showcase the JSON structure for language entries.

name: BlitzBASIC
description: BlitzBASIC is a high-level programming language designed for game development and multimedia applications. It is known for its ease of use and fast compilation times, making it popular among hobbyists and small developers.
connectionsExplanation: BlitzBASIC evolved from BASIC, specifically QBasic, and was designed to be more efficient and user-friendly for game development. It introduced features like inline assembly and fast graphics routines that were not available in standard BASIC dialects.

moons.name: Blitz3D
moons.description: An extension of BlitzBASIC for 3D game development.

moons.name: BlitzMax
moons.description: A modern successor to BlitzBASIC with improved performance and additional features.

curious_fact: BlitzBASIC was one of the first languages to popularize the use of inline assembly in high-level languages, allowing developers to optimize performance critical sections of code.

name: Blitz2D
description: Blitz2D is a 2D game development environment based on the BlitzBasic programming language. It provides a simple and intuitive syntax for creating games and multimedia applications.
connectionsExplanation: Blitz2D evolved from BlitzBasic, which was developed by Michael Bukowski. BlitzBasic was designed to be accessible for beginners while still offering powerful features for game development. Blitz2D extends this capability specifically for 2D game development, maintaining the simplicity and ease of use of its predecessor.
curious_fact: Blitz2D was one of the first environments to popularize the concept of 'game creation for beginners' with its simple syntax and powerful tools.

name: Blitz3D
description: Blitz3D is a high-level programming language designed for game development, offering a simple syntax and powerful graphics capabilities. It was developed by Michael Bukowski and released in 1998. Despite its age, Blitz3D remains popular among hobbyists and small indie developers for its ease of use and fast development cycle.
connectionsExplanation: Blitz3D evolved from BlitzBasic, which itself was inspired by QBasic. It shares a lineage with other BASIC dialects but introduces advanced features suitable for 3D game development, making it a specialized language in the gaming community.

moons.name: BlitzMax
moons.description: BlitzMax is a modern successor to Blitz3D, offering improved performance and additional features while maintaining compatibility with Blitz3D code.

curious_fact: Blitz3D was one of the first languages to popularize the concept of 'code generation' for game development, allowing developers to write high-level code that was then compiled into optimized machine code.
example:
Graphics3D
SetBuffer BackBuffer()
Cls
Camera 320, 240, 1000, 0, 0, 0
Light 0, 0, 0, 1000
EntityBox 0, 0, 0, 100, 100, 100
RenderWorld
Flip

name: BlitzMax
description: BlitzMax is a high-level programming language designed for game development and multimedia applications. It offers a simple syntax and a powerful graphics API, making it accessible for beginners while still being capable of handling complex projects.
connectionsExplanation: BlitzMax evolved from BlitzBasic, which was itself inspired by QBasic. It maintains the simplicity and ease of use of its predecessors while introducing new features and improvements, particularly in the realm of game development.

moons.name: Blitz3D
moons.description: A 3D graphics library for BlitzMax, providing advanced rendering capabilities.

moons.name: BlitzMaxNG
moons.description: A modern fork of BlitzMax, offering improved performance and additional features.

curious_fact: BlitzMax was developed by Andrew Stacey, who also created the popular game 'Bloxorz'.

name: BlitzPlus
description: BlitzPlus is a high-level programming language designed for game development and multimedia applications. It offers a simple syntax and a powerful integrated development environment (IDE) to help users create games and multimedia content quickly.
connectionsExplanation: BlitzPlus evolved from BlitzBasic, which itself was inspired by QBasic. It was designed to be accessible to beginners while still offering the power needed for professional game development.
curious_fact: BlitzPlus was one of the first languages to popularize the concept of 'game creation systems' in the early 2000s.

name: Blockly
description: Blockly is a visual programming editor developed by Google. It allows users to create programs by connecting blocks together, making it accessible for beginners and children. It is particularly popular in educational settings for teaching programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: Blockly was developed by Google as a tool to make programming more accessible, especially for children and beginners. It is not a direct descendant of any traditional programming language but is inspired by the concept of visual programming languages like Scratch. Its development was influenced by the need to simplify the learning curve of programming, making it a unique tool in the educational technology landscape.
curious_fact: Blockly was originally designed for Google's App Inventor, which aimed to allow users to create Android apps without writing code.

name: BlooP and FlooP
description: BlooP and FlooP are fictional programming languages created by Douglas Hofstadter in his book 'Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Golden Braid'. They are designed to illustrate the concept of recursion and the limitations of certain types of computational systems.
connectionsExplanation: BlooP and FlooP are conceptual languages used to discuss recursion and computational limits in theoretical computer science. They are not directly derived from any existing language but are inspired by the study of formal systems and logic.
curious_fact: BlooP can express any computable function that is total, but it cannot express functions that are not total, such as the halting problem.
example:
BlooP: (define (BlooP n) (if (= n 0) 1 (* n (BlooP (- n 1)))))
FlooP: (define (FlooP n) (if (= n 0) 1 (* n (FlooP (- n 1)))))

name: Bloom
description: Bloom is a modern, high-level programming language designed for rapid application development. It emphasizes simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible to beginners while still offering powerful features for experienced developers.
connectionsExplanation: Bloom was inspired by the paradigms of Python and JavaScript, aiming to combine their strengths into a single, cohesive language. It was developed by a team of researchers at a leading university in the United States, focusing on creating a language that could bridge the gap between scripting and traditional programming.

moons.name: BloomScript
moons.description: A lightweight scripting language built on top of Bloom, designed for web development.

moons.name: BloomLib
moons.description: A collection of libraries and tools for Bloom, enhancing its functionality and ease of use.

curious_fact: Bloom was named after the bloom of a flower, symbolizing growth and development in programming.

name: Blub
description: Blub is a fictional programming language created by Paul Graham in his essay 'On Lisp'. It is designed to be a simple and easy-to-use language, often used as a teaching tool to illustrate the concept of the 'Blub Paradox'.
connectionsExplanation: Blub is a fictional language created by Paul Graham to illustrate the 'Blub Paradox', which discusses the limitations of programmers who are comfortable with their own language and are unaware of more powerful languages.
curious_fact: Blub is a fictional language created to highlight the limitations of programmers who are comfortable with their own language.

name: Blue
description: Blue is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It was created to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of existing languages.
connectionsExplanation: Blue was inspired by the simplicity and clarity of Python, aiming to provide a more accessible learning experience for newcomers to programming.
curious_fact: Blue was developed by a team of educators to make programming more approachable for students.

name: Bluespec
description: Bluespec is a hardware description language (HDL) used for designing and verifying digital systems. It is particularly known for its high-level, functional approach to hardware design, which allows for more concise and readable code compared to traditional HDLs like VHDL or Verilog.
connectionsExplanation: Bluespec was developed by Bluespec Inc., a company founded by MIT researchers. It is based on the paradigm of hardware synthesis from high-level functional specifications, which is a significant departure from traditional HDLs that focus on low-level gate-level descriptions. This approach allows designers to express hardware designs in a more abstract and declarative manner, facilitating better verification and synthesis processes.
curious_fact: Bluespec was one of the first HDLs to support formal verification directly within the language, allowing designers to specify and prove properties of their hardware designs.

name: Inc.
description: Inc. is an esoteric programming language created by Chris Pressey in 2012. It is known for its minimalistic design and the challenge it poses to programmers. The language is Turing-complete but is designed to be as simple as possible, making it a popular choice for code golfing and minimalist programming challenges.
connectionsExplanation: Inc. is an esoteric programming language that does not have a direct historical predecessor. It is inspired by the minimalist philosophy of esoteric languages and is part of the broader category of esoteric programming languages that challenge programmers to think creatively and efficiently.
curious_fact: Inc. is one of the few esoteric languages that can be implemented in a single instruction.

name: Boehmunnamedcodingsystem
description: Boehmunnamedcodingsystem is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It features a unique syntax and is intended to illustrate concepts of programming language design.
connectionsExplanation: Boehmunnamedcodingsystem is a fictional language created for educational purposes and does not have a direct historical connection to any existing language. It is inspired by the need to demonstrate programming language design principles in a simplified manner.
curious_fact: Boehmunnamedcodingsystem was invented by Dr. Ulf Boehmunn to teach programming concepts in a fun and engaging way.

name: Bogus
description: Bogus is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It features a unique syntax and is not intended for practical use.
connectionsExplanation: Bogus was created as a hypothetical language to illustrate concepts in programming language design and syntax. It has no historical connections to any existing languages.
curious_fact: Bogus was invented by a group of computer science students to demonstrate the complexity of language design.

name: Bon
description: Bon is a functional programming language designed by Paul Hudak and others at Yale University. It is a dialect of Haskell, focusing on simplicity and efficiency. Bon is known for its use in teaching and research.
connectionsExplanation: Bon is a dialect of Haskell, which itself is a direct descendant of Miranda. Miranda was influenced by the functional programming paradigm, which traces back to the lambda calculus developed by Alonzo Church in the 1930s. Therefore, Bon's lineage can be traced through Haskell to Miranda and ultimately to the lambda calculus.
curious_fact: Bon was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of 'lazy evaluation' in a practical setting.

name: Boo
description: Boo is a statically typed, object-oriented programming language designed to be easy to learn and use. It is a .NET language that compiles to Common Intermediate Language (CIL) and is influenced by Python, Java, and C#.
connectionsExplanation: Boo was developed as a .NET language with the goal of providing a more Pythonic syntax while maintaining the performance and capabilities of the .NET framework. It was influenced by Python's simplicity and readability, making it a unique addition to the .NET ecosystem.
curious_fact: Boo was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of 'duck typing' in a statically typed language.

name: Boomerang
description: Boomerang is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It was created to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of more advanced languages.
connectionsExplanation: Boomerang was inspired by the simplicity and readability of BASIC, aiming to retain its user-friendly nature while incorporating modern programming paradigms. It was developed as part of a curriculum to teach programming to high school students.
curious_fact: Boomerang uses a unique syntax that resembles natural language, making it easier for beginners to understand.

name: Bosque
description: Bosque is a modern programming language designed to be simple, safe, and expressive. It emphasizes immutability and functional programming principles, aiming to reduce bugs and improve code readability.
connectionsExplanation: Bosque was developed by Microsoft as part of their efforts to create a safer and more efficient programming language. It draws inspiration from functional programming languages like Haskell and Scala, as well as modern language features seen in languages like TypeScript and Rust.
curious_fact: Bosque is one of the few languages to use the term 'moon' to refer to its libraries and frameworks, reflecting its modular design philosophy.

name: Bourne Shell
description: The Bourne Shell (sh) is a command-line interpreter developed by Stephen Bourne at Bell Labs. It was the first Unix shell to be widely used and became the standard shell for Unix-like operating systems. It introduced many features that are still used in modern shells.
connectionsExplanation: The Bourne Shell was developed as a replacement for the earlier Thompson Shell and introduced many features that influenced subsequent shell development. It became the standard shell for Unix-like systems and laid the groundwork for later shells like the C Shell and Bash.

moons.name: C Shell
moons.description: The C Shell (csh) is a command-line shell for Unix-like operating systems, developed by Bill Joy at UC Berkeley. It is known for its C-like syntax and job control features.

moons.name: Bash
moons.description: Bash (Bourne Again SHell) is a Unix shell and command language interpreter. It is the default shell for many Linux distributions and is a superset of the Bourne Shell.

curious_fact: The Bourne Shell was the first shell to support shell functions and command substitution.

name: Brainfuck
description: Brainfuck is an esoteric programming language known for its minimalistic design, consisting of only eight simple commands. Despite its simplicity, Brainfuck is Turing-complete, meaning it can theoretically compute anything computable given enough time and memory.
connectionsExplanation: Brainfuck was created by Urban Müller in 1993 as a thought experiment to challenge and amuse programmers. It has no direct ancestors but is often considered a descendant of the esoteric programming language 'befunge', which shares similar minimalist principles.
curious_fact: Brainfuck is one of the smallest programming languages, with a compiler that can be written in just a few hundred bytes.

name: BriefCode
description: BriefCode is a modern, minimalist programming language designed for rapid development and concise syntax. It emphasizes simplicity and efficiency, making it ideal for small-scale projects and scripting tasks.
connectionsExplanation: BriefCode was inspired by the desire to create a language that combines the ease of scripting with the performance of compiled languages. It draws influences from languages like Python and C, aiming to provide a balance between readability and execution speed.

moons.name: BriefLib
moons.description: A standard library for BriefCode, providing essential functions and utilities.

curious_fact: BriefCode was developed in just 6 months by a team of three developers.

name: Brutus2D
description: Brutus2D is a fictional programming language designed for 2D game development, emphasizing simplicity and ease of use. It is inspired by BASIC and C, aiming to provide a straightforward syntax for beginners.
connectionsExplanation: Brutus2D was created as a modern take on classic game development languages. It draws inspiration from BASIC for its simplicity and from C for its efficiency, making it a unique blend of these paradigms.
curious_fact: Brutus2D was developed in a weekend hackathon and gained popularity among indie game developers.

name: Bubble
description: Bubble is a visual programming language designed for building web and mobile applications without writing code. It uses a drag-and-drop interface to create applications, making it accessible to users with little to no programming experience.
connectionsExplanation: Bubble was created by a French company and is not directly derived from any traditional programming language. It operates on a visual paradigm, allowing users to design applications by connecting blocks rather than writing code. This makes it distinct from other languages and sets it apart in the realm of visual programming tools.
curious_fact: Bubble was one of the first visual programming languages to gain significant traction among non-programmers.
example: Create a button with the text 'Hello World' and set its action to display an alert with the message 'Hello, World!'.

name: BusinessApplicationLanguage
description: BusinessApplicationLanguage (BAL) is a specialized programming language designed for business and administrative applications. It was developed in the early 1980s to simplify the creation of business software, making it accessible to non-programmers. BAL is known for its straightforward syntax and focus on business logic.
connectionsExplanation: BusinessApplicationLanguage was developed in the early 1980s as a response to the growing demand for business software that could be used by non-technical personnel. It was influenced by the need to automate routine business tasks and reduce the dependency on manual processes. BAL aimed to bridge the gap between business needs and programming capabilities by providing a language that was easy to learn and use for those without a technical background.
curious_fact: BAL was one of the first languages to introduce a graphical user interface for programming, making it more accessible to non-technical users.

name: Business Intelligence Markup Language
description: Business Intelligence Markup Language (BIML) is a specialized XML-based language designed to automate the creation of SQL Server Integration Services (SSIS) packages. It simplifies the process of building complex data integration solutions by allowing developers to define SSIS packages using a declarative syntax.
connectionsExplanation: BIML was developed by Varigence, a Microsoft partner, to address the complexity and time-consuming nature of creating SSIS packages manually. It leverages the XML format, which is well-documented and widely supported, to provide a structured way of defining SSIS components and workflows.
curious_fact: BIML was one of the first languages to use XML for automating SSIS package creation, significantly reducing development time and effort.
example:
<Biml xmlns="http://schemas.varigence.com/biml.xsd">
    <Packages>
        <Package Name="HelloWorldPackage" ConstraintMode="Linear">
            <Tasks>
                <ExecuteSQL ConnectionName="AdventureWorksDW2017">
                    <DirectInput>SELECT 'Hello, World!' AS Message</DirectInput>
                </ExecuteSQL>
            </Tasks>
        </Package>
    </Packages>
</Biml>

name: Business Process Execution Language
description: Business Process Execution Language (BPEL) is an XML-based language used for defining business processes. It is part of the Web Services Interoperability Organization (WS-I) specifications and is designed to work with web services.
connectionsExplanation: BPEL is derived from the WSFL (Web Services Flow Language) and is part of the WS-I Basic Profile. It was developed to provide a standard way to define and execute business processes using web services, making it a key component in the evolution of enterprise integration and workflow automation.
curious_fact: BPEL was one of the first languages to standardize business process execution, paving the way for modern workflow management systems.
example:
define process name="HelloWorldProcess" targetNamespace="http://www.example.com/bpel/helloworld"

  partnerLinks
  
  variables
    variable name="greeting" type="xsd:string"
  
  sequence
    assign
      to variable="greeting"
      from literal="Hello, World!"
    
    invoke
      partnerLink="greetingService"
      operation="sendGreeting"
      inputVariable="greeting"
    
  

end process

name: C Intermediate Language
description: C Intermediate Language (CIL) is a low-level programming language designed to be a portable intermediate representation for the Common Language Infrastructure (CLI). It is used by compilers to generate code for the .NET framework.
connectionsExplanation: CIL was developed to provide a common intermediate language for .NET languages, allowing for interoperability and code sharing. It is a direct descendant of C and is influenced by the C programming language, which it aims to be a more structured and safer version of.
curious_fact: CIL is not directly executable; it must be compiled to native code by a Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler.

name: Cshell
description: Cshell, or C Shell, is a command language interpreter for the Unix operating system. It was developed by Bill Joy at the University of California, Berkeley, as part of the BSD Unix distribution. Cshell is known for its C-like syntax and features such as command history, job control, and aliasing.
connectionsExplanation: Cshell was developed as a successor to the Bourne shell (sh) and was influenced by the C programming language. It introduced features like command history, job control, and aliasing, which were later adopted by other shells like tcsh. Cshell's syntax and features have had a significant impact on the evolution of Unix shells.
curious_fact: Cshell was one of the first shells to support command history, a feature that has become standard in modern command-line interfaces.

name: C
description: C is a general-purpose, procedural computer programming language supporting structured programming, lexical variable scope, and recursion. It was originally developed by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs in the early 1970s.
connectionsExplanation: C was developed at Bell Labs in the early 1970s and is considered the successor to the B programming language. It has influenced many other languages, including C++, Java, and Python.

moons.name: C++
moons.description: C++ is an extension of C that adds support for object-oriented programming.

moons.name: C#
moons.description: C# is a modern, object-oriented language developed by Microsoft.

moons.name: Objective-C
moons.description: Objective-C is a superset of C that adds Smalltalk-style messaging to C.

curious_fact: C is the most widely used programming language in the world.

name: C++/CLI
description: C++/CLI (C++/Common Language Infrastructure) is a language extension that integrates C++ with the Common Language Infrastructure (CLI), which is the runtime environment for the .NET Framework. It allows developers to write code that can interoperate with other .NET languages and access the rich set of libraries provided by the .NET ecosystem.
connectionsExplanation: C++/CLI was developed by Microsoft as an extension to C++ to enable it to work seamlessly with the .NET Framework. It combines the performance and control of C++ with the managed code features of the .NET environment, making it a powerful tool for developers who need to leverage both native and managed code.
curious_fact: C++/CLI introduced the concept of 'interoperability' between native and managed code, allowing developers to use both paradigms in a single application.

name: C++/CX
description: C++/CX is a language extension for C++ that provides a syntax for writing Windows Runtime (WinRT) components. It is primarily used for developing applications for Windows 8 and later.
connectionsExplanation: C++/CX was introduced by Microsoft as an extension to C++ to facilitate the development of Windows Runtime components. It builds upon the C++ language, adding syntax and features specifically tailored for Windows development, making it a direct descendant of C++.
curious_fact: C++/CX introduced new keywords like 'ref', 'internal', and 'public sealed' to support WinRT component development.
example: ref class HelloWorld { public: void SayHello() { System::Console::WriteLine(L"Hello, World!"); } };

name: C--
description: C-- is a programming language designed to be a safer and more modern alternative to C. It introduces features like automatic memory management and type safety, while maintaining the performance and low-level control of C.
connectionsExplanation: C-- was developed as an attempt to address some of the safety and usability issues found in C, particularly in the context of system programming and embedded systems. It builds upon the C language, aiming to provide a more robust environment for developers while preserving the efficiency and control that C offers.
curious_fact: C-- was one of the early attempts to create a safer C-like language, but it did not gain widespread adoption.

name: C/AL
description: C/AL, short for C/Active, is a procedural programming language developed by Microsoft for use in Microsoft Dynamics NAV (now known as Dynamics 365 Business Central). It is designed to be used for customizing and extending the functionality of the Dynamics NAV platform.
connectionsExplanation: C/AL is derived from C and is specifically tailored for the Microsoft Dynamics NAV environment. It inherits the procedural programming paradigm from C, making it suitable for business logic development within the Dynamics NAV system.
curious_fact: C/AL was originally called C/SIDE, which stood for C/Software Development Environment.

name: C2
description: C2 is a programming language designed to be a modern, safe, and efficient alternative to C. It aims to provide a more robust and safer environment for system programming while maintaining the performance and low-level control of C.
connectionsExplanation: C2 was developed as an evolution of C, aiming to address some of the safety and usability issues present in the original language. It inherits the low-level system programming capabilities of C but introduces features like automatic memory management and safer type handling, making it a more modern alternative.
curious_fact: C2 was one of the first languages to introduce automatic memory management features while still maintaining C's performance.

name: C3
description: C3 is a modern systems programming language designed for performance and safety. It aims to provide a balance between the low-level control of C and the safety features of higher-level languages.
connectionsExplanation: C3 was inspired by the C programming language, aiming to improve upon its safety features while maintaining performance. It draws from the systems programming paradigm, which is rooted in the need for efficient and reliable software development.
curious_fact: C3 introduced a novel type system that aims to eliminate common programming errors without sacrificing performance.

name: CA-Telon
description: CA-Telon is a fictional programming language created for the purpose of this exercise. It is designed to simulate a mid-20th century mainframe language with a focus on business applications.
connectionsExplanation: CA-Telon is a fictional language created for demonstration purposes. It is not based on any real historical language but is inspired by the general characteristics of mainframe languages from the 1970s.
curious_fact: CA-Telon was designed to be a versatile language for business applications, but it never saw widespread adoption.

name: CAL
description: CAL, or Control Anomaly Language, is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes and to explore the concept of control flow anomalies. It is not widely used in practical applications but is often referenced in computer science literature for its unique approach to programming.
connectionsExplanation: CAL was inspired by the need to study and understand complex control flow structures. It is not directly derived from any existing language but shares some conceptual similarities with early programming languages like FORTRAN and BASIC.
curious_fact: CAL was created to simulate and study control anomalies in computer programs, making it a valuable tool for educational purposes.

name: CALActorLanguage
description: CALActorLanguage is a specialized programming language designed for concurrent and distributed computing. It is based on the Actor Model, which emphasizes message passing and state isolation. Despite its innovative approach, it has not gained widespread adoption.
connectionsExplanation: CALActorLanguage was developed as an extension of the Erlang programming language, which is also based on the Actor Model. It aimed to provide more advanced features for distributed systems, but its niche focus limited its adoption.
curious_fact: CALActorLanguage introduced a unique feature called 'Actor Clusters' to manage large-scale distributed systems more efficiently.
example: actor Main { receive { message -> println("Hello, World!") } }

name: CALM
description: CALM is a modern, functional programming language designed for simplicity and ease of use. It emphasizes immutability and declarative programming paradigms, making it suitable for both beginners and experienced developers. CALM is particularly well-suited for web development and data processing tasks.
connectionsExplanation: CALM was inspired by the Haskell programming language, which is known for its strong emphasis on functional programming principles. CALM aims to provide a more accessible syntax while maintaining the core benefits of functional programming.

moons.name: WebKit
moons.description: A framework for building web applications in CALM.

moons.name: DataPipe
moons.description: A library for efficient data processing in CALM.

curious_fact: CALM was developed by a team of researchers at Stanford University to address the growing need for more accessible functional programming languages.

name: CBASIC
description: CBASIC, or Compiled BASIC, is a variant of the BASIC programming language designed for faster execution by compiling to machine code rather than interpreting. It was developed to provide the ease of use of BASIC with the performance benefits of compiled languages.
connectionsExplanation: CBASIC evolved from the original BASIC language, which was developed in the late 1960s. It aimed to address the performance limitations of interpreted BASIC by compiling the code to machine code, making it suitable for applications requiring higher execution speed.
curious_fact: CBASIC was one of the first attempts to compile BASIC, significantly reducing execution time compared to its interpreted counterparts.

name: CDuce
description: CDuce is a statically typed functional programming language designed for XML processing. It integrates XML data types and XPath-like pattern matching into a purely functional language.
connectionsExplanation: CDuce was developed as a research project at INRIA, France, aiming to provide a more expressive and efficient way to handle XML data. It draws inspiration from functional programming languages like OCaml and Haskell, particularly in its use of pattern matching and type systems.
curious_fact: CDuce introduced the concept of XML types directly into the language, allowing for more robust XML processing.
example: let x = <root><child>hello</child></root> in match x with <root><child>{s}</child></root> -> s

name: CEEMAC
description: CEEMAC, short for Central European Exchange Machine Assembly Code, was a computer language developed in the early 1960s for use in Central Europe. It was designed to facilitate the exchange of data between different computer systems in the region. CEEMAC was primarily used in academic and research environments.
connectionsExplanation: CEEMAC was developed in the context of the early computer era when there was a need for standardized data exchange protocols. It was influenced by the need for interoperability between different computer systems in Central Europe, reflecting the geopolitical and technological landscape of the time.
curious_fact: CEEMAC was one of the early efforts to standardize computer languages for data exchange in Europe.

name: CEMMA
description: CEMMA is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is inspired by BASIC but with modern syntax and features.
connectionsExplanation: CEMMA was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts. While it draws inspiration from BASIC, it incorporates modern language features to make learning more engaging and relevant.
curious_fact: CEMMA was developed in a weekend hackathon by a group of educators.

name: CFScript
description: CFScript is a scripting language used primarily with Adobe ColdFusion, allowing developers to write server-side scripts in a syntax similar to JavaScript. It is designed to be more readable and concise than the traditional tag-based ColdFusion Markup Language (CFML).
connectionsExplanation: CFScript was introduced in ColdFusion MX 7 (2001) as a way to provide a more modern and flexible scripting alternative to the existing tag-based CFML. It is closely integrated with the ColdFusion platform, allowing developers to leverage the full capabilities of the ColdFusion server.
curious_fact: CFScript was one of the first languages to introduce a scripting syntax to the ColdFusion platform, significantly reducing the verbosity of server-side code.

name: CGOL
description: CGOL, or Conway's Game of Life, is a cellular automaton devised by mathematician John Conway in 1970. It is not a programming language in the traditional sense but a zero-player game that evolves through a set of rules applied to a grid of cells.
connectionsExplanation: CGOL is inspired by the concept of cellular automata and is often implemented in various programming languages. Its simplicity and the beauty of its emergent patterns have made it a popular subject in computer science education and research.
curious_fact: CGOL can simulate any Turing machine, making it theoretically capable of computing anything computable.

name: CHAIN
description: CHAIN is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is not widely used in industry but is popular in academic settings for teaching basic programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: CHAIN was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming. It draws inspiration from BASIC and Python, aiming to combine the simplicity of BASIC with the readability of Python. Its development was influenced by the need for a language that could be easily understood and used by students with little to no prior programming experience.
curious_fact: CHAIN was developed by a team of educators who wanted to create a language that could be taught in a single semester.

name: CHICKEN
description: CHICKEN is a Scheme-to-C compiler that translates Scheme code into C, allowing Scheme programs to be compiled into standalone executables. It is known for its efficiency and compatibility with C libraries.
connectionsExplanation: CHICKEN is a Scheme-to-C compiler, which means it evolved from the Scheme programming language. Scheme is a dialect of Lisp, and Lisp is one of the pioneers in the field of programming languages, originating in the 1950s. Therefore, CHICKEN can be traced back to the 'analytical-engine-order-code' through its lineage to Scheme and Lisp.

moons.name: Chicken Scheme
moons.description: A variant of Scheme that is specifically targeted for use with CHICKEN.

curious_fact: CHICKEN is named after the mascot of the Chicken Scheme project, a chicken wearing a monocle.

name: CHILL
description: CHILL is a programming language designed for systems programming, particularly in the context of telecommunications. It is a dialect of Pascal with extensions for character string manipulation and bit-level operations.
connectionsExplanation: CHILL evolved from Pascal, specifically targeting the needs of telecommunications systems. It was developed at Bell Labs to improve the safety and reliability of software in telecommunication networks.
curious_fact: CHILL was one of the first languages to introduce string handling as a first-class citizen in systems programming.
example: program HelloWorld; begin writeln('Hello, World!'); end.

name: CHIP
description: CHIP is a minimalist programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and ease of learning. It is often used in introductory computer science courses to teach basic programming concepts without the complexity of more advanced languages.
connectionsExplanation: CHIP was developed as part of a project aimed at creating a simple language for beginners. It draws inspiration from BASIC and other early educational languages but is designed to be even more straightforward, making it an excellent tool for teaching fundamental programming ideas.
curious_fact: CHIP was created to help students understand programming concepts without getting overwhelmed by syntax.

name: CHIP-8
description: CHIP-8 is an interpreted programming language and instruction set architecture (ISA) developed by Joseph Weisbecker in 1979. It was designed to be simple and easy to implement, making it ideal for educational purposes and as a basis for creating video games. Despite its age, CHIP-8 remains popular among retro gaming enthusiasts and hobbyists.
connectionsExplanation: CHIP-8 is considered a pioneer in the field of video game development and is often credited as one of the first systems to use an interpreted language for game development. Its simplicity and ease of implementation have inspired numerous other systems and languages, particularly in the realm of retro gaming and educational computing.

moons.name: Super CHIP
moons.description: An extension of the CHIP-8 language that adds additional features and instructions to enhance the capabilities of the original system.

moons.name: CHIP-48
moons.description: A variant of CHIP-8 that includes additional memory and features, expanding the possibilities for game development.

curious_fact: The CHIP-8 interpreter was originally designed to run on the RCA VIP computer, which was one of the first home computers.

name: CICS
description: CICS, which stands for Customer Information Control System, is a middleware product developed by IBM. It is used to manage transactions in business, finance, and other industries. CICS provides a runtime environment for applications that require high levels of transaction processing and data integrity.
connectionsExplanation: CICS was developed in the late 1960s as part of IBM's System/360 mainframe series. It was designed to handle complex transaction processing, making it a cornerstone of enterprise computing. CICS has evolved over the years to support modern programming languages and environments, maintaining its relevance in the business world.
curious_fact: CICS was one of the first systems to introduce the concept of transaction management in computing.
example:
EXEC CICS START TRANSACTION
EXEC CICS SEND TEXT('Hello, World!')
EXEC CICS END TRANSACTION

name: CLACL
description: CLACL is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing clarity and simplicity. It is not widely used in industry but is popular in academic settings for teaching basic programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: CLACL was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of more advanced languages. It is inspired by the simplicity of BASIC but with a modern syntax and structure.
curious_fact: CLACL was developed by a team of educators who wanted a language that could be easily understood by students with no prior programming experience.

name: CLIPS
description: CLIPS (C Language Integrated Production System) is a forward-chaining expert system shell developed by NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory. It is written in C and is designed for building rule-based expert systems.
connectionsExplanation: CLIPS is a direct descendant of the earlier expert system shell called Jess, which was developed in the 1980s. Jess was inspired by the Lisp-based expert system language called OPS5, which in turn was influenced by the earlier language called STRIPS (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver). CLIPS builds upon these foundations to provide a more integrated and efficient system for rule-based programming.

moons.name: Jess
moons.description: Jess is a rule engine for the Java platform, which is a direct descendant of CLIPS.

curious_fact: CLIPS was originally developed to support the Space Shuttle's software.

name: CLISP
description: CLISP is an implementation of the Common Lisp programming language, known for its portability and efficiency. It is widely used in academic settings and for research purposes.
connectionsExplanation: CLISP is a direct descendant of Common Lisp, which itself evolved from earlier Lisp dialects like Lisp 1.5 and Maclisp. It adheres closely to the ANSI Common Lisp standard, making it a reliable choice for both development and education.

moons.name: CFFI
moons.description: CFFI (Common Foreign Function Interface) allows CLISP to call C functions and use C libraries.

moons.name: CLSQL
moons.description: CLSQL is a Common Lisp library for database access, providing an interface to various SQL databases.

curious_fact: CLISP was one of the first Common Lisp implementations to support Unicode.

name: CLIST
description: CLIST is a command language developed by IBM for use with IBM mainframe systems. It allows users to create and execute command lists, which can automate tasks and simplify system administration.
connectionsExplanation: CLIST is a direct descendant of IBM's JCL (Job Control Language) and was introduced to enhance the capabilities of system administrators by providing a more flexible and powerful scripting environment.
curious_fact: CLIST was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of nested loops and conditional statements in a command language context.

name: CLP(R)
description: CLP(R) stands for Constraint Logic Programming over Reals. It is a programming paradigm that combines the power of logic programming with constraint solving over real numbers. CLP(R) is particularly useful for applications in continuous domains such as engineering, physics, and economics.
connectionsExplanation: CLP(R) evolved from the broader field of Constraint Logic Programming (CLP), which was developed in the late 1980s. It extends the capabilities of CLP to handle real numbers, making it suitable for applications that require continuous mathematical modeling.
curious_fact: CLP(R) was one of the first constraint programming languages to handle real numbers, enabling more complex mathematical modeling.

name: CLU
description: CLU is a programming language developed at MIT in the 1970s. It is known for its strong typing, modularity, and support for concurrent programming. CLU was designed to address the limitations of earlier languages like Pascal and to provide a more robust environment for system programming.
connectionsExplanation: CLU was developed at MIT in the 1970s as part of the Project Genie. It was influenced by earlier languages like Pascal and Algol but introduced several innovative features such as strong typing, modularity, and support for concurrent programming. CLU's design aimed to provide a more robust and flexible environment for system programming compared to its predecessors.
curious_fact: CLU was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of strong typing and modularity, which are now standard features in many modern programming languages.
example: MODULE HelloWorld IS PROCEDURE main() BEGIN print("Hello, World!") END main END HelloWorld

name: CLtL1
description: CLtL1, short for Common Lisp the Language, 1st Edition, is a seminal book that defines the Common Lisp programming language. It was published in 1984 and is considered a foundational text for Common Lisp programmers.
connectionsExplanation: CLtL1 is a defining document for Common Lisp, which evolved from earlier Lisp dialects. It provides a comprehensive specification and is considered the standard for the language, influencing its development and adoption.
curious_fact: CLtL1 was written by Guy Steele and Richard Gabriel, who later became key figures in the development of Java.
example:
;; Hello World in Common Lisp
(princ "Hello, World!")

name: CLtL2
description: CLtL2, short for Common Lisp the Language, 2nd Edition, is a comprehensive reference book for the Common Lisp programming language. It provides detailed descriptions of the language's syntax, semantics, and libraries, serving as a foundational text for both beginners and experienced Lisp programmers.
connectionsExplanation: CLtL2 is a seminal work in the evolution of Common Lisp, building upon earlier standards and providing a more comprehensive and standardized approach to the language. It is considered a direct descendant of the original Common Lisp language, which itself evolved from earlier Lisp dialects like LISP 1.5 and Maclisp.
curious_fact: CLtL2 introduced the concept of 'loop' as a high-level iteration construct, simplifying repetitive tasks in Lisp programs.
example:
;; Hello World in CLtL2 Common Lisp
(princ "Hello, World!")

name: CMS-2
description: CMS-2, or Control Data's Central Machine System II, was an early commercial computer system developed by Control Data Corporation (CDC) in the mid-1960s. It was designed to support business data processing and was notable for its use of microprogramming and virtual memory.
connectionsExplanation: CMS-2 was one of the early commercial systems that influenced the development of subsequent business-oriented computer systems. Its design principles, particularly in microprogramming and virtual memory, laid foundational concepts that were later adopted in other systems.
curious_fact: CMS-2 was one of the first computers to use microprogramming, allowing its instruction set to be changed without altering the hardware.

name: CMU Common Lisp
description: CMU Common Lisp is a high-performance implementation of the Common Lisp programming language developed at Carnegie Mellon University. It is known for its efficiency and is widely used in research and development.
connectionsExplanation: CMU Common Lisp is a direct descendant of Common Lisp, which was developed in the late 1980s. It inherits the core principles of Common Lisp, including its dynamic typing and powerful macro system, while focusing on performance optimizations.
curious_fact: CMU Common Lisp was one of the first implementations to support multithreading in Common Lisp.

name: COBOL.NET
description: COBOL.NET is a version of COBOL that allows COBOL programs to run on the .NET Framework. It integrates COBOL's strengths in business, finance, and administrative systems with the modern capabilities of .NET.
connectionsExplanation: COBOL.NET was developed by Micro Focus to enable legacy COBOL applications to leverage the .NET Framework, thus allowing them to run on Windows and take advantage of modern programming features and libraries.
curious_fact: COBOL.NET was one of the first efforts to integrate COBOL with modern .NET technologies, allowing for the reuse of existing COBOL code in a .NET environment.
example:
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. HelloWorld.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
DISPLAY 'Hello, World!'.
STOP RUN.

name: CoffeeScript
description: CoffeeScript is a programming language that transpiles to JavaScript. It aims to expose the good parts of JavaScript in a simple way, with a readable syntax. CoffeeScript is designed to make writing JavaScript more enjoyable and less error-prone.
connectionsExplanation: CoffeeScript was created by Jeremy Ashkenas in 2009 as a way to simplify JavaScript development. It is inspired by languages like Ruby and Python, aiming to provide a more elegant syntax. CoffeeScript compiles to JavaScript, making it a direct descendant of JavaScript.

moons.name: Literate CoffeeScript
moons.description: A variant of CoffeeScript that allows writing code in Markdown format.

curious_fact: CoffeeScript was named after the author's love for coffee and the desire to make JavaScript more 'coffee-like' in terms of readability.

name: COLA
description: COLA is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It was created to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of more advanced languages.
connectionsExplanation: COLA was inspired by the desire to create an accessible language for beginners, drawing from the simplicity of BASIC and the structured approach of Pascal. Its development aimed to bridge the gap between simple scripting languages and more complex programming paradigms.
curious_fact: COLA was created to be the 'Cola of Programming Languages', offering a refreshing and easy-to-drink experience for new programmers.

name: COMAL
description: COMAL (Combined Algorithmic Language) is a structured programming language designed for educational purposes, particularly in Denmark. It was developed to teach programming concepts in a clear and concise manner.
connectionsExplanation: COMAL was developed in Denmark in the late 1970s as an educational tool. It was influenced by structured programming concepts and aimed to simplify the learning process for beginners. While it did not become widely adopted outside of educational settings, it had a significant impact on programming education in Denmark.
curious_fact: COMAL was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of structured programming to Danish schools.

name: COMIT
description: COMIT (Communicating Sequential Processes) is a programming language designed for concurrent programming. It was developed by Tony Hoare at the British National Physical Laboratory. COMIT is notable for its formal approach to describing concurrent systems.
connectionsExplanation: COMIT is a pioneering language in the field of concurrent programming, developed in the late 1960s. It was one of the first languages to formally address the complexities of concurrent processes, influencing later languages and paradigms in this area.
curious_fact: COMIT introduced the concept of monitors, which are used to manage access to shared resources in concurrent systems.

name: COMPACTII
description: COMPACTII is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It emphasizes simplicity and minimalism, making it an ideal language for beginners to understand basic programming concepts without the complexities of more advanced languages.
connectionsExplanation: COMPACTII was created as an educational tool in the early 1970s, inspired by the need for a language that could teach fundamental programming principles in a straightforward manner. It is not derived from any existing language but was designed to be intuitive and easy to learn.
curious_fact: COMPACTII was one of the first languages to introduce a simple syntax that focused on clarity over complexity.

name: COMPOOL
description: COMPOOL is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on teaching concurrency and parallel programming concepts. It is not widely used outside of academic settings.
connectionsExplanation: COMPOOL was created as an educational tool to help students understand complex concepts in concurrency and parallel programming. It is not derived from any existing language but was inspired by the need for a simple yet effective way to teach these topics.
curious_fact: COMPOOL was developed by a team of educators to simplify the teaching of parallel programming.

name: COMTRAN
description: COMTRAN was an early high-level programming language designed for scientific and engineering computations. It was one of the first languages to support complex arithmetic and matrix operations directly in the language syntax.
connectionsExplanation: COMTRAN was developed at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) and was influenced by the earlier FORTRAN language. It aimed to simplify the process of writing complex mathematical programs by providing built-in support for matrix operations and complex numbers, which were not as readily available in other languages of its time.
curious_fact: COMTRAN was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of complex numbers directly in the language syntax, which was a significant advancement for scientific computing at the time.
example:
PROGRAM EXAMPLE
BEGIN
  COMPLEX A, B, C
  A = (3, 4)
  B = (1, 2)
  C = A + B
  PRINT C
END

name: CORC
description: CORC is a lesser-known programming language developed in the early 1970s. It was designed for educational purposes and was not widely adopted. CORC features a simple syntax and is often used in introductory programming courses.
connectionsExplanation: CORC was developed during a time when educational programming languages were gaining popularity. It was influenced by the simplicity and ease of use of BASIC, but with a focus on structured programming concepts.
curious_fact: CORC was one of the first languages to introduce structured programming concepts to beginners.

name: COWSEL
description: COWSEL is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, particularly to teach programming concepts through a playful and imaginative approach. It uses a unique syntax inspired by the popular internet meme 'cow say'. Despite its playful nature, COWSEL is Turing-complete and can be used to write complex programs.
connectionsExplanation: COWSEL was inspired by the need to create an engaging and memorable programming language for beginners. Its syntax draws from the 'cow say' internet meme, which features ASCII art of a cow saying something. This inspiration makes learning programming more fun and relatable for new programmers.
curious_fact: COWSEL was created by a group of computer science educators who wanted to make programming more accessible and enjoyable for students.

name: CPCCodingScheme
description: CPCCodingScheme is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and clarity. It is not intended for production use but serves as a tool for teaching basic programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: CPCCodingScheme was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming. It draws inspiration from various paradigms, aiming to provide a straightforward syntax that is easy to understand and implement.
curious_fact: CPCCodingScheme was developed in a single weekend by a group of educators to fill a gap in introductory programming materials.

name: CPL
description: CPL (Combined Programming Language) was an early programming language developed at the University of Cambridge in the UK. It was one of the first high-level languages to support structured programming concepts and was influential in the development of later languages like C.
connectionsExplanation: CPL was developed as part of the Cambridge University Computing Laboratory's efforts to create a more efficient and structured programming language. It was one of the first languages to introduce concepts like block structure and nested functions, which influenced the design of subsequent languages.
curious_fact: CPL was one of the first languages to use the term 'structured programming', which later became a fundamental concept in computer science.

name: CQP-kod
description: CQP-kod is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and clarity. It is inspired by the C programming language but introduces unique syntax and paradigms to enhance learning.
connectionsExplanation: CQP-kod was created as an educational tool, drawing inspiration from the simplicity and structure of C. It aims to provide a gentle learning curve for beginners while introducing fundamental programming concepts.
curious_fact: CQP-kod was developed by a team of educators to make programming more accessible to students.

name: CRN++
description: CRN++ is an experimental programming language designed for creating complex chemical reaction networks. It extends the capabilities of CRN (Chemical Reaction Network) theory by incorporating object-oriented principles, allowing for more modular and reusable code in biochemical simulations.
connectionsExplanation: CRN++ is inspired by the CRN paradigm, which models chemical reactions as computation. It aims to bridge the gap between computational theory and biochemical processes, potentially leading to new insights in both fields.
curious_fact: CRN++ is one of the first languages to apply object-oriented programming to chemical reaction networks, enabling more sophisticated simulations.

name: CS-4
description: CS-4 is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and ease of learning. It is part of a series of languages created for computer science curriculum.
connectionsExplanation: CS-4 was developed as a modern educational tool, inspired by the principles of structured programming and object-oriented design. It aims to bridge the gap between introductory programming concepts and more advanced language features.
curious_fact: CS-4 was created to be the first language taught in a new computer science curriculum aimed at high school students.

name: CSMPIII
description: CSMPIII is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and ease of learning. It is part of a series of languages aimed at introducing beginners to computer science concepts.
connectionsExplanation: CSMPIII was created as an educational tool, inspired by the need for a language that could be easily understood by beginners. It is not derived from any existing language but is part of a fictional universe where it serves as a stepping stone to more complex programming languages.
curious_fact: CSMPIII was invented by a team of educators to simplify the learning curve for introductory programming courses.

name: CSS Preprocessor
description: A CSS preprocessor extends the CSS language, adding scripting capabilities to generate CSS and offering features like variables, nesting, mixins, and functions. Popular preprocessors include Sass and Less.
connectionsExplanation: CSS preprocessors evolved from the need to enhance CSS with more dynamic and maintainable features. They emerged as a solution to the limitations of plain CSS, particularly in large-scale web development projects. Sass and Less, two of the most popular preprocessors, were developed in the late 1990s and early 2000s, building on the existing CSS syntax and introducing new features that significantly improved the development workflow.

moons.name: Sass
moons.description: Sass is a CSS preprocessor that adds scripting capabilities to CSS, allowing for variables, nesting, mixins, and functions.

moons.name: Less
moons.description: Less is a CSS preprocessor that extends CSS with variables, mixins, functions, and nested rules.

curious_fact: Sass was originally written in Ruby, but later versions were rewritten in Dart for better performance.

name: CWEB
description: CWEB is a literate programming system that combines C code with documentation. It was created by Donald Ervin Knuth to facilitate the development of large, complex software systems by allowing programmers to intersperse human-readable explanations with the actual code.
connectionsExplanation: CWEB is a direct descendant of WEB, which was developed by Donald Knuth for documenting Pascal programs. CWEB extends the concept to C, allowing for the creation of literate programs that are both human-readable and compilable.
curious_fact: CWEB was used to write TeX, one of the most widely used typesetting systems in the world.

name: CaPSL
description: CaPSL (Case Sensitive Programming Language) is a specialized programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing the importance of case sensitivity in programming. It is not widely used outside of academic settings.
connectionsExplanation: CaPSL was developed as an educational tool to highlight the nuances of case sensitivity in programming languages. It is not directly derived from any existing language but shares some conceptual similarities with other educational languages like Small-C.
curious_fact: CaPSL requires variables to be declared with specific case, teaching programmers the importance of case sensitivity early on.

name: Caché ObjectScript
description: Caché ObjectScript is a high-level, object-oriented programming language developed by InterSystems Corporation. It is primarily used for building enterprise applications, especially in healthcare and finance, leveraging the Caché database.
