description: Emacs Lisp (Elisp) is a dialect of the Lisp programming language used as the extension language for the GNU Emacs text editor. It is a functional programming language with dynamic typing and garbage collection.
connectionsExplanation: Emacs Lisp was developed as an extension language for the Emacs text editor, which was originally written in Maclisp. It is a dialect of Common Lisp and shares many features with it, but is tailored for the needs of text editing and automation within Emacs.
curious_fact: Emacs Lisp is one of the oldest dialects of Lisp still in widespread use today.
example: (defun hello-world () "Prints 'Hello, World!' to the console." (interactive) (message "Hello, World!"))

name: Embedded C
description: Embedded C is a variant of the C programming language tailored for writing software for embedded systems. It is widely used in the development of hardware devices and systems where resources are limited.
connectionsExplanation: Embedded C evolved from the standard C language, which was developed in the early 1970s. It inherits the syntax and features of C but is optimized for the constraints of embedded systems, such as limited memory and processing power.
curious_fact: Embedded C often requires manual memory management due to the limited resources of embedded systems.

name: Embedded Visual Basic
description: Embedded Visual Basic (EVB) is a version of Visual Basic designed for use in embedded systems. It allows developers to write applications for devices with limited resources, such as microcontrollers. EVB is known for its ease of use and integration with Microsoft's development tools.
connectionsExplanation: Embedded Visual Basic evolved from the standard Visual Basic language, which was developed by Microsoft in the early 1990s. It was specifically tailored to meet the needs of developers working on embedded systems, where resource constraints are a significant factor. EVB inherits the object-oriented and event-driven paradigms of Visual Basic, making it accessible for developers familiar with the language.
curious_fact: Embedded Visual Basic was one of the first languages to bring the ease of Visual Basic to the embedded systems market, enabling rapid development for devices with limited resources.
example:
Sub Main()
    MsgBox("Hello, World!")
End Sub

name: Emerald
description: Emerald is a programming language designed for teaching object-oriented programming concepts. It was created by David Gries and Andrew W. Appel at Princeton University. Emerald is a simplified version of the Eiffel language, focusing on clarity and ease of learning.
connectionsExplanation: Emerald was developed as an educational tool to introduce object-oriented programming principles. It is derived from the Eiffel language, which was designed for building robust and reusable software. Emerald simplifies Eiffel's syntax and features to make it more accessible for beginners.
curious_fact: Emerald was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of design by contract in an educational setting.
example:
class HelloWorld is
  create make
  feature
    make is
      io.put_string("Hello, World!\n")
end

name: Emmental
description: Emmental is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and ease of learning. It is inspired by the cheese of the same name, known for its unique holes and creamy texture.
connectionsExplanation: Emmental was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of existing languages. It draws inspiration from the simplicity and uniformity of the Emmental cheese, aiming to make the learning process as smooth and enjoyable as possible.
curious_fact: Emmental is the first programming language to use a cheese-themed syntax, making it a unique choice for culinary enthusiasts.

name: Emoticon
description: Emoticon is a language designed to express emotions and ideas through a series of symbols and characters. It originated in the early 1980s and has since become a widely used form of digital communication, particularly in text-based messaging and online forums.
connectionsExplanation: Emoticon, while not a traditional programming language, has influenced the development of modern text-based communication systems. Its use of symbols to convey emotions has paved the way for more expressive and nuanced digital interactions.
curious_fact: Emoticon was created by Scott Fahlman in 1982 to add emotional context to online discussions.

name: Enchanting
description: Enchanting is a fictional programming language designed for beginners, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It was created to introduce new programmers to the basics of coding with a magical theme, making learning more engaging and fun.
connectionsExplanation: Enchanting was inspired by the desire to create an accessible and engaging language for beginners. While it does not have a direct historical predecessor, it draws inspiration from the paradigms of modern scripting languages like Python and JavaScript, aiming to provide a gentle learning curve.
curious_fact: Enchanting uses a unique syntax that resembles fairy tales, with keywords like 'spell', 'wand', and 'magic'.

name: Enterprise Mashup Markup Language
description: Enterprise Mashup Markup Language (EMML) is a specialized markup language designed for creating enterprise-level mashups. It allows developers to integrate various enterprise applications and services into a cohesive, unified interface. EMML is particularly useful in environments where multiple systems need to communicate and share data seamlessly.
connectionsExplanation: EMML was developed as a response to the increasing need for enterprise-level integration solutions. It builds upon the principles of XML and HTML, providing a more structured and secure way to mashup enterprise applications. Its development was influenced by the growing demand for service-oriented architectures (SOAs) and the need to facilitate data exchange between different enterprise systems.

moons.name: EMML Framework
moons.description: A comprehensive framework for building enterprise mashups using EMML.

curious_fact: EMML was one of the first languages to introduce security features specifically designed for enterprise-level data integration.

name: Entry
description: Entry is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It is a simple, minimalistic language aimed at introducing beginners to the basics of programming concepts without the complexities of more advanced languages.
connectionsExplanation: Entry was created as a teaching tool to simplify the learning process for beginners. It draws inspiration from basic programming paradigms and is not based on any existing language.
curious_fact: Entry was developed to make programming accessible to children and beginners.

name: Envelop
description: Envelop is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is not intended for production use but serves as a tool for beginners to understand basic programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: Envelop was created as an educational tool and does not have a direct historical lineage. It is inspired by modern scripting languages but is not derived from any existing language.
curious_fact: Envelop was developed in a weekend hackathon by a group of educators.

name: Epigram
description: Epigram is a purely functional programming language designed for writing concise and expressive code. It is based on the dependently typed lambda calculus and is known for its strong type system and support for theorem proving.
connectionsExplanation: Epigram is inspired by the dependently typed programming language Agda, which itself is a descendant of Martin-Löf's Type Theory. It aims to provide a more practical approach to dependently typed programming by focusing on ease of use and integration with existing tools.
curious_fact: Epigram was developed by Conor McBride and James McKinna at the University of Birmingham.
example:
data Nat : Set where
  zero : Nat
  suc  : Nat -> Nat

add : Nat -> Nat -> Nat
add zero    y = y
add (suc x) y = suc (add x y)

name: Epson Standard Code for Printers
description: Epson Standard Code for Printers (ESC/P) is a printer command language developed by Epson in 1981. It is used to control Epson dot matrix printers and is known for its simplicity and efficiency in printing text and graphics.
connectionsExplanation: ESC/P was developed by Epson to provide a standardized way to control their dot matrix printers. It evolved from earlier printer control languages and became widely used in the 1980s and 1990s, especially in environments where Epson printers were prevalent.
curious_fact: ESC/P is known for its compact command set, which allows for efficient printer control.
example: ESC @ ESC E HT LF CR

name: Escher
description: Escher is a programming language designed by Peter Grogono in 1985. It is known for its unique syntax and its ability to generate Escher-like tessellations. The language is not widely used and is considered esoteric.
connectionsExplanation: Escher was created as an experiment in programming language design, inspired by the mathematical art of M.C. Escher. It does not have a direct lineage from other languages but shares a conceptual connection to languages that explore unconventional syntax and visual representation.
curious_fact: Escher's syntax is based on geometric shapes and patterns, reflecting its creator's interest in M.C. Escher's work.

name: Esterel
description: Esterel is a synchronous programming language designed for the development of real-time embedded systems. It is particularly suited for applications where timing and concurrency are critical, such as in avionics, automotive, and telecommunications.
connectionsExplanation: Esterel was developed at the École Nationale Supérieure des Télécommunications de Paris (ENST) in France. It is part of the synchronous programming paradigm, which emerged in the 1980s to address the challenges of real-time systems. Esterel's design is rooted in the need for precise control over timing and concurrency, making it a foundational language in this domain.

moons.name: Synchronous Programming
moons.description: A programming paradigm that focuses on the synchronization of events and actions in real-time systems.

curious_fact: Esterel was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of 'synchronous programming', which has since influenced many real-time systems languages.
example:
process HelloWorld is
  every 1s do
    print "Hello World"
  end
end

name: Eta
description: Eta is a purely functional programming language designed for high performance and expressiveness. It is a dialect of Haskell, aiming to improve upon its performance characteristics while maintaining the elegance of functional programming.
connectionsExplanation: Eta is a dialect of Haskell, which itself is a direct descendant of Miranda. Miranda was inspired by the lazy functional language ISWIM, which in turn was influenced by the lambda calculus. Therefore, Eta's lineage can be traced back to these foundational functional programming concepts.
curious_fact: Eta is known for its efficient implementation of lazy evaluation, which can lead to significant performance improvements in certain applications.

name: Etoys
description: Etoys is a visual programming environment developed by the MIT Media Lab. It is designed to be accessible to children and adults alike, allowing users to create interactive stories, simulations, and games through a drag-and-drop interface.
connectionsExplanation: Etoys is inspired by the Logo programming language, which was developed in the late 1960s at MIT. Like Logo, Etoys emphasizes learning through play and encourages users to explore computational concepts through visual and interactive means.
curious_fact: Etoys runs on the Squeak virtual machine, which is also the foundation for the Scratch programming language.

name: EuLisp
description: EuLisp is an experimental Lisp dialect developed in the early 1990s. It aimed to integrate object-oriented programming features into Lisp, focusing on simplicity and ease of use. Despite its innovative approach, EuLisp did not gain widespread adoption and is now considered a niche language.
connectionsExplanation: EuLisp was developed as an attempt to merge object-oriented programming paradigms with the functional programming strengths of Lisp. It was influenced by the Common Lisp community's desire for more flexible and modern language features. However, its specialized focus and limited adoption have kept it outside the mainstream Lisp family.
curious_fact: EuLisp introduced a unique object system that allowed for multiple inheritance and dynamic method binding, which were not common in Lisp dialects at the time.
example: (defclass point () ((x :initarg :x :accessor point-x) (y :initarg :y :accessor point-y))) (defmethod print-object ((p point) stream) (format stream "#<Point (~a, ~a)>" (point-x p) (point-y p))) (let ((p (make-instance 'point :x 10 :y 20))) (print p))

name: Euclid
description: Euclid is a programming language designed for educational purposes, particularly for teaching geometry and mathematical concepts. It was created by Dr. Robert W. Floyd at Stanford University in 1964. Euclid is known for its simple syntax and its focus on clarity and correctness, making it an excellent tool for beginners and students learning programming.
connectionsExplanation: Euclid was developed as an educational tool to teach mathematical concepts and programming principles. It is not directly derived from any other language but shares some similarities with early educational languages like BASIC. Its simplicity and focus on clarity make it a unique entry in the history of programming languages.
curious_fact: Euclid was one of the first programming languages to use a structured approach, emphasizing clarity and correctness over speed.

name: Euphoria
description: Euphoria is an interpreted, procedural programming language designed to be easy to learn and use. It was created by Robert G. Simpson and is known for its simplicity and readability, making it popular for beginners and small projects.
connectionsExplanation: Euphoria was developed as a modern alternative to BASIC, aiming to provide a more structured and efficient programming environment. It was influenced by the simplicity of BASIC but introduced more advanced features like dynamic arrays and associative arrays, which were not present in traditional BASIC.

moons.name: Euphoria IDE
moons.description: An integrated development environment specifically designed for Euphoria programming.

curious_fact: Euphoria was one of the first languages to support associative arrays natively.

name: EusLispRobotProgrammingLanguage
description: EusLispRobotProgrammingLanguage is a dialect of Common Lisp specifically designed for robotics. It integrates seamlessly with the KDL (Kinematics and Dynamics Library) and is widely used in robotics research and development in Japan.
connectionsExplanation: EusLispRobotProgrammingLanguage evolved from Common Lisp, which itself is a direct descendant of Lisp. The language was developed to address the specific needs of robotics, particularly in Japan, where robotics research is highly advanced.
curious_fact: EusLispRobotProgrammingLanguage was developed at the Robotics Laboratory of Tokyo Institute of Technology.

name: EventScripts
description: EventScripts is a scripting language designed for creating custom game modifications, particularly for Source engine games like Half-Life 2. It allows developers to add new features, modify game behavior, and automate tasks within the game environment.
connectionsExplanation: EventScripts evolved from the need for more flexible and powerful scripting capabilities in Source engine games. It was inspired by existing scripting languages used in game development but tailored specifically to the Source engine's architecture, making it a direct descendant of game-specific scripting languages.
curious_fact: EventScripts was developed by Valve Corporation to enhance modding capabilities for their Source engine games, allowing for a wide range of customizations and additions.

name: Executive Systems Problem Oriented Language
description: Executive Systems Problem Oriented Language (ESPOL) was an early programming language developed in the 1960s. It was designed to simplify the programming of executive systems, which are systems that manage other systems. ESPOL aimed to provide a more intuitive and user-friendly approach to programming compared to lower-level languages of its time.
connectionsExplanation: ESPOL was developed as part of the early efforts to create higher-level programming languages that could be used by non-expert programmers. It was influenced by the need to manage complex executive systems, which required a language that could handle system-level operations efficiently.
curious_fact: ESPOL was one of the early attempts to create a language that could be used by non-expert programmers, reflecting the growing demand for software development in the 1960s.
example:
BEGIN PROGRAM
  PRINT "Hello, World!"
END PROGRAM

name: Expect
description: Expect is a scripting language and a tool for automating interactive applications. It is built on top of Tcl and is widely used for automating tasks that require user interaction, such as telnet, ftp, and ssh sessions.
connectionsExplanation: Expect was developed by Don Libes at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) in the early 1990s. It was created to automate interactive applications, particularly those that require user input. Expect is built on top of Tcl, which provides a robust scripting environment. The development of Expect was a significant step in automating network and system administration tasks, making it a crucial tool for system administrators and developers.
curious_fact: Expect was named after the concept of 'expectation' in programming, where the script anticipates the output of a program and reacts accordingly.
example:
spawn telnet example.com
expect "login:"
send "username\r"
expect "Password:"
send "password\r"
expect eof

name: Extempore
description: Extempore is a programming language designed for live coding and rapid prototyping. It is particularly suited for real-time audio and video applications. The language is dynamically typed and runs on the Extempore runtime, which is built on top of the LLVM compiler infrastructure.
connectionsExplanation: Extempore was developed by Andrew S. Gordon at the University of New South Wales. It is inspired by Lisp and Scheme, inheriting their functional programming paradigms. However, Extempore introduces a unique approach to live coding, allowing for real-time modification and execution of code, which is particularly useful in creative and interactive applications.

moons.name: Extempore Audio
moons.description: A library for real-time audio processing in Extempore.

moons.name: Extempore Graphics
moons.description: A library for real-time graphics rendering in Extempore.

curious_fact: Extempore's runtime is designed to be as fast as possible while maintaining the flexibility of a dynamically typed language.

name: ExtendScript
description: ExtendScript is a scripting language developed by Adobe Systems for use with Adobe Creative Suite applications. It is based on JavaScript and allows users to automate tasks and customize workflows within Adobe software.
connectionsExplanation: ExtendScript evolved from JavaScript, specifically tailored for Adobe applications. It was introduced in 1999 to provide a scripting environment that could interact with Adobe's proprietary software, enabling users to automate repetitive tasks and enhance productivity.
curious_fact: ExtendScript is one of the earliest scripting languages specifically designed for Adobe applications, predating Adobe's integration of JavaScript into its software.

name: Extended Pascal
description: Extended Pascal is an extension of the Pascal programming language, designed to add more features and capabilities to the original Pascal. It was developed to address some of the limitations of the standard Pascal language and to provide a more modern programming environment.
connectionsExplanation: Extended Pascal was developed as an extension of the original Pascal language, which was created by Niklaus Wirth in the late 1960s. The development of Extended Pascal aimed to enhance the capabilities of Pascal by adding new features such as pointers, dynamic memory allocation, and more advanced data types. This evolution reflects the growing needs of programmers for more powerful and flexible programming tools in the early 1980s.
curious_fact: Extended Pascal introduced the concept of 'with' statements, which allowed for more concise code when accessing fields of records.
example: program HelloWorld; begin writeln('Hello, World!'); end.

name: Extended Syntax of Sinclair BASIC
description: Extended Syntax of Sinclair BASIC is an enhanced version of the original Sinclair BASIC used on the ZX Spectrum. It introduced additional commands and features to improve functionality and programming capabilities on the Spectrum platform.
connectionsExplanation: Extended Syntax of Sinclair BASIC evolved from the original Sinclair BASIC, which was developed for the ZX Spectrum. It aimed to provide more advanced features to cater to the growing needs of programmers and users of the Spectrum.
curious_fact: The Extended Syntax of Sinclair BASIC was one of the first attempts to enhance the capabilities of the original BASIC language on home computers.

name: Extensible Application Markup Language
description: Extensible Application Markup Language (XAML) is a declarative XML-based language used for designing user interfaces in applications. It is primarily associated with Microsoft's .NET framework, particularly in the development of Windows Presentation Foundation (WPF) and Silverlight applications. XAML allows developers to separate the user interface from the application logic, promoting a cleaner and more maintainable codebase.
connectionsExplanation: XAML was introduced as part of the .NET framework in 2002, evolving from earlier XML-based UI technologies. It is a direct descendant of XML, which itself is a subset of SGML. XAML's design philosophy emphasizes separation of concerns, allowing developers to define the UI structure and appearance in a declarative manner, while the application logic is handled separately. This separation makes XAML particularly well-suited for complex UI applications, such as those in the Windows ecosystem.

moons.name: Windows Presentation Foundation (WPF)
moons.description: WPF is a UI framework for building Windows desktop applications. It uses XAML to define the UI elements and their properties.

moons.name: Silverlight
moons.description: Silverlight was a cross-platform application framework for building rich internet applications. It used XAML to define the UI and was particularly popular for web-based applications.

curious_fact: XAML's name is often pronounced as 'ZAML', which is a playful way to say 'Zee-A-Mel'.
example:
<Window xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml/presentation" xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml" Title="Hello World" Height="200" Width="300"> 
 <Grid> 
 <TextBlock Text="Hello, World!" HorizontalAlignment="Center" VerticalAlignment="Center" FontSize="20"/> 
 </Grid> 
</Window>

name: ExtensibleEmbeddableLanguage
description: ExtensibleEmbeddableLanguage (EEL) is a modern, flexible programming language designed for ease of extension and embedding in various applications. It supports dynamic typing and is intended for rapid development and prototyping.
connectionsExplanation: EEL was developed as a successor to the popular ScriptingLanguage, aiming to provide enhanced features for modern software development needs. It inherits the dynamic typing and ease of use from ScriptingLanguage but introduces more robust extension mechanisms and better performance optimizations.

moons.name: EEL-Web
moons.description: A web framework for building dynamic web applications with EEL.

moons.name: EEL-Game
moons.description: A game development library for creating interactive games with EEL.

curious_fact: EEL was created by a team of developers who wanted to address the limitations of existing scripting languages in handling complex, large-scale projects.

name: Ezhil
description: Ezhil is a programming language designed for beginners, particularly those in Tamil Nadu, India. It uses a syntax that is similar to Tamil, making it accessible to non-English speakers. Ezhil is interpreted and is used in educational settings to teach programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: Ezhil was developed to make programming accessible to Tamil-speaking students in India. It draws inspiration from the simplicity and readability of natural languages, aiming to reduce the barrier to entry for programming education.
curious_fact: Ezhil is one of the few programming languages that uses Tamil script, making it unique in the programming world.

name: F
description: F is a programming language developed by IBM in the late 1960s. It was designed for scientific and engineering applications, particularly for numerical computations. F is known for its simplicity and efficiency in handling large datasets and complex mathematical operations.
connectionsExplanation: F was developed as part of IBM's efforts to create a high-level language for scientific computing. It was influenced by earlier languages like FORTRAN and COBOL, but aimed to provide a more modern and flexible approach to programming. F's design focused on ease of use and performance, making it a popular choice for researchers and engineers in the 1970s.
curious_fact: F was one of the first languages to support complex numbers natively, which was a significant advancement for scientific computing at the time.

name: F#
description: F# is a strongly typed, multi-paradigm programming language that encompasses functional, imperative, and object-oriented programming. It is developed by Microsoft and is part of the .NET framework. F# is known for its expressiveness and performance, making it popular in areas like data analysis and scientific computing.
connectionsExplanation: F# was developed by Microsoft as part of the .NET initiative. It is a direct descendant of OCaml, inheriting its functional programming paradigms. F# was first released in 2005 and has since been integrated into the .NET ecosystem, allowing it to leverage the extensive libraries and tools available in .NET.

moons.name: Fable
moons.description: A compiler that translates F# to JavaScript, enabling F# code to run in web browsers.

moons.name: FsCheck
moons.description: A tool for property-based testing in F#.

curious_fact: F# is named after the F# key on a piano, symbolizing its versatility and the range of programming paradigms it supports.

name: F-Script
description: F-Script is an object-oriented scripting language for the Mac OS X platform. It is designed to be easy to learn and use, with a syntax similar to Smalltalk. F-Script is primarily used for scripting and prototyping Objective-C applications.
connectionsExplanation: F-Script was developed by Apple Inc. as a scripting language for Mac OS X. It is based on Smalltalk and Objective-C, allowing developers to write scripts that interact with Cocoa applications. F-Script was introduced as part of the Xcode IDE and was intended to provide a more intuitive way to work with Objective-C code.
curious_fact: F-Script was one of the first scripting languages to be integrated into Xcode, providing a powerful tool for Objective-C developers.

name: FACT
description: FACT is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, often used in computer science courses to illustrate basic programming concepts. It is not widely used in industry.
connectionsExplanation: FACT was created as a teaching tool to help students understand fundamental programming ideas without the complexities of more advanced languages. It is not derived from any existing language but inspired by the simplicity of early programming paradigms.
curious_fact: FACT was invented by Dr. Jane Doe in the 1970s to simplify programming education.

name: FALSE
description: FALSE is a purely functional programming language designed for educational purposes. It is a dialect of the Lisp programming language, specifically targeting the teaching of functional programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: FALSE is a dialect of Lisp, which was one of the pioneering programming languages developed in the 1950s. Lisp, along with Fortran, ALGOL, and COBOL, was influenced by the analytical-engine-order-code, making it a descendant of this foundational language.
curious_fact: FALSE stands for 'Forth-like Algorithmic String Expression', reflecting its design philosophy.

name: FARGO
description: FARGO is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is inspired by BASIC but with modern syntax and features.
connectionsExplanation: FARGO was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts. While it draws inspiration from BASIC, it incorporates modern language features to make learning more engaging and relevant.
curious_fact: FARGO was developed by a team of educators to simplify the learning curve of programming for students.

name: FAUST
description: FAUST is a domain-specific programming language for digital signal processing (DSP). It allows for the creation of audio signal processing algorithms with a focus on simplicity and efficiency. FAUST is designed to be both expressive and portable, enabling developers to write code that can be compiled to various platforms and hardware.
connectionsExplanation: FAUST was developed by Julius O. Smith III and is inspired by the functional programming paradigm. It is not directly derived from any specific language but draws from the principles of functional programming and the need for efficient DSP code. FAUST's design emphasizes the use of mathematical expressions to define signal processing algorithms, making it a unique tool in the DSP domain.

moons.name: faust.lib
moons.description: A library of reusable DSP components and functions for FAUST.

moons.name: faust2
moons.description: A version of FAUST with extended features and improved performance.

curious_fact: FAUST is named after the French composer and mathematician Jean-Baptiste Christophe Faust, known for his work in music theory and acoustics.

name: FJAX
description: FJAX is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is inspired by modern JavaScript but with a more straightforward syntax and fewer features.
connectionsExplanation: FJAX was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of traditional languages. It draws inspiration from JavaScript, aiming to retain its dynamic nature while simplifying its syntax for educational purposes.
curious_fact: FJAX was developed by a team of educators to make programming more accessible to students.

name: FL
description: FL is a fictional programming language created for educational purposes. It is designed to be simple and easy to understand, making it suitable for beginners. Despite its simplicity, FL is Turing-complete, allowing it to solve complex problems.
connectionsExplanation: FL was created as a teaching tool in the early 1960s, inspired by the need for a language that could be easily taught to students. It is not directly related to any existing programming language but shares some conceptual similarities with early educational languages like BASIC.
curious_fact: FL was the first language to introduce the concept of 'fluent programming', which emphasizes readability and simplicity.

name: FLACC
description: FLACC is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is inspired by the BASIC language but with a modern twist, making it accessible for beginners.
connectionsExplanation: FLACC was created as an educational tool to introduce programming concepts in a straightforward manner. While it draws inspiration from BASIC, it incorporates modern language features to engage contemporary learners.
curious_fact: FLACC stands for 'Fun Learning And Creative Coding'.

name: FLOW
description: FLOW is a programming language designed for educational purposes, particularly for teaching programming concepts to beginners. It was developed at the University of California, Berkeley, and is known for its simple syntax and straightforward approach to programming.
connectionsExplanation: FLOW was developed as part of the Berkeley Time-Sharing System (TSS) project, which aimed to create an interactive computing environment. It was influenced by earlier educational languages like BASIC and was designed to be easy to learn and use, making it suitable for introductory programming courses.
curious_fact: FLOW was one of the first languages to introduce structured programming concepts to educational settings.

name: FLOW-MATIC
description: FLOW-MATIC was one of the first high-level programming languages designed specifically for business data processing. It was developed by IBM to automate data processing tasks and was used extensively in the 1960s.
connectionsExplanation: FLOW-MATIC is considered a precursor to COBOL. It was developed by IBM to address the need for a more user-friendly language for business data processing. Its design influenced the creation of COBOL, which became the standard for business, finance, and administrative systems for decades.
curious_fact: FLOW-MATIC was one of the first languages to use English-like keywords, making it accessible to non-programmers.

name: FO(.)
description: FO(.) is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and minimalism. It is not intended for practical use but serves as a tool for teaching basic programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: FO(.) was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexities of more advanced languages. It is not derived from any existing language but inspired by the simplicity of BASIC and the structured approach of Pascal.
curious_fact: FO(.) was created by a team of educators to simplify the learning curve for new programmers.

name: FOCAL
description: FOCAL (FORmula Compiler and ALgorithmic Compiler) is a high-level programming language developed by Jean Ichbiah at MIT in 1964. It was designed for scientific and engineering applications, particularly for the IBM System/360 mainframe. FOCAL aimed to provide a more user-friendly and efficient alternative to assembly language.
connectionsExplanation: FOCAL was developed as part of Project MAC at MIT, which aimed to create a more accessible programming environment for scientists and engineers. It was influenced by earlier languages like FORTRAN and COBOL but introduced several innovations, such as a more structured syntax and support for arrays. FOCAL was particularly popular in academic and research settings in the 1960s and 1970s.
curious_fact: FOCAL was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of structured programming, which influenced the development of later languages like Pascal and C.

name: FOCAL-69
description: FOCAL-69 is a programming language developed by Jean Ichbiah for the MIT Instrumentation Laboratory. It was designed for the Apollo space program to control the Apollo Guidance Computer. FOCAL-69 is known for its simplicity and efficiency, making it suitable for real-time applications.
connectionsExplanation: FOCAL-69 was developed specifically for the Apollo space program, making it a direct descendant of the need for specialized programming languages for space exploration. It evolved from earlier efforts in real-time computing and was influenced by the need for reliability and performance in critical applications.
curious_fact: FOCAL-69 was one of the first languages to use a structured approach, influencing later languages like Pascal and Ada.

name: FOCUS
description: FOCUS is a high-level programming language designed for business data processing. It was developed by IBM and was one of the first languages to support complex data structures and operations. FOCUS is known for its ease of use and its ability to handle large volumes of data efficiently.
connectionsExplanation: FOCUS was developed by IBM in the 1960s and is considered a pioneer in business data processing languages. It evolved from earlier IBM languages and influenced the development of subsequent business-oriented programming languages.
curious_fact: FOCUS was one of the first languages to support complex data structures and operations, making it highly efficient for business data processing.
example:
DEFINE FILE EMPLOYEE
  SALARY / 1000
END
READ FILE EMPLOYEE
  DISPLAY 'EMPLOYEE NAME: ' EMPLOYEE.NAME
  DISPLAY 'SALARY: ' SALARY
END

name: FOIL
description: FOIL (Finding Optimal Induction of Logic) is a machine learning algorithm developed by Ross Quinlan. It is used for inductive inference, particularly in the context of learning logic rules from examples. FOIL is part of the broader field of artificial intelligence and is known for its efficiency in generating concise and accurate rules.
connectionsExplanation: FOIL is a descendant of the broader field of machine learning algorithms, which evolved from the need to automate the process of learning from data. It is inspired by the logical reasoning capabilities of expert systems and is part of the paradigm of inductive logic programming.
curious_fact: FOIL was one of the first algorithms to focus on learning logic rules, which has influenced many subsequent developments in machine learning and artificial intelligence.

name: FORMAC
description: FORMAC (FORmula MAnipulation Compiler) is an early computer algebra system developed in the 1960s. It was one of the first systems designed to manipulate mathematical expressions symbolically.
connectionsExplanation: FORMAC was developed at IBM and is considered a precursor to modern computer algebra systems. It was inspired by the need to automate symbolic mathematics, which was a significant step in the evolution of computational tools for mathematics and science.
curious_fact: FORMAC was one of the first languages to support symbolic computation, allowing users to perform algebraic manipulations directly on the computer.

name: FP
description: FP is a fictional programming language designed to explore the principles of functional programming in an abstract and minimalistic way. It emphasizes immutability and first-class functions, making it an interesting case study for theoretical computer science.
connectionsExplanation: FP was inspired by the early functional programming languages like Lisp and Haskell, aiming to simplify and generalize their concepts. It serves as a thought experiment in the realm of programming language design.
curious_fact: FP introduced the concept of 'pure functions' as a core language feature, influencing modern functional programming languages.

name: FP84
description: FP84 is a modern, functional programming language designed for high-performance computing. It emphasizes immutability and pure functions, making it suitable for concurrent and parallel applications.
connectionsExplanation: FP84 was inspired by Haskell and Erlang, aiming to combine the strengths of both functional programming paradigms with modern performance optimizations. Its design was influenced by the need for efficient execution in data-intensive applications.

moons.name: FP84-IO
moons.description: A library for handling input/output operations in FP84, ensuring they remain pure and side-effect free.

moons.name: FP84-Parallel
moons.description: A framework for parallel execution of FP84 programs, leveraging the language's functional nature for efficient concurrency.

curious_fact: FP84 was developed in a single weekend by a team of language enthusiasts.

name: FRACTRAN
description: FRACTRAN is an esoteric programming language invented by John Conway. It is a Turing-complete language that uses a sequence of fractions to perform computation. Despite its simplicity, it can simulate any Turing machine.
connectionsExplanation: FRACTRAN was created by John Conway in 1972 as a thought experiment to explore the nature of computation. It is considered a Turing-complete language, meaning it can compute anything computable, although it is not practical for real-world applications due to its inefficiency.
curious_fact: FRACTRAN is one of the simplest known Turing-complete languages, demonstrating the minimal requirements for computation.

name: FX-87
description: FX-87 is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and ease of learning. It was conceptualized in 1987 to introduce basic programming concepts to beginners in a fun and interactive way.
connectionsExplanation: FX-87 was inspired by the BASIC language, which was widely used in educational settings during the 1980s. Its design aims to capture the simplicity and accessibility of BASIC while introducing modern programming paradigms.
curious_fact: FX-87 was created to make programming accessible to children and beginners, using a simple syntax and interactive learning environment.

name: FXML
description: FXML (JavaFX Markup Language) is an XML-based language used for defining the user interface of JavaFX applications. It allows developers to separate the UI design from the application logic, making it easier to manage and maintain.
connectionsExplanation: FXML is a direct extension of JavaFX, which itself is a rich set of APIs for building cross-platform applications. FXML was introduced to provide a declarative way to define UI components, complementing the imperative approach of Java code. This separation of concerns enhances the development process by allowing designers to work on the UI without needing to understand the underlying Java code.
curious_fact: FXML was introduced in JavaFX 2.0 to simplify the creation of complex user interfaces.

name: FaceModelingLanguage
description: FaceModelingLanguage is a specialized programming language designed for creating and manipulating facial models in computer graphics and artificial intelligence applications. It focuses on providing a high-level, intuitive syntax for defining facial features and expressions.
connectionsExplanation: FaceModelingLanguage was developed as a specialized tool for the computer graphics industry, drawing inspiration from existing languages like Python and C++. Its primary purpose is to simplify the process of facial modeling, making it accessible to both professionals and hobbyists.

moons.name: FacialAnimationLib
moons.description: A library for animating facial expressions in FaceModelingLanguage.

moons.name: FacialRecognitionAPI
moons.description: An API for integrating facial recognition capabilities into FaceModelingLanguage applications.

curious_fact: FaceModelingLanguage was created by a team of computer graphics experts who wanted to streamline the process of facial modeling for both entertainment and medical applications.

name: Factor
description: Factor is a concatenative programming language with a strong emphasis on simplicity and efficiency. It is designed to be both powerful and easy to use, with a focus on functional programming paradigms.
connectionsExplanation: Factor was created by Slava Pestov in 2003. It is inspired by Forth and Lisp, aiming to combine the simplicity of Forth with the expressiveness of Lisp. Factor's design emphasizes the use of composable functions and a stack-based evaluation model, which sets it apart from traditional programming languages.

moons.name: FactorVM
moons.description: The virtual machine that runs Factor code.

moons.name: Factor Libraries
moons.description: A collection of libraries that extend Factor's functionality.

curious_fact: Factor's syntax is designed to be minimalistic, with a focus on readability and simplicity.

name: Falcon
description: Falcon is a high-level, dynamic programming language designed for web development. It was created to be fast and easy to use, with a syntax inspired by Python and Ruby. Falcon is particularly suited for building web applications and services.
connectionsExplanation: Falcon was developed to address the need for a modern, high-performance language for web development. It draws inspiration from Python and Ruby, aiming to combine their ease of use with the speed required for web applications. Falcon's design reflects the evolution of web development practices, focusing on performance and developer productivity.

moons.name: Falcon-WS
moons.description: A WebSocket extension for Falcon, enabling real-time communication between clients and servers.

moons.name: Falcon-HTTP
moons.description: An HTTP client library for Falcon, simplifying HTTP requests and responses.

curious_fact: Falcon was one of the first languages to introduce a built-in asynchronous framework, making it highly efficient for handling concurrent requests.
example:
from falcon import App, Response

def hello_world(req, resp):
    resp.text = 'Hello, World!'

app = App()
app.add_route('/', hello_world)

name: Family of Programming Languages
description: A conceptual framework for understanding the relationships between programming languages, rather than a specific language itself.
connectionsExplanation: The concept of a 'Family of Programming Languages' is a theoretical construct used to categorize and understand the relationships between different programming languages based on their design principles, syntax, and paradigms. It helps in identifying commonalities and differences among languages, aiding in language design and learning.
curious_fact: The term 'Family of Programming Languages' is not a specific language but a way to classify languages based on shared characteristics.

name: FamousWriter
description: FamousWriter is a fictional programming language designed for creative writing enthusiasts. It allows users to write stories and novels with a focus on narrative structure and character development.
connectionsExplanation: FamousWriter was created as a niche language for writers who wanted to integrate programming concepts into their creative process. It draws inspiration from natural language processing and storytelling techniques.
curious_fact: FamousWriter uses a unique syntax that mimics the flow of storytelling, making it easier for writers to translate their ideas into code.

name: FantasyGameBookEngine
description: FantasyGameBookEngine is a fictional programming language designed for creating interactive text-based adventure games, particularly those inspired by the Choose Your Own Adventure series. It allows developers to script complex narrative paths and branching storylines.
connectionsExplanation: FantasyGameBookEngine was inspired by the need for a language that could easily handle the intricate narrative structures of text-based adventure games. It draws parallels with early scripting languages used in game development, such as Inform, but with a focus on fantasy and interactive storytelling.
curious_fact: FantasyGameBookEngine was created by a group of game enthusiasts in the early 1980s to bring their imagination to life in digital form.
example: print "You find yourself in a dark forest.\nDo you want to go left or right?"

name: Fantom
description: Fantom is a modern, object-oriented programming language designed for the JVM and .NET platforms. It emphasizes simplicity, readability, and productivity, with a syntax that is clean and concise. Fantom is particularly suited for building web applications, services, and other distributed systems.
connectionsExplanation: Fantom was designed to be a modern alternative to Java and C#, offering a more concise syntax and improved tooling. It draws inspiration from languages like Java and C# but introduces its own unique features and paradigms, making it a distinct entry in the object-oriented programming landscape.

moons.name: Fantom Web
moons.description: A web framework for building web applications in Fantom.

moons.name: Fantom Database
moons.description: A database library for interacting with databases in Fantom.

curious_fact: Fantom's syntax is designed to be minimalistic, with a focus on reducing boilerplate code.
example: class Hello { static Void main() { echo("Hello, World!") } }

name: Feckfeck
description: Feckfeck is a fictional programming language designed for humor and esoteric purposes. It features a unique syntax and semantics that challenge conventional programming paradigms.
connectionsExplanation: Feckfeck was created as a playful response to the esoteric programming language community, drawing inspiration from the quirky and unconventional nature of languages like Brainfuck and Befunge.
curious_fact: Feckfeck's syntax is intentionally designed to be as confusing as possible, making it a favorite among programmers looking for a challenge.

name: Felix
description: Felix is a statically typed, multi-paradigm programming language designed to be a successor to C++. It aims to combine the performance of C++ with the safety and expressiveness of modern languages.
connectionsExplanation: Felix was developed as a research project at the University of Cambridge, aiming to address the limitations of C++ while incorporating features from other languages. It is not a direct descendant of any specific language but draws inspiration from C++ and other modern languages.
curious_fact: Felix was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of 'effect systems' to manage side effects in a more controlled manner.

name: FenixProject
description: FenixProject is a modern, high-performance programming language designed for concurrent and distributed systems. It emphasizes safety, reliability, and ease of use, making it suitable for large-scale applications. Despite its recent emergence, FenixProject has garnered attention for its innovative approach to memory management and concurrency.
connectionsExplanation: FenixProject was developed as a successor to the Phoenix language, which was known for its robust error handling and efficient memory management. The development team aimed to build upon the strengths of Phoenix while addressing its limitations, resulting in a language that is both powerful and user-friendly.

moons.name: FenixLib
moons.description: A comprehensive library for distributed systems and concurrent programming.

moons.name: FenixNet
moons.description: A networking framework optimized for high-performance communication in FenixProject applications.

curious_fact: FenixProject was named after the mythical phoenix, symbolizing rebirth and renewal in the world of programming languages.

name: Fennel
description: Fennel is a Lisp-like scripting language that compiles to Lua. It aims to provide a more expressive and concise syntax while maintaining the performance and flexibility of Lua.
connectionsExplanation: Fennel is inspired by Lisp and is designed to be a more modern and user-friendly alternative to Lua. It compiles to Lua, allowing it to leverage the existing Lua ecosystem and performance.
curious_fact: Fennel was created to make Lua more accessible to programmers familiar with Lisp-like languages.

name: Ferite
description: Ferite is a high-level, object-oriented programming language designed for rapid application development. It was created by Andrew Collington and is known for its simplicity and ease of use. Ferite is not widely used today, but it was influential in its time.
connectionsExplanation: Ferite was developed as an alternative to existing scripting languages, aiming to provide a more intuitive and efficient environment. It was influenced by languages like C++ and Python, but with a focus on simplicity and ease of use. Ferite's design was intended to make it accessible to developers without requiring extensive programming knowledge.
curious_fact: Ferite was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of 'virtual classes', which allowed for more flexible and dynamic object-oriented programming.

name: Ficl
description: Ficl is a Forth-inspired concatenative programming language designed for embedded systems. It is known for its small footprint and portability, making it suitable for resource-constrained environments.
connectionsExplanation: Ficl is a descendant of Forth, a stack-based language developed in the 1970s. It was created to provide a lightweight, flexible Forth implementation for various embedded systems, particularly those in the telecommunications industry.
curious_fact: Ficl stands for 'Forth Inspired Command Language'.

name: FileMakerScript
description: FileMakerScript is a scripting language developed by FileMaker Inc. for automating tasks within the FileMaker database platform. It is primarily used to create custom functions, scripts, and calculations to enhance the functionality of FileMaker databases.
connectionsExplanation: FileMakerScript is closely tied to the FileMaker database platform, which was first released in 1983. It evolved from the need to automate repetitive tasks within FileMaker databases, making it a specialized language for database management and automation.
curious_fact: FileMakerScript is one of the few scripting languages designed specifically for database management, making it unique in the programming landscape.
example:
Set Variable [ $greeting ; Value: "Hello, World!" ] 
Show Custom Dialog [ "Greeting" ; $greeting ]

name: Filetab
description: Filetab is a fictional programming language designed for simplicity and ease of use, particularly for beginners. It emphasizes readability and minimal syntax, making it an ideal choice for educational purposes.
connectionsExplanation: Filetab was inspired by the desire to create a language that could be easily learned and used by beginners. It draws inspiration from BASIC and Python, combining their simplicity with modern language features.
curious_fact: Filetab was created as part of a university project to explore the impact of language design on learning outcomes.

name: Fjölnir
description: Fjölnir is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It is inspired by the Icelandic language and culture, aiming to introduce programming concepts in a culturally relevant way.
connectionsExplanation: Fjölnir was created as an educational tool to introduce programming concepts to Icelandic students. While it is not derived from any existing language, it draws inspiration from the structure and syntax of the Icelandic language, making it unique and culturally significant.
curious_fact: Fjölnir's syntax is designed to mimic Icelandic grammar, making it a unique language for language learners.

name: FlagShip
description: FlagShip is a high-level programming language developed by Microsoft for the CP/M operating system. It was designed to be easy to use and to provide a structured approach to programming. Despite its initial popularity, FlagShip's usage has declined over the years.
connectionsExplanation: FlagShip was developed in the early 1980s as a structured programming language for CP/M systems. It was influenced by the BASIC language but aimed to provide more robust features and a more structured syntax. The decline in CP/M's popularity and the rise of more advanced operating systems led to a decrease in FlagShip's usage.
curious_fact: FlagShip was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of structured programming to CP/M users.

name: Flapjax
description: Flapjax is a JavaScript library designed to simplify the development of AJAX-based web applications. It provides a functional reactive programming style to manage asynchronous data flows, making it easier to handle dynamic content updates without reloading the page.
connectionsExplanation: Flapjax was developed as a research project at Harvard University to address the challenges of building complex, interactive web applications. It builds upon the functional programming paradigm, inspired by languages like Haskell, to manage state and side effects in a more declarative manner.
curious_fact: Flapjax was one of the early attempts to bring functional programming concepts to JavaScript, aiming to improve the manageability of asynchronous web applications.
example: var model = flapjax.lift(function(input) { return input * 2; }, inputSignal);

name: Flavors
description: Flavors is a fictional programming language designed to explore the concept of syntactic diversity. It allows developers to write code in various flavors or styles, making it highly adaptable and expressive.
connectionsExplanation: Flavors was created as an experimental language to study the impact of syntax on code readability and maintainability. It draws inspiration from a variety of existing languages, aiming to provide a unique experience for developers.
curious_fact: Flavors introduced the concept of 'flavor profiles' to allow developers to switch between different syntactic styles within the same program.

name: Flex
description: Flex is a tool for generating scanners, also known as lexical analyzers, in the field of computer science. It is used to recognize lexical patterns in source code, which are then used by parsers to understand the syntax of the program.
connectionsExplanation: Flex evolved from the older Lex tool, which was developed in the 1970s. Lex was part of the Unix operating system, and Flex is its modern successor, providing more features and better performance.
curious_fact: Flex is written in C and can generate C code for the scanners it creates.

name: Flix
description: Flix is a purely functional programming language designed for high-performance computing. It emphasizes immutability, type safety, and concurrency, making it suitable for large-scale applications.
connectionsExplanation: Flix is inspired by functional programming languages like Haskell and Scala. It aims to provide a modern, efficient, and expressive language for concurrent and parallel programming.

moons.name: Flix Standard Library
moons.description: A comprehensive library providing essential data structures and algorithms.

curious_fact: Flix compiles to JVM bytecode and native code, offering both portability and performance.
example: def main(): Unit \ IO = println("Hello, World!")

name: FlowJava
description: FlowJava is a modern programming language designed to simplify concurrent programming by using a flow-based approach. It aims to make it easier to write correct and efficient concurrent applications.
connectionsExplanation: FlowJava is inspired by the principles of functional programming and concurrent programming paradigms. It draws inspiration from languages like Haskell and Erlang, focusing on immutability and explicit concurrency models.
curious_fact: FlowJava introduced a novel syntax for handling concurrency that emphasizes data flow over thread management.
example: public class HelloWorld { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hello, World!"); } }

name: Flowcode
description: Flowcode is a graphical programming environment designed for microcontrollers and embedded systems. It allows users to create programs by connecting blocks representing code functions, making it accessible for beginners and hobbyists.
connectionsExplanation: Flowcode was developed by Matrix Multimedia, which has a history of creating educational and development tools for microcontrollers. It is not directly derived from any specific programming language but is inspired by the need for a more intuitive approach to programming embedded systems.
curious_fact: Flowcode introduced a unique block-based programming system that revolutionized how microcontroller programming was taught and practiced.
example:
Start
  Set LED1 to ON
  Wait 1 second
  Set LED1 to OFF
End

name: Flowgorithm
description: Flowgorithm is a visual programming language designed to teach programming concepts to beginners. It uses a flowchart-based interface to represent algorithms, making it accessible for those new to programming.
connectionsExplanation: Flowgorithm was developed to simplify the learning process of programming by using a visual, flowchart-based approach. It is not directly derived from any traditional programming language but is inspired by the need for an intuitive introduction to programming concepts.
curious_fact: Flowgorithm was created by Richard G. Baldwin, a professor at Austin Community College.

name: Formulalanguage
description: Formulalanguage is a programming language developed in the 1960s for mathematical and scientific computations. It was designed to be a high-level language that could express complex mathematical formulas succinctly.
connectionsExplanation: Formulalanguage was developed at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) as part of the MAD project, which aimed to create a language for mathematical computations. It was influenced by earlier languages like FORTRAN and aimed to provide a more intuitive way to express mathematical formulas.
curious_fact: Formulalanguage was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of arrays and matrices as first-class data types.

name: Forsythe
description: Forsythe is a programming language developed in the 1960s at Stanford University. It was designed for scientific and numerical computations, particularly in the field of linear algebra. Forsythe is notable for its early adoption of structured programming principles.
connectionsExplanation: Forsythe was developed during the early days of computer science when structured programming was gaining prominence. It was influenced by the need for more readable and maintainable code in scientific applications, which led to its design principles.
curious_fact: Forsythe was one of the first languages to introduce structured programming concepts, which significantly influenced the development of modern programming languages.

name: Forte4GL
description: Forte4GL is a 4GL (Fourth Generation Language) designed to simplify database application development. It is based on the concept of writing programs in a high-level language that closely resembles natural language, making it accessible to users with little programming experience.
connectionsExplanation: Forte4GL is a descendant of the 4GL paradigm, which emerged in the 1980s as a response to the complexity of third-generation languages. It builds upon the principles of simplifying database access and application development, aiming to reduce the need for extensive programming knowledge.
curious_fact: Forte4GL was developed to provide a more user-friendly environment for database applications, particularly for those without extensive programming backgrounds.
example:
PROGRAM HelloWorld
  DISPLAY 'Hello, World!'
END PROGRAM

name: Forth
description: Forth is a stack-based programming language originally developed by Charles Moore in the early 1970s. It is known for its minimalism and the ability to define new words with ease, making it highly extensible. Forth is often used in embedded systems and real-time applications.
connectionsExplanation: Forth is considered a direct descendant of the stack-based language PostScript, which was developed by Adobe Systems. Both languages share the stack-based paradigm and the ability to define new words dynamically. Forth's influence can also be seen in the development of other stack-based languages like Factor.

moons.name: Gforth
moons.description: A portable, fast, and standards-compliant implementation of Forth.

moons.name: ForthSharp
moons.description: A Forth-like language implemented in C#.

curious_fact: Forth was one of the first languages to use reverse Polish notation (RPN) for arithmetic operations.

name: Fortran
description: Fortran (FORmula TRANslation) is a high-level programming language primarily used for scientific and engineering applications. It was one of the first high-level languages and is known for its efficiency in numerical computations.
connectionsExplanation: Fortran was developed by IBM in the 1950s to facilitate the translation of mathematical formulas into machine code. It was a pioneering language that laid the groundwork for modern programming languages by introducing concepts like subroutines and arrays.

moons.name: Fortran 77
moons.description: Fortran 77 is a standardized version of Fortran, published in 1978, which added several features to the original language.

moons.name: Fortran 90
moons.description: Fortran 90, released in 1991, introduced modern features such as modules, pointers, and dynamic memory allocation, making it more versatile and powerful.

moons.name: Fortran 95
moons.description: Fortran 95, published in 1997, was a minor update to Fortran 90, adding support for more advanced features and improving the language's usability.

moons.name: Fortran 2003
moons.description: Fortran 2003, released in 2004, introduced object-oriented programming features, further enhancing the language's capabilities for complex scientific computations.

moons.name: Fortran 2008
moons.description: Fortran 2008, published in 2008, added more object-oriented features, coarrays for parallel programming, and other enhancements to improve performance and functionality.

moons.name: Fortran 2018
moons.description: Fortran 2018, released in 2018, introduced further improvements, including support for complex data types, improved array handling, and other enhancements to modernize the language.

curious_fact: Fortran was named after its purpose: FORmula TRANslation, reflecting its initial use in translating mathematical formulas into machine code.
example:
program hello
  print *, 'Hello, World!'
end program hello

name: Fortress
description: Fortress is a programming language designed for high-performance numerical computing. It was developed by Sun Microsystems as part of the High Performance Computing Group. Fortress aimed to simplify parallel programming and improve performance on multi-core systems.
connectionsExplanation: Fortress was inspired by the need for a language that could handle complex numerical computations efficiently. It evolved from the desire to address the challenges of parallel programming in the early 2000s, drawing on concepts from functional programming and domain-specific languages.

moons.name: Parallel Collections
moons.description: A library for parallel data processing.

moons.name: Numerical Libraries
moons.description: Libraries for mathematical computations.

curious_fact: Fortress was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of 'regions' for managing memory and parallelism.

name: FoxPro
description: FoxPro is a relational database management system and programming language developed by Microsoft. It was designed to be user-friendly and efficient for database management and application development.
connectionsExplanation: FoxPro evolved from dBASE III, which was a popular database management system in the 1980s. It was later integrated into Visual FoxPro, which added object-oriented features and a graphical user interface.

moons.name: Visual FoxPro
moons.description: An enhanced version of FoxPro with object-oriented features and a graphical user interface.

curious_fact: FoxPro was one of the first database systems to support SQL, making it a precursor to modern relational database management systems.

name: FpgaC
description: FpgaC is a specialized programming language designed for writing hardware description languages targeting Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs). It aims to provide a high-level abstraction for FPGA programming, making it accessible to developers without deep hardware design expertise.
connectionsExplanation: FpgaC is inspired by C and Verilog, two widely used languages in hardware design. It combines the simplicity and familiarity of C with the hardware-specific capabilities of Verilog to facilitate FPGA programming. This makes FpgaC a unique tool in the FPGA development ecosystem, bridging the gap between software and hardware design.
curious_fact: FpgaC was developed by a team of hardware and software engineers at a leading semiconductor company.

name: Frame-based Editor
description: Frame-based Editor is a specialized text editor developed in the 1980s that uses a frame-based approach to editing. It was designed to facilitate the creation and manipulation of structured documents, particularly in the context of hypertext systems. The editor's unique interface allowed users to visually organize and edit text within a frame-based environment, which was innovative for its time.
connectionsExplanation: Frame-based Editor emerged during the early days of hypertext development, influenced by the need for more flexible and structured text editing. It was part of a broader movement towards more sophisticated text processing tools that could handle complex document structures. The editor's frame-based approach was inspired by the concept of frames in artificial intelligence, which aimed to represent knowledge in a more human-like manner.
curious_fact: Frame-based Editor was one of the early tools that contributed to the development of modern hypertext systems.
example:
frame("document", [
  frame("title", "My Document"),
  frame("content", "This is the content of the document.")
])

name: FranzLisp
description: FranzLisp is a dialect of the Lisp programming language developed by Franz Inc. It was one of the first Lisp implementations and was widely used in academic and research environments. FranzLisp introduced several innovations, including the concept of garbage collection and the development of the Franz Lisp Machine, which was one of the first commercial Lisp machines.
connectionsExplanation: FranzLisp is a direct descendant of the original Lisp language, developed by John McCarthy and his team at MIT in the late 1950s. It was one of the first Lisp dialects and played a significant role in the evolution of Lisp programming.
curious_fact: FranzLisp was used to develop the first commercial Lisp machine, which significantly influenced the development of Lisp-based systems.

name: FreeBASIC
description: FreeBASIC is an open-source, 32-bit BASIC compiler with syntax similar to QuickBASIC, but with additional features such as pointers, inline assembly, and object-oriented programming. It is designed for Windows and Linux platforms.
connectionsExplanation: FreeBASIC evolved from QuickBASIC, which was a popular dialect of the BASIC programming language. It maintains compatibility with QuickBASIC while adding modern features, making it suitable for both beginners and experienced programmers.

moons.name: FBIDE
moons.description: Integrated Development Environment for FreeBASIC.

curious_fact: FreeBASIC supports both 32-bit and 64-bit architectures, allowing for cross-platform development.

name: FreeFem++
description: FreeFem++ is a high-level finite element software for numerically solving partial differential equations (PDEs) in 2D and 3D. It is particularly well-suited for problems involving fluid mechanics, elasticity, and other fields requiring complex geometries and boundary conditions.
connectionsExplanation: FreeFem++ evolved from earlier versions of FreeFem, which was developed in the late 1980s. It builds upon the principles of finite element methods and incorporates modern C++ features to enhance usability and performance. The language is designed to be intuitive for mathematicians and engineers who need to implement numerical simulations.

moons.name: FreeFem++ Mesh
moons.description: A library for mesh generation and manipulation within FreeFem++.

moons.name: FreeFem++ Solver
moons.description: A module for solving PDEs using various numerical methods.

curious_fact: FreeFem++ was originally developed by Frederic Hecht at the University of Paris 6.
example: mesh Th = square(10, 10); fespace Vh(Th, P1); Vh u, v; solve Poisson(u, v) = int2d(Th)(dx(u)*dx(v) + dy(u)*dy(v)) - int2d(Th)(f*v) + on(1, 2, 3, 4, u=0); plot(u);

name: Frege
description: Frege is a purely functional programming language that compiles to Java bytecode. It is designed to be a practical alternative to Haskell, aiming to integrate seamlessly with the Java ecosystem.
connectionsExplanation: Frege was developed by Ingo Wechsung as a way to bring the benefits of functional programming to Java developers. It is inspired by Haskell and aims to provide a similar expressive power while being interoperable with Java.
curious_fact: Frege is named after the German mathematician Gottlob Frege, who is considered one of the founders of modern logic.
example:
module Main where
main = println "Hello, World!"

name: FreiburgerCode
description: FreiburgerCode is a lesser-known programming language developed in the 1970s at the University of Freiburg in Germany. It was designed for educational purposes and is not widely used today. The language is notable for its unique syntax and structure, which diverge from mainstream programming paradigms.
connectionsExplanation: FreiburgerCode was developed as part of a research project aimed at exploring new programming paradigms. While it does not have a direct lineage from earlier languages, it shares some conceptual similarities with early functional programming languages like Lisp, which were also developed in the 1950s and 1960s.
curious_fact: FreiburgerCode was one of the first languages to incorporate features inspired by natural language processing techniques.

name: Frenetic
description: Frenetic is a domain-specific language (DSL) designed for programming network applications. It is particularly suited for specifying and implementing network policies and behaviors in software-defined networking (SDN) environments. Frenetic is built on top of the OpenFlow protocol and is used to define how data packets should be processed and routed within a network.
connectionsExplanation: Frenetic is inspired by the need for more expressive and efficient ways to program network behaviors in SDN. It draws from the principles of functional programming and is designed to be integrated with existing SDN controllers. Frenetic's DSL allows network administrators and developers to specify complex network policies using a high-level, declarative syntax, which simplifies the process of network configuration and management.
curious_fact: Frenetic was developed at the University of California, Berkeley, as part of research into SDN and network programming.

name: FriCAS
description: FriCAS is a general-purpose computer algebra system (CAS) that is inspired by Axiom. It is designed to be a powerful tool for symbolic mathematics, with a focus on algebraic computations. FriCAS is written in Common Lisp and is known for its strong type system and extensive library of mathematical functions.
connectionsExplanation: FriCAS evolved from Axiom, a similar computer algebra system. Both are rooted in the tradition of symbolic computation and share a common goal of providing a comprehensive environment for mathematical exploration. FriCAS builds upon the foundational work of Axiom, incorporating improvements and additional features to enhance its capabilities.

moons.name: FriCAS Library
moons.description: A vast collection of mathematical algorithms and functions.

moons.name: FriCAS Spad Compiler
moons.description: A compiler for the Spad programming language used in FriCAS.

curious_fact: FriCAS supports a wide range of mathematical domains, including algebraic numbers, polynomials, and differential equations.

name: Fril
description: Fril is an esoteric programming language designed to challenge the programmer's creativity and problem-solving skills. It features a unique syntax and unconventional approach to programming, making it a favorite among puzzle enthusiasts.
connectionsExplanation: Fril was created as a modern take on esoteric programming languages, drawing inspiration from the minimalist and unconventional nature of Brainfuck. Its design encourages programmers to think outside the box and develop innovative solutions.
curious_fact: Fril uses a custom alphabet for its syntax, which adds an extra layer of complexity to the language.

name: Frink
description: Frink is a practical calculating tool and programming language designed to make physical calculations simple, fast, and accurate. It is particularly useful for scientists, engineers, and anyone who needs to perform complex calculations involving units of measurement.
connectionsExplanation: Frink was developed by Alan Eliasen as a tool for his own use and was inspired by the need for a language that could handle units of measurement seamlessly. It is not directly descended from any traditional programming language but draws from a variety of paradigms to provide a unique approach to computation.
curious_fact: Frink can automatically convert between units of measurement, such as converting miles to kilometers or Celsius to Fahrenheit, without any additional code.

name: FullBASIC
description: FullBASIC is a dialect of the BASIC programming language, designed to be a full-featured version of BASIC with additional capabilities. It was developed in the early 1980s and was popular on various home computers of that era.
connectionsExplanation: FullBASIC evolved from the earlier versions of BASIC, which were developed in the late 1950s and early 1960s. It aimed to provide a more comprehensive set of features and better performance compared to its predecessors, making it a significant step in the evolution of the BASIC language family.
curious_fact: FullBASIC introduced several advanced features for its time, including structured programming constructs and improved graphics capabilities.

name: Futhark
description: Futhark is a purely functional programming language designed for high-performance computing, particularly for parallel and vectorized computations. It is inspired by Haskell and is used for writing programs that can run efficiently on multicore processors and GPUs.
connectionsExplanation: Futhark is inspired by Haskell, a purely functional language, and is designed to leverage parallel computing architectures. Its development was influenced by the need for efficient, high-performance programming in scientific computing and data analysis.

moons.name: Futhark CUDA
moons.description: A backend for Futhark that compiles to CUDA for GPU execution.

moons.name: Futhark OpenCL
moons.description: A backend for Futhark that compiles to OpenCL for GPU execution.

curious_fact: Futhark's name is a reference to the Futhark alphabet, an ancient runic script used in Scandinavia.
example:
let add (x: i32) (y: i32) = x + y
let main = add 2 3

name: FutureBASIC
description: FutureBASIC is a dialect of the BASIC programming language designed for the Apple Macintosh. It was developed by Dave Sisler and was first released in 1987. FutureBASIC is known for its ease of use and its ability to create native Macintosh applications. It is particularly popular among hobbyists and small developers.
connectionsExplanation: FutureBASIC evolved from the BASIC programming language, which was one of the first high-level programming languages.
