It was specifically designed to take advantage of the Macintosh operating system and its graphical user interface, making it a significant step in the evolution of Macintosh software development.
curious_fact: FutureBASIC was one of the first languages to support the development of Macintosh applications with a graphical user interface.

name: FuzzyControlLanguage
description: FuzzyControlLanguage (FCL) is a programming language designed for the development of fuzzy logic systems. It provides a structured way to implement fuzzy logic controllers, which are used in various applications like robotics, control systems, and artificial intelligence.
connectionsExplanation: FuzzyControlLanguage was developed to simplify the implementation of fuzzy logic systems. It builds upon the principles of fuzzy logic, which was first introduced in the 1960s. FCL aims to provide a more accessible and efficient way to create fuzzy logic applications compared to traditional programming languages.
curious_fact: FCL was one of the first languages to integrate fuzzy logic directly into its syntax, making it easier for developers to implement complex control systems.
example: IF temperature IS hot THEN fan IS high

name: G-Portugol
description: G-Portugol is a programming language designed to teach programming concepts to Brazilian students. It is based on Portuguese syntax, making it accessible for those learning to code in their native language.
connectionsExplanation: G-Portugol was developed in Brazil to facilitate the teaching of programming to students who are more comfortable with Portuguese. It is not directly derived from any other language but is inspired by structured programming paradigms.
curious_fact: G-Portugol was created to reduce the language barrier for Brazilian students learning to code.

name: G-code
description: G-code is a language used for controlling the movements of CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machines. It is widely used in manufacturing and machining industries to automate the operation of machine tools.
connectionsExplanation: G-code was developed by the MIT Servomechanisms Laboratory in the 1950s. It was designed to provide a standardized way to control the motion of machine tools, which was crucial for the burgeoning manufacturing industry. The language has evolved over the years to include a wide range of commands for various machine functions.
curious_fact: G-code is one of the oldest programming languages still in widespread use today.

name: GAP
description: GAP (Groups, Algorithms, Programming) is a system for computational discrete algebra, with particular emphasis on computational group theory. It provides a programming language, a library of thousands of functions implementing algebraic algorithms written in the GAP language, and large data libraries of algebraic objects.
connectionsExplanation: GAP was developed by a team at the University of St Andrews in Scotland. It is primarily used in research and teaching for studying groups and their representations, rings, vector spaces, algebras, combinatorial structures, and more. GAP is built on a foundation of earlier work in symbolic computation and has influenced the development of other algebraic software systems.

moons.name: GAPDoc
moons.description: A package for GAP that provides tools for writing documentation in GAP.

moons.name: Polycyclic
moons.description: A package for computing with polycyclic groups.

curious_fact: GAP was one of the first systems to provide a comprehensive library of group theory algorithms.

name: GAUSS
description: GAUSS is a matrix programming language developed by Aptech Systems. It is widely used in econometrics, statistics, and financial analysis. GAUSS is known for its speed and efficiency in handling large datasets.
connectionsExplanation: GAUSS was developed in the 1980s as a specialized tool for econometricians and statisticians. It evolved from earlier matrix-oriented languages and was designed to handle complex statistical computations efficiently.

moons.name: GAUSS Engine
moons.description: A high-performance computing engine for GAUSS.

moons.name: GAUSS Graphics
moons.description: A library for creating advanced graphics and visualizations in GAUSS.

curious_fact: GAUSS was named after the mathematician Carl Friedrich Gauss, known for his contributions to number theory and statistics.

name: GDScript
description: GDScript is a high-level, dynamically typed scripting language designed for use with the Godot Engine. It is inspired by Python and is known for its simplicity and ease of use, making it popular among beginners and game developers.
connectionsExplanation: GDScript was created by Juan Linietsky, the founder of Godot Engine, as the primary scripting language for the engine. It is designed to be intuitive and efficient, closely aligning with the Godot Engine's architecture and workflow.

moons.name: Godot Engine
moons.description: The game engine that uses GDScript as its primary scripting language.

curious_fact: GDScript was originally called 'VisualScript' and was later renamed to GDScript to better reflect its syntax and capabilities.

name: Generic
description: Generic is a fictional programming language designed for illustrative purposes. It is a high-level language with a simple syntax, intended to demonstrate basic programming concepts without focusing on specific paradigms or optimizations.
connectionsExplanation: Generic is a hypothetical language created for educational and illustrative purposes. It is not based on any existing language but serves as a generic example to discuss programming language features and concepts.
curious_fact: Generic was invented to simplify the explanation of programming concepts without being tied to any specific language's syntax or features.

name: GEORGE
description: GEORGE is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It features a simple syntax and is often used in introductory computer science courses to teach basic programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: GEORGE was created as an educational tool in the early 1960s, inspired by the need for a language that could be easily understood by beginners. It is not based on any existing language but shares some similarities with BASIC in its simplicity and approachability.
curious_fact: GEORGE was named after the fictional character George Orwell, reflecting its role in teaching about the impact of technology on society.

name: GFABASIC
description: GFABASIC is a specialized BASIC dialect designed for educational purposes, particularly in the field of computer graphics. It was developed to introduce students to programming concepts while allowing them to create simple graphical applications.
connectionsExplanation: GFABASIC evolved from standard BASIC, incorporating additional commands and functions specifically tailored for graphical operations. Its development was influenced by the growing demand for educational software in the early 1980s, aiming to make programming accessible to beginners.
curious_fact: GFABASIC was one of the first BASIC dialects to include built-in graphics commands, making it a pioneer in educational programming languages.

name: GNU Awk
description: GNU Awk (gawk) is an extension of the classic Unix awk command, providing additional features and performance improvements. It is widely used for text processing and scripting tasks.
connectionsExplanation: GNU Awk evolved from the original awk, which was developed in the 1970s. It is part of the GNU project and is known for its powerful text processing capabilities, making it a popular tool among Unix and Linux users.
curious_fact: GNU Awk introduced the 'nextfile' statement, which allows skipping the rest of the current file and moving to the next one.

name: GNU Common Lisp
description: GNU Common Lisp (GCL) is an implementation of the Common Lisp programming language. It is known for its portability and adherence to the ANSI Common Lisp standard. GCL is often used in academic settings and for research purposes.
connectionsExplanation: GNU Common Lisp is a direct descendant of Common Lisp, which itself evolved from earlier Lisp dialects like LISP 1.5 and Maclisp. It adheres to the ANSI Common Lisp standard, making it a significant part of the Lisp family tree.
curious_fact: GNU Common Lisp is one of the oldest implementations of Common Lisp, dating back to the 1980s.

name: GNUE
description: GNUE is a fictional programming language designed to explore the intersection of functional programming and artificial intelligence. It was created as part of a thought experiment to understand how language design could influence AI capabilities.
connectionsExplanation: GNUE was inspired by the desire to create a language that could seamlessly integrate AI algorithms into its syntax. While it has no direct historical predecessors, it draws conceptual influences from Lisp and Haskell, particularly in its functional paradigms.
curious_fact: GNUE was one of the first languages to incorporate AI-driven code optimization at compile time.

name: GNU Guile
description: GNU Guile is an implementation of the Scheme programming language, designed to be embedded in applications. It provides a rich set of libraries and is used in various GNU projects.
connectionsExplanation: GNU Guile is a direct descendant of Scheme, which was developed in the late 1970s at MIT. It is part of the GNU project and is intended to be a versatile extension language for applications.

moons.name: Slib
moons.description: A portable Scheme library.

moons.name: Guix
moons.description: A package manager and system configuration tool.

curious_fact: GNU Guile supports multiple programming paradigms, including procedural, functional, and object-oriented programming.

name: GNUMathProg
description: GNUMathProg is a modeling language for mathematical programming problems. It is part of the GNU project and is used for linear programming, mixed-integer programming, and other optimization tasks.
connectionsExplanation: GNUMathProg is a part of the GNU project and is closely related to other GNU tools. It is built on top of the GLPK (GNU Linear Programming Kit) library, which provides the necessary algorithms for solving mathematical programming problems.
curious_fact: GNUMathProg is known for its integration with other GNU tools and its use in academic and research environments.
example: model { set I := 1..3; var x{I} >= 0; maximize z: sum{i in I} x[i]; s.t. c1: sum{i in I} i*x[i] <= 10; }

name: GNU Octave
description: GNU Octave is a high-level interpreted language primarily intended for numerical computations. It is compatible with MATLAB and is widely used in scientific and engineering applications.
connectionsExplanation: GNU Octave was developed to be a free and open-source alternative to MATLAB. It shares many features and syntax with MATLAB, making it a direct descendant in the numerical computing domain.

moons.name: Octave Forge
moons.description: A collection of packages providing additional functionality to GNU Octave.

curious_fact: GNU Octave was named after the mathematician and inventor of the first computer, Charles Babbage.

name: GNU Prolog
description: GNU Prolog is an open-source Prolog compiler and runtime system. It is based on the ISO Prolog standard and is known for its efficiency and portability. GNU Prolog is written in C and is available on various platforms.
connectionsExplanation: GNU Prolog is a direct descendant of the ISO Prolog standard, which itself evolved from earlier Prolog systems like Edinburgh Prolog. It maintains compatibility with these systems while adding modern features and optimizations.
curious_fact: GNU Prolog was developed by Daniel Diaz, who also created the SWI-Prolog system.

name: GNUepsilon
description: GNUepsilon is a lesser-known programming language developed in the early 2000s. It is designed to be a simple and efficient language for educational purposes and small-scale applications. Despite its simplicity, it incorporates some advanced features that make it suitable for more complex tasks.
connectionsExplanation: GNUepsilon was developed as an educational tool and is not directly derived from any major programming language. It was inspired by the simplicity of BASIC and the structured approach of Pascal, aiming to provide a balance between ease of learning and functionality.
curious_fact: GNUepsilon was one of the first languages to introduce a simple syntax for handling arrays and loops, making it popular in introductory programming courses.

name: GOAL
description: GOAL is an object-oriented programming language developed by Object Technology International (OTI) in the early 1990s. It was designed to be a modern, efficient language for building distributed applications. GOAL was influenced by Smalltalk and C++.
connectionsExplanation: GOAL was developed as a successor to Smalltalk, aiming to combine the dynamic features of Smalltalk with the efficiency and performance of C++. It was part of the Object Technology System (OTS), which was intended to provide a comprehensive environment for object-oriented programming.
curious_fact: GOAL was one of the first languages to support distributed object-oriented programming, allowing objects to be located on different machines.

name: GPM
description: GPM is a modern, high-level programming language designed for rapid application development. It emphasizes simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible to beginners while still offering powerful features for experienced developers.
connectionsExplanation: GPM was developed as a successor to the popular scripting language XYZ, aiming to improve performance and add modern features. It draws inspiration from both functional and object-oriented paradigms, making it versatile for various programming tasks.

moons.description: A framework for building web applications with GPM.

moons.name: GPM-Data
moons.description: A library for data manipulation and analysis in GPM.

curious_fact: GPM was created by a team of developers who previously worked on the XYZ project.

name: GPSS
description: GPSS (General Purpose Simulation System) is a high-level programming language designed for the simulation of discrete-event systems. It was one of the first simulation languages and was widely used in the 1960s and 1970s for various applications, including manufacturing, transportation, and computer systems.
connectionsExplanation: GPSS was developed at IBM in 1959 by Saul Gass and others. It was one of the pioneering simulation languages and influenced the development of subsequent simulation languages. GPSS is considered a direct descendant of the analytical-engine-order-code, as it represents an early attempt to create a language specifically for simulation purposes.
curious_fact: GPSS was one of the first languages to use a graphical interface for simulation modeling.
example:
PROCESS SIMULATION
BEGIN
  ASSIGN 1 TO A
  ASSIGN 2 TO B
  PRINT A, B
END

name: GRASS
description: GRASS (Geographic Resources Analysis Support System) is an open-source software suite used for geographic information system (GIS) analysis, image processing, and spatial modeling. It is widely used in academia and research for environmental and natural resource management.
connectionsExplanation: GRASS was developed at the U.S. Army Construction Engineering Research Laboratory (CERL) in Champaign, Illinois. It was inspired by the need for a comprehensive GIS system that could handle large datasets and complex spatial analysis. GRASS is not directly derived from any other language but shares some similarities with other GIS software like ArcGIS and QGIS.

moons.name: GRASS GIS
moons.description: A component of GRASS for geographic information system analysis.

moons.name: GRASS GIS Addons
moons.description: A collection of additional modules and tools for GRASS GIS.

curious_fact: GRASS was one of the first GIS systems to support 3D raster data.
example:
g.region n=40 s=30 w=-100 e=-90
r.mapcalc "elevation = 1000"
g.region -p

name: GSQL
description: GSQL is a declarative graph query language developed by Neo4j. It is designed to work with graph databases, allowing users to express complex graph patterns and queries efficiently.
connectionsExplanation: GSQL is a specialized language developed by Neo4j to work with graph databases. It is not directly derived from any other language but is inspired by SQL and graph theory principles. Its primary purpose is to facilitate querying and manipulating graph data structures.
curious_fact: GSQL allows users to write queries that traverse graph structures in a way that is both intuitive and efficient.

name: GScript
description: GScript is a modern, high-level programming language designed for rapid application development. It emphasizes simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible to beginners while still offering powerful features for experienced developers.
connectionsExplanation: GScript was developed as a successor to JavaScript, aiming to address some of the limitations and complexities of its predecessor. It inherits many of JavaScript's paradigms but introduces new syntax and features to enhance developer productivity.

moons.name: GWeb
moons.description: A framework for building web applications with GScript.

moons.name: GMobile
moons.description: A framework for developing mobile applications using GScript.

curious_fact: GScript was created by a team of former JavaScript developers who wanted to streamline the language without sacrificing its versatility.

name: GT.M
description: GT.M is a high-performance, multi-platform implementation of the M programming language, developed by InterSystems. It is widely used in healthcare and financial systems for its robust transaction processing capabilities.
connectionsExplanation: GT.M is a direct descendant of the M programming language, which was developed at the Massachusetts General Hospital in the 1960s. It inherits the M language's strengths in handling complex data structures and transaction processing, making it particularly suited for healthcare applications.
curious_fact: GT.M is known for its ability to handle large volumes of concurrent transactions, making it a critical component in many healthcare systems worldwide.

name: GW-BASIC
description: GW-BASIC, or Microsoft GW-BASIC, is a version of the BASIC programming language developed by Microsoft for the MS-DOS operating system. It was widely used in the early days of personal computing for educational purposes and simple applications.
connectionsExplanation: GW-BASIC evolved from Dartmouth BASIC, which was one of the earliest versions of BASIC. It was later succeeded by QBASIC and Visual Basic, maintaining the BASIC paradigm but with enhanced features and a more user-friendly interface.
curious_fact: GW-BASIC was included with many early IBM PC computers, making it accessible to a wide audience.

name: Gallina
description: Gallina is a dialect of the Coq proof assistant, designed to be more user-friendly and accessible. It is primarily used in formal verification and proof development.
connectionsExplanation: Gallina is a dialect of Coq, which was developed in the early 1980s at INRIA in France. It extends Coq's syntax to make it more intuitive for users, particularly those new to formal verification.
curious_fact: Gallina is named after the French word for chicken, reflecting the language's origins in French academia.

name: Gambas
description: Gambas is a free software development environment based on a dialect of the BASIC programming language. It is designed to be easy to use and is particularly popular in France. Gambas supports object-oriented programming and is built on top of the GTK+ toolkit.
connectionsExplanation: Gambas is a dialect of BASIC, which has a long history dating back to the 1960s. It was developed to provide a modern, object-oriented approach to BASIC programming, making it accessible for beginners while offering powerful features for experienced developers.

moons.name: Gambas3
moons.description: The third major version of Gambas, introducing significant improvements and new features.

curious_fact: Gambas was originally developed by Benoît Minisini, who continues to lead the project.

name: Gambit
description: Gambit is a Scheme implementation that compiles Scheme code to C, aiming for high performance and portability. It is known for its efficiency and has been used in various applications requiring Scheme's functional programming capabilities.
connectionsExplanation: Gambit is a direct descendant of the Scheme programming language, which was developed in the late 1970s. It inherits the functional programming paradigm and Lisp-like syntax from its parent, making it a part of the Lisp/Scheme family tree.
curious_fact: Gambit is one of the oldest and most stable Scheme implementations, known for its ability to compile to C, which allows it to run on a wide range of platforms.

name: GameMaker Language
description: GameMaker Language (GML) is a scripting language used in the GameMaker Studio software for game development. It is designed to be accessible and easy to learn, making it popular among beginners and hobbyists.
connectionsExplanation: GameMaker Language was developed by YoYo Games, which was founded in 2000. GML is specifically designed for game development and is closely integrated with the GameMaker Studio environment, allowing users to create games without needing extensive programming knowledge.
curious_fact: GML is known for its simplicity and ease of use, which has contributed to its popularity among game developers who prefer a more visual and intuitive approach to programming.

name: GameOrientedAssemblyLisp
description: GameOrientedAssemblyLisp is a fictional programming language designed specifically for game development. It combines the low-level control of assembly with the expressive power of Lisp, aiming to provide developers with a unique toolset for crafting high-performance games.
connectionsExplanation: GameOrientedAssemblyLisp was inspired by the need for a language that could leverage both the efficiency of assembly and the flexibility of Lisp. It draws from the paradigms of both languages, aiming to bridge the gap between high-level scripting and low-level hardware interaction in the gaming industry.
curious_fact: GameOrientedAssemblyLisp was created by a team of game developers who wanted to streamline the process of writing efficient and expressive game code.

name: GameMonkeyScript
description: GameMonkeyScript is a lightweight, high-performance scripting language designed for game development. It is known for its speed and ease of integration with C++ applications.
connectionsExplanation: GameMonkeyScript was developed by GarageGames, the creators of the Torque Game Engine. It was designed to be a fast and efficient scripting language for game development, allowing for real-time interaction and dynamic gameplay. Its integration with C++ made it a popular choice for game developers looking to enhance their game's scripting capabilities.
curious_fact: GameMonkeyScript was one of the first scripting languages to be integrated directly into a game engine, allowing for real-time scripting and dynamic gameplay.

name: Gauche
description: Gauche is a Scheme implementation that emphasizes portability and ease of use. It is designed to be a practical tool for both scripting and application development.
connectionsExplanation: Gauche is a Scheme implementation that evolved from the need for a more modern and portable version of Scheme. It draws inspiration from other Scheme implementations like Racket and Chicken Scheme, but focuses on providing a lightweight and efficient environment.
curious_fact: Gauche supports multiple threads and is designed to be embeddable in other applications.

name: GeneXus
description: GeneXus is a high-level, integrated development environment (IDE) for building applications. It supports multiple platforms, including web, mobile, and desktop, and is known for its rapid application development capabilities.
connectionsExplanation: GeneXus was developed by GeneXus S.A., an Argentine company. It is not directly derived from any traditional programming language but is built on modern software development principles and technologies. GeneXus is designed to simplify the development process, making it accessible to developers with varying levels of expertise.

moons.name: GeneXus X
moons.description: A version of GeneXus with enhanced features and improved performance.

moons.name: GeneXus Server
moons.description: A platform for deploying and managing GeneXus applications.

curious_fact: GeneXus was one of the first tools to introduce the concept of 'low-code development', allowing developers to create applications with minimal coding.

name: General Algebraic Modeling System
description: GAMS is a high-level modeling system for mathematical programming problems. It is particularly useful for linear, nonlinear, and mixed-integer programming. GAMS is widely used in industries such as energy, transportation, and finance for optimization problems.
connectionsExplanation: GAMS was developed to provide a user-friendly interface for formulating and solving mathematical programming problems. It was inspired by earlier modeling languages like AMPL and was designed to be more accessible to non-experts in mathematical programming. GAMS has been influential in the development of subsequent modeling languages and tools.
curious_fact: GAMS was one of the first systems to allow users to specify models in a high-level language, making it accessible to a broader audience.
example: Sets i /1*3/; Parameters a(i) /1 10, 2 20, 3 30/; Variables x(i); Equations sum_eq; sum_eq.. sum(i, a(i)*x(i)) =e= 60; Model m /sum_eq/; Solve m using lp minimizing sum_eq;

name: General Refine Expression Language
description: General Refine Expression Language (GREL) is a fictional programming language designed for advanced data manipulation and transformation. It is inspired by functional programming paradigms and is intended for use in data-intensive applications.
connectionsExplanation: GREL is inspired by functional programming languages like Haskell and Lisp, which emphasize immutability and higher-order functions. Its design aims to provide a powerful yet concise way to express complex data processing tasks.
curious_fact: GREL was created to address the need for a more expressive language for data refinement tasks in modern software development.

name: Generalized Automation Language
description: Generalized Automation Language (GAL) is a specialized programming language designed for automating industrial processes. It was developed in the 1970s to simplify the programming of complex machinery and production lines.
connectionsExplanation: GAL was developed as an evolution of earlier industrial control languages, aiming to provide a more generalized and user-friendly approach to automation. It was influenced by the need for more flexible and scalable solutions in manufacturing environments.
curious_fact: GAL was one of the first languages to introduce high-level abstractions for industrial automation, significantly reducing the complexity of programming for machine control.

name: Generative Modelling Language
description: Generative Modelling Language (GML) is a modern programming language designed for creating generative models and artificial intelligence applications. It focuses on ease of use and integration with existing machine learning frameworks.
connectionsExplanation: GML is inspired by Python and JavaScript, combining their simplicity and flexibility with advanced features tailored for generative modeling. It aims to provide a more intuitive and efficient way to develop AI applications.

moons.name: GML-NLP
moons.description: A natural language processing toolkit for GML.

curious_fact: GML was developed by a team of AI researchers and software engineers from leading tech companies.

name: Genie
description: Genie is a modern, high-level programming language designed to be easy to learn and use, with a syntax inspired by Vala. It is intended to be a general-purpose language suitable for a wide range of applications, from scripting to full-fledged applications.
connectionsExplanation: Genie was developed as a modern alternative to Vala, aiming to simplify the syntax while maintaining the performance and capabilities of the GObject-based framework. It is part of the GNOME project and is designed to work seamlessly with the GNOME ecosystem.
curious_fact: Genie was created to make GObject-based programming more accessible to a broader audience by simplifying the syntax.

name: Genus
description: Genus is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It is inspired by modern scripting languages but with a unique syntax and minimalistic approach.
connectionsExplanation: Genus was created as an educational tool to introduce programming concepts in a straightforward manner. While it draws inspiration from contemporary scripting languages, it does not have a direct lineage from any existing language.
curious_fact: Genus was developed by a team of educators aiming to simplify complex programming concepts for beginners.

name: GeometricDescriptionLanguage
description: GeometricDescriptionLanguage (GDL) is a specialized programming language designed for describing and manipulating geometric shapes and structures. It is particularly useful in fields such as computer-aided design (CAD) and architecture.
connectionsExplanation: GDL was developed as an extension of the Visual Programming Language (VPL) to enhance its capabilities in handling complex geometric data. It inherits the procedural paradigm from VPL, allowing for the creation of intricate designs through a series of geometric commands.
curious_fact: GDL was created to simplify the process of generating 3D models for architectural projects.

name: Gforth
description: Gforth is a highly portable, fast and efficient implementation of the Forth programming language. It is known for its speed and minimalism, making it popular among developers who require high performance and low resource usage.
connectionsExplanation: Gforth is a direct descendant of Forth, which was developed in the late 1960s. It inherits the stack-based architecture and interactive development environment of Forth, while also introducing improvements in performance and portability.
curious_fact: Gforth is written in itself, demonstrating the language's self-hosting capability.

name: Gibiane
description: Gibiane is a fictional programming language created for the purpose of being a playful and whimsical alternative to traditional programming languages. It is designed to be easy to read and write, with a syntax inspired by French culture and language.
connectionsExplanation: Gibiane was created as a lighthearted alternative to mainstream programming languages, drawing inspiration from the French language and culture. It is not derived from any existing language but rather stands as a unique creation.
curious_fact: Gibiane's syntax includes commands like 'bonjour' for 'hello' and 'merci' for 'thank you', reflecting its French-inspired design.
example:
bonjour gibiane
code commence
ecrire 'Bonjour le monde !'
code finit

name: Glass
description: Glass is a programming language designed for building web applications. It is known for its simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible to beginners. Glass compiles to JavaScript, allowing it to run in any web browser.
connectionsExplanation: Glass was created as a modern alternative to JavaScript, aiming to simplify web development. It draws inspiration from languages like Python and Ruby, focusing on readability and developer productivity.
curious_fact: Glass was one of the first languages to introduce a syntax similar to Python's, making it appealing to developers familiar with Python.

name: Gleam
description: Gleam is a statically typed, compiled language designed for building scalable web applications. It emphasizes simplicity and performance, aiming to provide a modern alternative to languages like Erlang and Elixir.
connectionsExplanation: Gleam is inspired by Erlang and Elixir, both of which are functional languages known for their performance and scalability in building distributed systems. Gleam aims to offer a more modern syntax and tooling while maintaining the strengths of its predecessors.

moons.name: Gleam JSON
moons.description: A library for parsing and generating JSON in Gleam.

curious_fact: Gleam compiles to Erlang bytecode, allowing it to run on the BEAM virtual machine.

name: GlobalArrays
description: GlobalArrays is a parallel programming language designed for high-performance computing. It is particularly suited for scientific and engineering applications that require distributed memory systems.
connectionsExplanation: GlobalArrays was developed at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign as part of the Message Passing Interface (MPI) project. It builds upon the MPI standard to provide a higher-level abstraction for parallel array operations, making it easier to write parallel programs for distributed memory systems.
curious_fact: GlobalArrays was one of the early languages to support parallel array operations, facilitating scientific computations on large-scale distributed systems.

name: Globule
description: Globule is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and visual programming concepts. It uses a unique syntax that resembles globular structures, making it visually distinct and engaging for beginners.
connectionsExplanation: Globule was created as an educational tool to introduce programming concepts in a fun and intuitive way. While it does not have a direct historical predecessor, it draws inspiration from modern educational languages like Scratch, which also focus on simplicity and visual programming.
curious_fact: Globule's syntax is based on globular shapes, reflecting its playful and educational nature.

name: Gnolang
description: Gnolang is a smart contract language designed to be a high-level alternative to Solidity, focusing on simplicity and efficiency. It is built on the Cosmos SDK and aims to provide a more developer-friendly environment for blockchain development.
connectionsExplanation: Gnolang is inspired by Go, a statically typed, compiled language known for its simplicity and efficiency. It shares some syntactical similarities and design philosophies with Go, making it a natural evolution for blockchain smart contract development.
curious_fact: Gnolang was developed to address some of the complexities and verbosity of Solidity, aiming to make blockchain development more accessible.

name: GnuCOBOL
description: GnuCOBOL is an open-source implementation of COBOL, a high-level programming language primarily used for business, finance, and administrative systems. It is a continuation of the COBOL project that began in the 1960s and is widely used in legacy systems.
connectionsExplanation: GnuCOBOL is a direct descendant of COBOL, which was developed in the 1960s. It is part of the GNU project and is designed to be compatible with existing COBOL programs, allowing for easy migration and maintenance of legacy systems.
curious_fact: GnuCOBOL supports the latest COBOL standards, including the 2002 and 2018 editions.
example:
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. HelloWorld.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
DISPLAY 'Hello, World!'.
STOP RUN.

name: Go
description: Go, also known as Golang, is an open-source programming language developed by Google. It is statically typed and compiled, known for its simplicity, efficiency, and strong support for concurrent programming.
connectionsExplanation: Go was designed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson. It draws inspiration from several languages, including C, Pascal, and Python, but also incorporates new ideas to address the shortcomings of these languages, particularly in the context of modern software development practices.

moons.name: Go Modules
moons.description: A dependency management and versioning system for Go.

moons.name: Go Routines
moons.description: A lightweight thread of execution that is managed by the Go runtime.

curious_fact: Go's mascot is an anthropomorphic gopher.

name: Go
description: Go, also known as Golang, is a statically typed, compiled language developed by Google. It is known for its simplicity, efficiency, and strong support for concurrent programming. Go is designed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson.
connectionsExplanation: Go was developed at Google and is influenced by several languages, including C, C++, and Pascal. It draws inspiration from the simplicity and efficiency of C, the safety and garbage collection of Java, and the concurrency model of Erlang. The language was designed to address the shortcomings of existing languages in terms of performance, safety, and ease of use, particularly in large-scale distributed systems.

moons.name: Go Modules
moons.description: A dependency management and versioning system for Go.

moons.name: Go Routines
moons.description: Lightweight threads managed by the Go runtime for concurrent execution.

curious_fact: Go's mascot is an anthropomorphic gopher.

name: Gobra
description: Gobra is a programming language designed to support the development of concurrent and secure software. It is a dialect of Go, extending it with formal verification capabilities.
connectionsExplanation: Gobra is a dialect of Go, which was developed at Google. It inherits the syntax and many features of Go but adds formal verification features to ensure the correctness of concurrent programs.
curious_fact: Gobra uses a combination of Go's simplicity and formal verification to ensure safe concurrent programming.

name: Gobstones
description: Gobstones is an educational programming language designed to teach basic programming concepts through the manipulation of stones on a board. It is particularly popular in Argentina for introductory computer science courses.
connectionsExplanation: Gobstones was developed at the University of Buenos Aires, Argentina, as part of an initiative to improve programming education. It is inspired by the Logo programming language, which uses turtle graphics to teach programming concepts, but instead of a turtle, Gobstones uses stones on a board to create a more engaging and tangible learning experience.
curious_fact: Gobstones was created to make programming accessible to children and beginners by using a simple, intuitive interface.

name: Gofer
description: Gofer is a purely functional programming language developed in the early 1980s at the University of St Andrews in Scotland. It was an early implementation of Haskell, aiming to provide a simple and clean syntax for functional programming.
connectionsExplanation: Gofer was developed as an early implementation of Haskell, which itself is a direct descendant of Miranda. Miranda was inspired by the lazy functional language ISWIM, developed by David Turner in the 1970s. Thus, Gofer's lineage can be traced back to ISWIM through Miranda and Haskell.
curious_fact: Gofer was one of the first languages to implement lazy evaluation in a practical way, significantly influencing the development of Haskell.

name: GolfScript
description: GolfScript is a minimalist golf-oriented programming language created by Janis Birkholz. It is designed to solve programming challenges in as few characters as possible, making it popular in code golf competitions.
connectionsExplanation: GolfScript was inspired by the Perl programming language, particularly in its use of regular expressions and text manipulation capabilities. It shares some syntax similarities with Perl, reflecting its roots in text processing tasks.
curious_fact: GolfScript is known for its ability to solve complex problems with extremely concise code, often using fewer characters than other languages.

name: Golo
description: Golo is a dynamic language for the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) designed to be simple and easy to learn. It aims to provide a balance between simplicity and expressiveness, making it suitable for scripting and small to medium-sized applications.
connectionsExplanation: Golo was created as a response to the need for a more dynamic and expressive language on the JVM. It draws inspiration from languages like Ruby and Groovy, aiming to provide a more modern and concise syntax compared to traditional Java.
curious_fact: Golo was developed by Sébastien Deleuze and other contributors as an open-source project.

name: Google Apps Script
description: Google Apps Script is a scripting language based on JavaScript that allows users to automate tasks in Google Workspace applications like Google Docs, Sheets, and Slides. It provides a simple way to add custom functionality to these applications without needing to deploy a full-fledged web application.
connectionsExplanation: Google Apps Script is based on JavaScript, which makes it a direct descendant of the ECMAScript standard. JavaScript, in turn, evolved from Netscape's LiveScript and was standardized by ECMA International in the late 1990s. Therefore, Google Apps Script's lineage can be traced back to the analytical-engine-order-code through JavaScript.
curious_fact: Google Apps Script runs on Google's servers, allowing it to access a wide range of Google services and APIs.

name: Gosu
description: Gosu is a statically typed, object-oriented programming language designed to be easy to learn and use. It compiles to Java bytecode and runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). Gosu is particularly known for its concise syntax and its ability to seamlessly integrate with Java libraries.
connectionsExplanation: Gosu was developed by Guidewire Software to address the need for a more modern and productive language for Java-based applications. It draws inspiration from Java and other languages like C# and Groovy, aiming to provide a more intuitive and expressive syntax while maintaining compatibility with the Java ecosystem.
curious_fact: Gosu was created to improve developer productivity by reducing boilerplate code and enhancing type safety.
example: class HelloWorld { function sayHello() { println("Hello, World!") } } var hello = new HelloWorld() hello.sayHello()

name: GrGen
description: GrGen is a domain-specific language and tool environment for graph transformation. It is used in software engineering for modeling and transforming graph-based structures.
connectionsExplanation: GrGen evolved from the need for more efficient graph transformation techniques in software engineering. It builds upon the foundational concepts of graph theory and transformational systems, providing a robust framework for developers to work with graph-based models.
curious_fact: GrGen supports parallel graph transformations, which can significantly speed up processing times for large graph structures.
example: rule ExampleRule { from { N1 : Node; N2 : Node; E : Edge; } to { N1 : Node; N2 : Node; E : Edge; } where { E.source == N1 && E.target == N2; } }

name: Grace
description: Grace is a programming language designed by Charles H. Moore in 1972 for the Intel 4004 microprocessor. It was one of the first high-level languages to be used for microprocessor programming and aimed to be simple and easy to learn.
connectionsExplanation: Grace was developed as a high-level language for the Intel 4004 microprocessor, making it one of the early languages to target microprocessors. It was influenced by the simplicity and readability of BASIC but was designed with the constraints of early microprocessors in mind.
curious_fact: Grace was one of the first languages to be used for programming microprocessors, predating many modern languages.

name: Grammatical Framework
description: Grammatical Framework (GF) is a declarative programming framework for multilingual grammar engineering. It allows the specification of grammars in a formal way, enabling the generation and parsing of sentences in multiple languages.
connectionsExplanation: Grammatical Framework was developed at the University of Copenhagen as part of the research in computational linguistics. It is based on the idea of using mathematical logic to define grammars, which makes it a unique tool in the field of natural language processing.
curious_fact: GF uses a typed lambda calculus to represent grammars, which allows for precise and efficient grammar engineering.
example: The GF framework is used to define grammars in a formal way, enabling the generation and parsing of sentences in multiple languages.

name: Grap
description: Grap is a programming language designed for text processing and typesetting. It was developed by Brian Kernighan and is known for its simplicity and efficiency in handling structured text. Grap is particularly useful for creating diagrams and graphs from text descriptions.
connectionsExplanation: Grap was developed by Brian Kernighan, who is also known for creating the C programming language. Grap is not directly related to any other major programming languages but shares the same lineage as C in terms of its development by Kernighan.
curious_fact: Grap was one of the first tools to allow users to create diagrams and graphs directly from text descriptions.

name: GraphicsLayoutEngine
description: GraphicsLayoutEngine is a specialized layout engine designed for creating complex graphical user interfaces. It is particularly useful in applications requiring dynamic and responsive layouts.
connectionsExplanation: GraphicsLayoutEngine was developed as a specialized tool to address the limitations of existing layout engines in handling complex graphical user interfaces. It draws inspiration from the paradigms of declarative UI design and is often used in conjunction with modern UI frameworks.

moons.name: LayoutManager
moons.description: A component of GraphicsLayoutEngine responsible for managing the positioning and sizing of UI elements.

moons.name: ConstraintEngine
moons.description: Handles the constraints and rules that define the layout behavior of UI elements.

curious_fact: GraphicsLayoutEngine was one of the first layout engines to support live previewing of layout changes during development.

name: Graphics Programming Language
description: Graphics Programming Language (GPL) is a specialized programming language designed for creating and manipulating graphical content. It was developed in the early 1980s to facilitate the creation of graphical applications and games. Despite its early development, GPL has remained a niche language, primarily used in educational settings and by hobbyists.
connectionsExplanation: Graphics Programming Language was developed in the early 1980s as a specialized tool for graphical applications. It was influenced by the need for more efficient and intuitive ways to handle graphics, which was a growing area of interest in the computer science community at the time. While it did not become widely adopted, it laid some foundational concepts that influenced later graphical programming languages.
curious_fact: GPL was one of the first languages to introduce high-level graphics functions, making it easier for programmers to create complex visual effects.

name: Grasshopper3d
description: Grasshopper3d is a visual scripting tool for Rhino3D, allowing users to create complex 3D models and designs through a graphical interface. It is widely used in architecture, engineering, and design for parametric modeling.
connectionsExplanation: Grasshopper3d was developed by Robert McNeel & Associates, the creators of Rhino3D. It is a plugin for Rhino3D and is not a standalone programming language, but it uses a scripting language called Grasshopper Visual Scripting Language (GH) which is based on C# and .NET framework.
curious_fact: Grasshopper3d was named after the grasshopper, a symbol of transformation and change, reflecting its role in transforming design concepts into 3D models.

name: Green
description: Green is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It emphasizes simplicity and readability, making it an ideal language for beginners. Despite its simplicity, Green is Turing-complete and supports a wide range of programming paradigms.
connectionsExplanation: Green was created as an educational tool to introduce programming concepts in a straightforward manner. It draws inspiration from various paradigms but is not directly derived from any existing language.
curious_fact: Green was developed by a team of educators to make programming accessible to children.

name: Gremlin
description: Gremlin is a graph traversal language and query language for Apache TinkerPop, designed to work with graph databases. It allows users to traverse, query, and manipulate graph structures efficiently.
connectionsExplanation: Gremlin is a direct descendant of the TinkerPop stack, which is a graph computing framework. It was developed to provide a powerful and flexible way to interact with graph databases, building upon the foundational concepts of graph theory and traversal algorithms.

moons.name: Gremlin-Java
moons.description: A Java-based implementation of Gremlin for use with TinkerPop-enabled graph databases.

moons.name: Gremlin-Python
moons.description: A Python-based implementation of Gremlin, allowing Python developers to interact with graph databases using Gremlin syntax.

curious_fact: Gremlin's name is inspired by the mythological creatures known for causing mischief, reflecting its role in navigating complex graph structures.

name: Guarded Horn Clauses
description: Guarded Horn Clauses (GHC) is a formal logic system used in automated theorem proving and logic programming. It extends Horn clauses with guards, allowing for more expressive and flexible rule-based systems.
connectionsExplanation: Guarded Horn Clauses evolved from classical Horn clauses, which are a foundational concept in logic programming. The introduction of guards provides a mechanism to control the applicability of clauses, enhancing the expressiveness of logic programs.
curious_fact: Guarded Horn Clauses were developed to address limitations in traditional Horn clauses by allowing conditional execution of rules.

name: Guix Workflow Language
description: Guix Workflow Language is a domain-specific language designed to simplify the creation and management of complex software workflows using the GNU Guix package manager. It aims to provide a declarative way to define and execute workflows, making it easier to manage dependencies and automate tasks.
connectionsExplanation: Guix Workflow Language is inspired by the declarative paradigm and is built on top of the GNU Guix package manager. It leverages Guix's powerful package management capabilities to define and execute workflows, making it a specialized tool for software development and automation tasks.
curious_fact: Guix Workflow Language is one of the first DSLs specifically tailored for workflow management in the GNU ecosystem.

name: Guppy
description: Guppy is a simple, educational programming language designed to introduce beginners to programming concepts. It features a straightforward syntax and is often used in schools and educational settings to teach basic programming principles.
connectionsExplanation: Guppy was developed as an educational tool and is not directly derived from any other language. Its simplicity makes it a unique entry in the programming language landscape, designed specifically for teaching purposes.
curious_fact: Guppy was one of the first languages to incorporate interactive learning features directly into its runtime environment.

name: Gödel
description: Gödel is a theoretical programming language inspired by the mathematical logic and incompleteness theorems of Kurt Gödel. It is designed to explore the limits of computation and formal systems.
connectionsExplanation: Gödel is a theoretical language that draws inspiration from the mathematical work of Kurt Gödel, particularly his incompleteness theorems. These theorems have profound implications for the foundations of mathematics and computer science, influencing the development of formal systems and the understanding of computational limits.
curious_fact: Gödel's incompleteness theorems show that no formal system can prove all true statements about itself.

name: HAL/S
description: HAL/S (Hardware Autocode Language for Systems) is a computer programming language developed by the MIT Instrumentation Laboratory in the early 1960s for the Apollo space program. It was designed to be used for writing control programs for spacecraft.
connectionsExplanation: HAL/S was developed specifically for the Apollo space program and was not intended for general use. It was influenced by earlier languages like FORTRAN and ALGOL, but was tailored to the needs of space mission control systems.
curious_fact: HAL/S was one of the first languages used in space exploration, specifically for the Apollo missions.

name: HC-BASIC
description: HC-BASIC, or Home Computer BASIC, was a variant of the BASIC programming language designed for home computers in the early 1980s. It was known for its simplicity and ease of use, making it popular among beginners and hobbyists.
connectionsExplanation: HC-BASIC evolved from the original Dartmouth BASIC, which was developed in the 1960s. It was specifically tailored to fit the hardware and software constraints of home computers of the era, such as the Commodore PET, Apple II, and Sinclair ZX Spectrum.
curious_fact: HC-BASIC was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of sprites and sound commands directly in the language syntax.

name: HPBASICforOpenVMS
description: HPBASICforOpenVMS is a dialect of BASIC designed for use on the OpenVMS operating system. It was developed by Hewlett-Packard to provide a familiar environment for users transitioning from other BASIC implementations to OpenVMS.
connectionsExplanation: HPBASICforOpenVMS evolved from the BASIC programming language, which was developed in the late 1950s and early 1960s. It is a direct descendant of the BASIC language, with specific adaptations for the OpenVMS environment, allowing it to leverage the system's capabilities while maintaining compatibility with existing BASIC programs.
curious_fact: HPBASICforOpenVMS was one of the first BASIC dialects to be specifically tailored for a mainframe operating system, demonstrating the language's adaptability and versatility.

name: HP Time-Shared BASIC
description: HP Time-Shared BASIC was an early implementation of the BASIC programming language developed by Hewlett-Packard for use in their time-sharing systems. It was one of the first BASIC dialects and played a significant role in the popularization of BASIC among scientists and engineers.
connectionsExplanation: HP Time-Shared BASIC was developed as an extension of Dartmouth BASIC, which was one of the earliest versions of BASIC. It was designed to be more efficient and suitable for time-sharing environments, reflecting the growing demand for interactive computing in the 1960s.
curious_fact: HP Time-Shared BASIC was one of the first languages to support structured programming constructs, influencing later versions of BASIC.

name: HP-GL
description: HP-GL (Hewlett-Packard Graphics Language) is a page description language developed by Hewlett-Packard in 1984. It is used for sending commands to HP plotters and other compatible devices to create vector graphics.
connectionsExplanation: HP-GL was developed by Hewlett-Packard in the 1980s to provide a standard for vector graphics plotting. It evolved from earlier HP proprietary languages and was designed to be compatible with HP's line of plotters. Its development was driven by the need for a standardized way to control HP's graphics devices, ensuring consistency and ease of use for engineers and designers.
curious_fact: HP-GL uses a set of commands that are similar to those found in BASIC, making it somewhat accessible to programmers familiar with that language.

name: HPCC
description: HPCC (High Performance Computing Cluster) is a parallel computing platform and programming environment designed to solve large-scale data processing problems. It is particularly suited for big data analytics and business intelligence tasks.
connectionsExplanation: HPCC evolved from the need for efficient data processing in the financial industry. It was developed by LexisNexis Risk Solutions to handle large volumes of data and complex queries, making it a specialized tool for big data analytics.

moons.name: ECL
moons.description: ECL (Enterprise Control Language) is the primary programming language used in HPCC Systems for data processing and analytics.

curious_fact: HPCC Systems was originally developed to process credit risk data for LexisNexis.

name: HQ9+
description: HQ9+ is a minimalist esoteric programming language created by Cliff L. Biffle in 1993. It consists of only four one-character instructions: H, Q, 9, and +. Despite its simplicity, it can perform complex tasks when combined in creative ways.
connectionsExplanation: HQ9+ is an esoteric programming language that emerged as a playful response to the minimalist spirit of other esoteric languages like Brainfuck. It demonstrates the concept of minimalism in programming by using only four commands to simulate a Turing-complete language.
curious_fact: HQ9+ is one of the few esoteric languages that can theoretically perform any computation, despite its extremely simple instruction set.

name: HQ9++
description: HQ9++ is a joke programming language created by Cliff L. Biffle in 1993. It is a parody of esoteric programming languages and is designed to be intentionally difficult to understand and implement. The language consists of only four one-character instructions.
connectionsExplanation: HQ9++ is a playful and nonsensical language that emerged as a humorous take on the complexity and obscurity of esoteric programming languages. It is not derived from any specific language but rather a satirical commentary on the genre.
curious_fact: HQ9++ is one of the few programming languages that does not have a Turing-complete instruction set.

name: Hack
description: Hack is a statically typed, compiled programming language developed by Facebook. It is designed to provide the speed of C++ with the simplicity and ease of use of PHP.
connectionsExplanation: Hack was created by Facebook to address the limitations of PHP in terms of performance and type safety. It is a direct descendant of PHP, sharing many of its syntax and features while introducing static typing and other optimizations.

moons.name: HHVM
moons.description: HHVM is the runtime environment for Hack, providing a virtual machine that executes Hack code.

curious_fact: Hack was initially developed as a secret project and was only publicly announced in 2014.

name: Haggis
description: Haggis is a fictional programming language created for comedic and educational purposes. It is designed to be as unintuitive and quirky as possible, making it a fun challenge for programmers to learn and use.
connectionsExplanation: Haggis was created as a parody of complex and obtuse programming languages, drawing inspiration from the Scottish dish of the same name. It is not derived from any existing language but serves as a humorous example of what can happen when language design prioritizes whimsy over practicality.
curious_fact: Haggis is the only language to have a mascot that resembles a Scottish sheep.

name: Halide
description: Halide is an open-source programming language and compiler designed to simplify the process of writing high-performance image processing code. It allows developers to express algorithms in a high-level language while automatically generating optimized code for a variety of hardware architectures.
connectionsExplanation: Halide was developed at MIT and is inspired by the need to improve the efficiency of image processing tasks. It builds upon the concepts of domain-specific languages and compiler optimization techniques to provide a more intuitive and efficient way to write image processing code.

moons.name: HalideIR
moons.description: An intermediate representation used by the Halide compiler.

curious_fact: Halide was created to address the complexity of writing efficient image processing code for modern hardware.

name: Hamilton C Shell
description: Hamilton C Shell is a command-line interface and scripting language designed for use with the Hamilton C Compiler. It is a specialized shell that integrates C-like syntax for scripting, making it suitable for system administration tasks on Windows environments.
connectionsExplanation: Hamilton C Shell evolved from the traditional Unix shell concepts but was specifically tailored for Windows systems, integrating C-like syntax to leverage the capabilities of the Hamilton C Compiler. This makes it a unique tool in the Windows scripting landscape, distinct from other shells like CMD or PowerShell.
curious_fact: Hamilton C Shell was one of the first shells to bring C-like syntax to Windows scripting, allowing for more complex and efficient system administration tasks.

name: Haml
description: Haml is a templating engine for HTML, designed to make writing HTML more elegant and less repetitive. It is often used in web development alongside Ruby on Rails.
connectionsExplanation: Haml was created by Hampton Catlin in 2006 as a way to simplify HTML syntax. It is inspired by Ruby's syntax and is often used in Ruby on Rails projects to streamline the process of writing HTML templates.
curious_fact: Haml stands for HTML Abstraction Markup Language.

name: Handel-C
description: Handel-C is a high-level programming language designed for the development of real-time embedded systems, particularly in the field of digital signal processing (DSP). It was developed by Acorn Computers and is known for its ability to generate efficient machine code for ARM processors.
connectionsExplanation: Handel-C evolved from the need for a more efficient and higher-level language for DSP applications on ARM processors. It was influenced by C and assembly languages, aiming to provide the ease of C with the performance of assembly.
curious_fact: Handel-C was used in the development of the BBC Microcomputer's sound chip.

name: Harbour
description: Harbour is a free, open-source, 32-bit, multi-platform database engine and compiler compatible with xHarbour. It is designed to be a modern, efficient, and easy-to-use alternative to Clipper and other xBase dialects.
connectionsExplanation: Harbour evolved from Clipper, a popular xBase database language. It was created to maintain compatibility with Clipper while adding modern features and improvements. The language is widely used in Brazil for business applications.

moons.name: HBWin
moons.description: A Windows GUI library for Harbour.

moons.name: HBQt
moons.description: A Qt-based GUI library for Harbour.

curious_fact: Harbour is known for its compatibility with Clipper, allowing developers to migrate existing applications with minimal changes.

name: Hare
description: Hare is a modern, concurrent, and safe programming language designed for systems programming. It aims to provide a balance between performance and safety, making it suitable for building reliable and efficient software.
connectionsExplanation: Hare is inspired by the Go programming language, which is known for its simplicity, efficiency, and strong support for concurrent programming. Hare draws from Go's syntax and design principles while introducing its own unique features to enhance safety and performance.
curious_fact: Hare was developed by a team of researchers at the University of California, Berkeley, aiming to address the challenges of modern systems programming.

name: Hartmannpipeline
description: Hartmannpipeline is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and ease of learning. It is inspired by modern scripting languages but with a unique syntax and structure.
connectionsExplanation: Hartmannpipeline was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts without the complexity of existing languages. It draws inspiration from Python and JavaScript, aiming to provide a gentle learning curve.
curious_fact: Hartmannpipeline was named after a fictional character from a science fiction novel, emphasizing its role in futuristic educational scenarios.

name: Haskell98features
description: Haskell98 is a standardization of the Haskell programming language, focusing on providing a robust and pure functional programming environment. It introduced several key features that have influenced modern functional programming languages.
connectionsExplanation: Haskell98 was a significant milestone in the Haskell language's evolution, standardizing features that were previously available in various Haskell implementations. This standardization helped in promoting wider adoption and interoperability among Haskell compilers.
curious_fact: Haskell98 introduced the concept of type classes, which are a powerful feature for defining generic functions.

name: Haxe
description: Haxe is a multiplatform programming language that compiles to JavaScript, C++, C#, Flash, and more. It is designed to be fast and flexible, allowing developers to target multiple platforms with a single codebase.
connectionsExplanation: Haxe was created by Nicolas Cannasse in 2006. It is inspired by ActionScript and is designed to be a successor to it, offering better performance and more features. Haxe's ability to compile to multiple platforms makes it a unique tool in the programming landscape.

moons.name: OpenFL
moons.description: A framework for creating cross-platform applications using Haxe.

moons.name: Heaps
moons.description: A high-performance 2D and 3D game engine for Haxe.

curious_fact: Haxe's name comes from the French word 'hax', meaning 'to cut', reflecting its ability to cut through development time with its multiplatform capabilities.
example: class HelloWorld { static function main() { trace('Hello, world!'); } }

name: Helium
description: Helium is a modern, high-performance programming language designed for building scalable and efficient applications. It emphasizes simplicity and performance, making it suitable for both web and system-level programming.
connectionsExplanation: Helium was developed as a successor to JavaScript, aiming to address some of its limitations while maintaining its ease of use. It draws inspiration from JavaScript's syntax and semantics but introduces optimizations and new features to enhance performance and developer productivity.

moons.name: Helium Framework
moons.description: A comprehensive framework for building web applications with Helium.

curious_fact: Helium's name is inspired by the element helium, known for its lightness and lifting properties, symbolizing the language's goal of being lightweight and efficient.

name: Hermes
description: Hermes is a modern, statically-typed programming language designed for high-performance systems programming. It emphasizes safety, concurrency, and efficiency, making it suitable for developing robust and scalable applications.
connectionsExplanation: Hermes was developed as a successor to Rust, aiming to improve upon its safety features and performance. It draws inspiration from Rust's ownership model and systems-level capabilities, while also incorporating modern language features to enhance developer productivity.

moons.name: Hermes Core
moons.description: The core library providing essential system functionalities.

moons.name: Hermes Async
moons.description: A library for asynchronous programming and concurrency.

curious_fact: Hermes was named after the Greek god of commerce, travel, and communication, reflecting its focus on efficient data handling and inter-process communication.

name: Hewlett-Packard Graphics Language
description: Hewlett-Packard Graphics Language (HPGL) is a page description language (PDL) developed by Hewlett-Packard in 1968. It is used for sending graphics commands to HP plotters and other HP printers. HPGL is a vector-based language, meaning it describes graphics using mathematical paths and curves rather than pixels.
connectionsExplanation: HPGL was developed as a proprietary language to control HP's line of plotters and printers. It was one of the first page description languages and influenced the development of other languages like PostScript and PDF.
curious_fact: HPGL was one of the first languages to use a pen-down, pen-up model for drawing.

name: HiAsm
description: HiAsm is a fictional assembly-like language designed for educational purposes. It aims to simplify the concepts of assembly language programming while maintaining the essential features of low-level programming.
connectionsExplanation: HiAsm is inspired by traditional assembly languages but is designed with a focus on simplicity and ease of learning. It does not have a direct historical predecessor but draws from the principles of assembly languages used in the 1960s and 1970s.
curious_fact: HiAsm was created to help beginners understand the basics of assembly language without the complexity of real-world assembly dialects.

name: Hierarchical Editing Language for Macromolecules
description: Hierarchical Editing Language for Macromolecules (HELm) is a specialized language designed for the representation and editing of chemical structures, particularly macromolecules. It provides a hierarchical syntax to describe complex chemical entities with precision.
connectionsExplanation: HELm evolved from earlier chemical markup languages and was developed to address the need for a more structured and hierarchical way of representing macromolecular structures. It builds upon the principles of chemical information systems and is used in bioinformatics and cheminformatics.
curious_fact: HELm uses a tree-like structure to represent chemical bonds and atoms, making it easier to handle complex macromolecular data.

name: Hierarchical Music Specification Language
description: Hierarchical Music Specification Language (HMSL) is a programming language designed for the composition and manipulation of music. It allows users to create complex musical structures through hierarchical programming, making it a powerful tool for algorithmic composition.
connectionsExplanation: HMSL was developed as an extension of the Music V language, which itself was inspired by the earlier Music I and Music II languages. It builds upon the concept of using programming to create music, offering a more structured and hierarchical approach to composition.
curious_fact: HMSL was one of the first languages to integrate music composition with programming, allowing for the creation of algorithmic music.
example: setNote(60, 4, 1); // C4 for 4 beats at velocity 1

name: HighLevelAssembly
description: HighLevelAssembly (HLA) is a high-level assembly language designed to provide a more structured and readable approach to assembly programming. It is built on top of x86 assembly and aims to bridge the gap between high-level languages and low-level assembly.
connectionsExplanation: HighLevelAssembly was developed as an extension to x86 assembly, aiming to make assembly programming more accessible by introducing higher-level constructs. It is not a direct descendant of any specific language but is inspired by the need for more structured assembly programming, particularly in educational contexts.
curious_fact: HighLevelAssembly was created by Randall Hyde to simplify assembly programming for educational purposes.

name: High-Level Shader Language
description: High-Level Shader Language (HLSL) is a high-level shading language developed by Microsoft for use with DirectX. It is designed to be used in graphics programming to create complex visual effects in games and other applications.
connectionsExplanation: HLSL was developed by Microsoft as part of the DirectX suite, which is a collection of APIs for handling tasks related to multimedia, especially game programming. It is closely related to and often used alongside C++ in game development environments.

name: High Performance Fortran
description: High Performance Fortran (HPF) is an extension of Fortran 90 designed to support parallel computing. It introduces constructs for explicit parallelism, making it easier to write efficient parallel programs.
connectionsExplanation: High Performance Fortran was developed to enhance the capabilities of Fortran 90 for parallel computing. It builds upon the structured programming and array handling features of Fortran 90, adding directives and constructs to facilitate parallel execution.
curious_fact: HPF was one of the first attempts to standardize parallel programming in Fortran, aiming to bridge the gap between high-level language features and parallel hardware.

name: Himawari

name: Hindawi Programming System
description: Hindawi Programming System is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes. It emphasizes simplicity and readability, making it an ideal choice for beginners. The language is inspired by modern scripting languages but with a unique syntax and structure.
connectionsExplanation: Hindawi Programming System was created as an educational tool to introduce programming concepts in a straightforward manner. name: Hollywood

name: HolyC
description: HolyC is a minimalist C-like programming language designed by Robert G. Brown. It aims to provide a simple and efficient way to write system-level code. Despite its simplicity, HolyC is capable of compiling to native machine code, making it suitable for performance-critical applications.
connectionsExplanation: HolyC is inspired by the C programming language, which is widely regarded as the foundation of modern system programming. It shares many syntactical similarities with C, making it easy for developers familiar with C to transition to HolyC. The language's minimalist design reflects a desire to reduce complexity while maintaining the performance and control associated with low-level programming.
curious_fact: HolyC compiles directly to machine code without an intermediate assembly step, which can lead to faster compilation times.

name: Honeywell ARGUS
description: Honeywell ARGUS was an early computer programming language developed by Honeywell in the 1960s. It was designed for use on Honeywell's computer systems and was part of the company's efforts to provide high-level programming languages for its customers. ARGUS was not widely adopted outside of Honeywell's own systems.
connectionsExplanation: Honeywell ARGUS was developed as part of Honeywell's internal efforts to provide a high-level programming language for its computer systems. It was not a direct descendant of any other language but was influenced by the general trends in programming language development during the 1960s.
curious_fact: Honeywell ARGUS was one of the early languages developed by a major computer manufacturer for its own systems.

name: Hop
description: Hop is a programming language designed for distributed computing and web services. It is built on top of the Scheme programming language and provides a framework for developing scalable web applications.
connectionsExplanation: Hop is a modern extension of Scheme, which itself is a dialect of Lisp. It inherits the functional programming paradigm and Lisp's emphasis on code as data. Hop extends these concepts to facilitate distributed computing and web service development, making it a specialized language for modern web applications.
curious_fact: Hop was developed at INRIA, the French National Institute for Research in Computer Science and Automation.

name: Hope
description: Hope is a purely functional programming language developed at Indiana University in the early 1980s. It was designed to explore the semantics of functional programming and to provide a simple, elegant syntax for expressing computations. Despite its academic origins, Hope has had a lasting influence on the development of modern functional languages.
connectionsExplanation: Hope was developed as a research project at Indiana University in the early 1980s. It was influenced by earlier functional programming languages like Lisp and ML, but aimed to simplify and refine the functional paradigm. Hope's design focused on clarity and expressiveness, making it a significant stepping stone in the evolution of functional programming languages.
curious_fact: Hope was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of 'lazy evaluation' in a purely functional context.

name: Hopscotch
description: Hopscotch is a visual programming language designed for beginners, especially children, to learn programming concepts through drag-and-drop blocks. It was developed by the MIT Media Lab and introduced in 2013.
connectionsExplanation: Hopscotch is inspired by Scratch, another visual programming language developed by the MIT Media Lab. Both languages aim to make programming accessible to beginners by using a block-based interface. However, Hopscotch focuses more on mobile app development, offering a more streamlined experience for creating games and interactive applications on smartphones and tablets.
curious_fact: Hopscotch was one of the first visual programming languages to target mobile devices, making it accessible to a wide audience of young programmers.
