name: ODD
description: ODD (Oriented to Data Description) is a programming language designed by Peter Naur in 1964. It is notable for its focus on data description and is considered one of the first high-level programming languages to be formally defined using a metalanguage.
connectionsExplanation: ODD was developed as part of the ALGOL 60 project and is considered a precursor to modern programming languages. It introduced concepts that influenced the design of later languages, particularly in the area of data description.
curious_fact: ODD was one of the first languages to use a formal syntax definition, which was a significant step in the evolution of programming languages.

name: OML
description: OML, or Object Model Language, is a specialized programming language designed for creating complex object-oriented models. It was developed in the early 2000s to address the limitations of existing object-oriented languages in handling large-scale modeling tasks.
connectionsExplanation: OML was developed as an extension of UML (Unified Modeling Language) to provide a more robust framework for object-oriented modeling. It aimed to integrate seamlessly with existing UML tools and methodologies, offering enhanced features for model management and transformation.
curious_fact: OML introduced the concept of 'model-driven development' which significantly influenced modern software engineering practices.

name: OMNIBACSymbolicAssembler
description: OMNIBACSymbolicAssembler is a fictional assembly language designed for educational purposes. It is a symbolic assembler that allows for the creation of machine code using mnemonic codes and labels, making it easier for beginners to understand the basics of assembly language programming.
connectionsExplanation: OMNIBACSymbolicAssembler is a fictional language created for educational purposes and does not have a direct historical connection to any real-world programming language. It is inspired by the concept of symbolic assemblers like the Macro Assembler (MASM) and the GNU Assembler (GAS), which are used to write low-level code for various computer architectures.
curious_fact: OMNIBACSymbolicAssembler was created to simplify the learning curve of assembly language by using symbolic mnemonics instead of binary codes.
example:
START: MOV AX, 0x1234
MOV BX, 0x5678
ADD AX, BX
HLT

name: OMeta
description: OMeta is a pattern-matching language for programming language processing. It is designed to be used for tasks such as parsing, transformation, and analysis of programming languages. OMeta is built on top of JavaScript and is inspired by the concept of pattern matching found in functional programming languages.
connectionsExplanation: OMeta was developed by Alessandro Warth, Gilad Bracha, and Alan Kay. It is inspired by the concept of pattern matching found in functional programming languages and is built on top of JavaScript. OMeta extends JavaScript with a pattern-matching syntax that allows for concise and expressive language processing tasks.
curious_fact: OMeta was created to simplify the process of writing parsers and language processors by using pattern matching.

name: ONScripter
description: ONScripter is a scripting engine for visual novels, primarily used in Japan. It allows developers to create interactive stories with images, text, and sound. Despite its popularity in Japan, it has limited international adoption.
connectionsExplanation: ONScripter was developed in Japan and is widely used for creating visual novels. It has influenced other visual novel engines and scripting languages used in the industry, particularly in Japan.
curious_fact: ONScripter was originally developed for the Windows 95 platform and has since evolved to support various operating systems.

name: OPS5
description: OPS5 is a hypothetical programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and clarity. It is inspired by early assembly languages but with a more structured syntax to ease the learning curve for beginners.
connectionsExplanation: OPS5 was conceptualized as an educational tool to introduce programming concepts without the complexity of machine-level languages. It draws inspiration from the structured programming paradigm, aiming to bridge the gap between high-level languages and assembly code.
curious_fact: OPS5 was never officially released but is used in some computer science curricula as a teaching aid.

name: ORCA/Modula-2
description: ORCA/Modula-2 is a programming language developed by Oberon Microsystems. It is an extension of Modula-2, designed to be more efficient and easier to use. ORCA/Modula-2 is known for its use in embedded systems and real-time applications.
connectionsExplanation: ORCA/Modula-2 evolved from Modula-2, which was developed by Niklaus Wirth at ETH Zurich. Modula-2 was designed to be a structured programming language that emphasized modularity and data encapsulation. ORCA/Modula-2 extends these principles by adding features such as improved memory management and better support for real-time systems.
curious_fact: ORCA/Modula-2 was used in the development of the Amiga operating system.
example: MODULE HelloWorld; IMPORT Out; PROCEDURE Main; BEGIN Out.String("Hello, World!"); Out.Ln; END Main; END HelloWorld.

name: OTX
description: OTX is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on simplicity and ease of learning. It is not intended for production use but serves as a tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: OTX was created as an educational tool and does not have a direct historical lineage. It is inspired by modern scripting languages but is designed to be more accessible for beginners.
curious_fact: OTX was developed by a team of educators to make programming more approachable for students.

name: Oak
description: Oak is a modern, statically-typed programming language designed for building scalable and efficient applications. It emphasizes performance and safety, making it suitable for systems programming and large-scale software development.
connectionsExplanation: Oak was developed by a team of researchers at Stanford University as a successor to C++. It aims to improve upon the performance and safety features of C++ while maintaining its low-level control. Oak draws inspiration from languages like Rust and Swift, incorporating features such as memory safety without a garbage collector and a more expressive type system.

moons.name: OakLib
moons.description: A standard library for Oak, providing essential utilities and abstractions.

curious_fact: Oak was designed to compile to native code, ensuring maximum performance on any platform.

name: Oaklisp
description: Oaklisp is a dialect of Lisp developed by Richard P. Gabriel. It is known for its efficiency and support for object-oriented programming. Oaklisp was designed to be a practical and efficient Lisp system, incorporating features from both Scheme and Common Lisp.
connectionsExplanation: Oaklisp evolved from the Lisp family of languages, specifically drawing inspiration from Scheme and Common Lisp. It was developed to address some of the inefficiencies found in earlier Lisp implementations while maintaining the flexibility and power of Lisp.
curious_fact: Oaklisp was one of the first Lisp dialects to support object-oriented programming, predating CLOS (Common Lisp Object System).

name: Oberon
description: Oberon is a simple, efficient, and elegant programming language developed by Niklaus Wirth and his team at ETH Zurich. It is known for its minimalistic syntax and structured approach, making it suitable for educational purposes and small-scale applications.
connectionsExplanation: Oberon was developed as a successor to Pascal, aiming to address some of the limitations of its predecessor while maintaining simplicity and clarity. It was influenced by the Modula-2 language, which itself was a descendant of Pascal.

moons.name: Oberon-2
moons.description: Oberon-2 is an extension of Oberon that adds object-oriented features, making it more versatile for larger applications.

moons.name: Component Pascal
moons.description: Component Pascal is a variant of Oberon that focuses on component-based programming, allowing for modular and reusable software development.

curious_fact: Oberon was designed to be taught in schools, and its simplicity made it an excellent choice for introducing programming concepts to beginners.
example: MODULE HelloWorld; IMPORT Out; PROCEDURE Main; BEGIN Out.String("Hello, World!"); Out.Ln END Main; END HelloWorld.

name: Oberon-2
description: Oberon-2 is a structured programming language developed by Niklaus Wirth at ETH Zurich. It is an extension of the Oberon language, adding features like type definitions and improved module system. Oberon-2 is known for its simplicity and clarity, making it a popular choice for educational purposes and small-scale applications.
connectionsExplanation: Oberon-2 evolved from Oberon, a language developed by Niklaus Wirth in the late 1980s. Oberon was designed to be a simple and efficient language for teaching programming concepts. Oberon-2 extends Oberon by introducing more advanced features such as type definitions and a more robust module system, which makes it suitable for a wider range of applications.
curious_fact: Oberon-2 was developed to be a successor to Pascal, aiming to maintain simplicity while adding modern features.
example: MODULE HelloWorld; IMPORT Out; PROCEDURE Main; BEGIN Out.String("Hello, World!"); Out.Ln END Main; END HelloWorld.

name: Obix
description: Obix is a web-based protocol and data model designed for building distributed systems. It is particularly suited for industrial automation and IoT applications. Unlike many other protocols, Obix emphasizes simplicity and ease of integration.
connectionsExplanation: Obix was developed by General Electric (GE) to address the need for a more flexible and scalable protocol for industrial applications. It builds on the principles of RESTful web services and JSON, aiming to provide a more intuitive and efficient way to interact with industrial devices and systems.
curious_fact: Obix was originally developed as part of GE's Predix platform, which was later acquired by PTC.

name: ObjVProlog
description: ObjVProlog is an object-oriented extension of Visual Prolog, designed to integrate object-oriented programming paradigms with the logic programming capabilities of Prolog. It aims to provide a more flexible and powerful environment for developing complex applications.
connectionsExplanation: ObjVProlog evolved from Visual Prolog, which itself is a descendant of Prolog. Prolog, as a pioneer in logic programming, has a rich history dating back to the 1970s. Visual Prolog, developed by PDC, introduced a more structured and object-oriented approach to Prolog, making it more accessible to developers familiar with object-oriented paradigms. ObjVProlog further extends this by enhancing the object-oriented features, aiming to bridge the gap between object-oriented and logic programming.
curious_fact: ObjVProlog was developed to leverage the strengths of both object-oriented and logic programming, making it unique in the Prolog family.
example: object HelloWorld extends object { predicate main() { console::write("Hello, World!") } }

name: ObjVlisp
description: ObjVlisp is an object-oriented extension of the LISP programming language, developed by Symbolics Inc. It was designed to support object-oriented programming paradigms within the LISP environment, offering a more structured approach to software development compared to traditional LISP.
connectionsExplanation: ObjVlisp evolved from LISP, inheriting its functional programming roots while introducing object-oriented features. This evolution was part of the broader trend in the 1980s to integrate object-oriented programming into existing languages to enhance modularity and code reusability.
curious_fact: ObjVlisp was one of the first languages to integrate object-oriented programming into LISP, paving the way for modern object-oriented extensions.

name: ObjectLisp
description: ObjectLisp is an extension of Common Lisp that integrates object-oriented programming features. It was developed to enhance Lisp's capabilities for building complex software systems with object-oriented design principles.
connectionsExplanation: ObjectLisp was created by adding object-oriented features to Common Lisp, making it a direct descendant of Common Lisp. This extension aimed to provide Lisp programmers with the benefits of object-oriented programming without abandoning the language's functional programming roots.
curious_fact: ObjectLisp was one of the early attempts to integrate object-oriented programming into Lisp, predating languages like CLOS.

name: ObjectOberon
description: ObjectOberon is an extension of the Oberon programming language that introduces object-oriented features. It was developed by Niklaus Wirth and his team at ETH Zurich. ObjectOberon is known for its simplicity and elegance, making it a popular choice for educational purposes and small-scale applications.
connectionsExplanation: ObjectOberon evolved from the original Oberon language, which was developed in the late 1980s. Oberon was itself inspired by Modula-2 and Pascal, and it introduced the concept of modules to manage program structure. ObjectOberon extends Oberon by adding object-oriented programming capabilities, making it a significant step in the evolution of modular and object-oriented languages.
curious_fact: ObjectOberon was one of the first languages to introduce object-oriented features in a simple and elegant manner, influencing later languages like Java and C++.
example: MODULE HelloWorld; IMPORT Out; PROCEDURE Main; BEGIN Out.String("Hello, World!"); Out.Ln END Main; END HelloWorld.

name: ObjectPascal
description: ObjectPascal is an extension of the Pascal programming language that adds object-oriented features. It was developed by Borland and is known for its use in the Delphi IDE. ObjectPascal is widely used in Windows application development.
connectionsExplanation: ObjectPascal evolved from Pascal, which was developed by Niklaus Wirth in the late 1960s. The introduction of object-oriented features in ObjectPascal marked a significant step in the evolution of Pascal, making it more suitable for modern software development practices.

moons.name: Delphi
moons.description: An integrated development environment (IDE) for ObjectPascal, developed by Borland.

curious_fact: ObjectPascal was one of the first languages to introduce object-oriented programming concepts to a wide audience.

name: ObjectREXX
description: ObjectREXX is an extension of the REXX programming language that adds object-oriented features. It was developed by IBM to enhance the capabilities of REXX for building complex applications.
connectionsExplanation: ObjectREXX evolved from REXX, which was originally developed by Mike Cowlishaw at IBM in the 1970s. The addition of object-oriented features in ObjectREXX was a significant step towards making REXX more versatile for enterprise-level applications.
curious_fact: ObjectREXX was one of the first languages to introduce object-oriented programming concepts to the REXX family.

name: Object-Oriented Turing
description: Object-Oriented Turing is a hypothetical programming language designed to explore the intersection of object-oriented principles with the foundational concepts of Alan Turing's theoretical computing model. It aims to provide a modern, object-oriented approach to problem-solving while maintaining the computational completeness of Turing machines.
connectionsExplanation: Object-Oriented Turing is inspired by the paradigm shift introduced by object-oriented programming languages like Java and C++. It seeks to integrate the structured and modular design principles of these languages with the universal computation capabilities of Turing machines, offering a unique perspective on software architecture and computation.
curious_fact: Object-Oriented Turing is a thought experiment that challenges traditional programming paradigms by merging object-oriented design with the theoretical underpinnings of Turing completeness.

name: Object-PL/SQL
description: Object-PL/SQL is an extension of Oracle Corporation's PL/SQL procedural language that adds object-oriented features to the Oracle relational database environment. It allows for the creation of object types, methods, and collections, enhancing the capabilities for database application development.
connectionsExplanation: Object-PL/SQL evolved from PL/SQL, which was introduced in Oracle 7.3 in 1992. It was designed to integrate object-oriented programming concepts into the Oracle database, allowing developers to create more complex and modular database applications.
curious_fact: Object-PL/SQL was one of the first extensions to add object-oriented features to a relational database language.
example: CREATE OR REPLACE TYPE employee_type AS OBJECT (name VARCHAR2(50), salary NUMBER, MEMBER FUNCTION get_bonus RETURN NUMBER);

name: ObjectPAL
description: ObjectPAL is a programming language developed by Borland for use in their database management systems, particularly in dBASE and Clipper. It is an object-oriented extension of dBASE and was designed to simplify database application development.
connectionsExplanation: ObjectPAL evolved from dBASE, which was one of the pioneering database management systems in the 1980s. It introduced object-oriented features to dBASE, making it a direct descendant of the dBASE language family.
curious_fact: ObjectPAL was one of the first languages to bring object-oriented programming to the dBASE environment, significantly enhancing its capabilities.
example:
OBJECT Customer
  PRIVATE name AS STRING
  PRIVATE age AS INTEGER

  METHOD Initialize(name AS STRING, age AS INTEGER)
    SELF.name = name
    SELF.age = age
  END METHOD

  METHOD DisplayName()
    ? SELF.name
  END METHOD
END OBJECT

VAR cust AS Customer
SET cust = NEW Customer("John Doe", 30)
cust.DisplayName()

name: ObjectStar
description: ObjectStar is a modern, object-oriented programming language designed for high-performance applications. It emphasizes simplicity and efficiency, making it suitable for both beginners and experienced developers. ObjectStar is particularly well-suited for systems programming and real-time applications.
connectionsExplanation: ObjectStar was developed as a successor to the popular ObjectLang, aiming to improve performance and add modern features. It inherits the object-oriented paradigm from ObjectLang, which itself was inspired by Smalltalk.

moons.name: ObjectLib
moons.description: A standard library for ObjectStar, providing essential utilities and data structures.

moons.name: ObjectNet
moons.description: A networking framework for building distributed applications in ObjectStar.

curious_fact: ObjectStar was developed in just 6 months by a team of 10 developers.

name: Objective-C
description: Objective-C is a superset of the C programming language and adds Smalltalk-style messaging to C. It was originally developed by NeXT and is now primarily used for macOS, iOS, and watchOS app development.
connectionsExplanation: Objective-C was developed by NeXT in the 1980s and is a direct descendant of C, with Smalltalk-style messaging added. It served as the primary language for NeXTSTEP, which later became the foundation for macOS. Objective-C's influence can be seen in Swift, which was designed to be more modern and safe while maintaining compatibility with Objective-C.

moons.name: Cocoa
moons.description: Cocoa is a framework for macOS and iOS development that uses Objective-C.

moons.name: Swift
moons.description: Swift is a modern, high-performance system programming language developed by Apple that builds on the Objective-C runtime.

curious_fact: Objective-C was one of the first languages to popularize the use of dynamic typing and runtime binding in a statically typed language.

name: Objective-J
description: Objective-J is a prototype-based object-oriented programming language that runs on top of JavaScript. It was designed to provide a more object-oriented syntax and semantics similar to Objective-C, while still being compatible with JavaScript.
connectionsExplanation: Objective-J was created by John Maloney and others at Apple as part of the WebObjects framework. It was intended to provide a more object-oriented approach to web development, leveraging the existing JavaScript ecosystem. Objective-J introduced features like prototypes and messaging, inspired by Objective-C, to JavaScript.
curious_fact: Objective-J was one of the first attempts to bring Objective-C-like syntax to JavaScript, predating many modern JavaScript frameworks.

name: Objlog
description: Objlog is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, focusing on object-oriented programming principles. It was created to demonstrate the basics of object-oriented concepts in a simplified manner.
connectionsExplanation: Objlog was inspired by Smalltalk, one of the first object-oriented programming languages. It shares similar syntax and concepts but is designed to be more accessible for beginners.
curious_fact: Objlog was created to teach object-oriented programming concepts in a simplified manner.

name: Obliq
description: Obliq is a reflective, object-oriented programming language designed for distributed computing. It was developed at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology (ETH Zurich) and is known for its simplicity and flexibility in handling distributed systems.
connectionsExplanation: Obliq was developed as part of the Obliq project at ETH Zurich, which aimed to create a language suitable for distributed computing environments. It draws inspiration from Smalltalk and other object-oriented languages but focuses on the unique challenges of distributed systems.
curious_fact: Obliq was one of the first languages to support distributed object-oriented programming, allowing objects to be located on different machines.
example: Object hello { method say() { print("Hello, World!") } } hello.say()

name: Octave
description: GNU Octave is a high-level language primarily intended for numerical computations. It provides capabilities for solving linear and nonlinear problems, performing matrix manipulations, and conducting numerical experiments.
connectionsExplanation: GNU Octave was designed to be largely compatible with MATLAB, making it a popular choice for those transitioning from MATLAB to an open-source alternative. Its development began as a companion to MATLAB, aiming to provide a free software environment for numerical computations.

moons.name: Octave Forge
moons.description: A collection of packages providing additional functionality to GNU Octave.

curious_fact: GNU Octave was named after the mathematician and inventor of the first computer, Charles Babbage.

name: Odin
description: Odin is a statically typed, compiled programming language designed for performance and simplicity. It is inspired by C and Go, aiming to provide a modern alternative with a focus on safety and efficiency.
connectionsExplanation: Odin was created by Jordan Rieger as a response to the perceived shortcomings of existing systems programming languages. It draws inspiration from C and Go, incorporating features from both while aiming to improve upon them in terms of safety and performance.
curious_fact: Odin's syntax is designed to be minimalistic, with a focus on readability and simplicity.

name: OmniMark
description: OmniMark is a programming language designed for text processing and markup transformation. It is particularly suited for tasks involving SGML, XML, and HTML. OmniMark is known for its ability to handle complex text processing tasks efficiently.
connectionsExplanation: OmniMark was developed by Software AG in 1991. It is based on the paradigm of stream-oriented programming, which is a direct evolution from the procedural programming concepts found in languages like C and Pascal. OmniMark's design emphasizes the processing of text streams, making it a specialized tool for markup languages.
curious_fact: OmniMark was one of the first languages to support XML natively, predating many modern XML processing tools.

name: Ooc
description: Ooc is a statically typed, compiled systems programming language designed to be a modern alternative to C. It aims to provide a more concise and safer syntax while maintaining performance.
connectionsExplanation: Ooc was inspired by the desire to create a language that combines the performance of C with modern features and safety improvements. It draws influences from languages like C and Java, aiming to offer a more intuitive syntax and better memory management.
curious_fact: Ooc was developed by a single individual, making it a unique example of a solo-created programming language.
example: class HelloWorld { static main() { println("Hello, World!") } }

name: Oolong
description: Oolong is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It is inspired by the Chinese Oolong tea, known for its unique processing method that results in a balanced flavor. Oolong is intended to introduce beginners to programming concepts without overwhelming them with complex syntax.
connectionsExplanation: Oolong was created as an educational tool, drawing inspiration from the process of making Oolong tea. It is not directly derived from any existing programming language but shares some conceptual similarities with Python in terms of syntax and ease of use.
curious_fact: Oolong's name is inspired by the Chinese Oolong tea, known for its unique processing method that results in a balanced flavor.

name: Opa
description: Opa is a programming language designed for building web applications. It compiles to JavaScript and runs in the browser, allowing for full-stack development in a single language. Opa emphasizes security and simplicity, aiming to reduce the complexity of web development.
connectionsExplanation: Opa was developed by MLstate, a French company, and is influenced by functional programming paradigms. It is not directly derived from any specific language but draws inspiration from languages like Haskell and Erlang, particularly in its approach to concurrency and security.

moons.name: OpaJS
moons.description: A JavaScript runtime for Opa applications.

curious_fact: Opa's compiler generates JavaScript that is both secure and efficient, aiming to eliminate common web vulnerabilities.

name: Opal
description: Opal is a Ruby-like language that compiles to JavaScript. It allows developers to write Ruby code that runs in web browsers and Node.js environments.
connectionsExplanation: Opal was created to bring the elegance and simplicity of Ruby to the JavaScript ecosystem. It compiles Ruby code into JavaScript, enabling developers to use Ruby's syntax and features in web development.

moons.name: opal-browser
moons.description: A library for building web applications with Opal.

moons.name: opal-rails
moons.description: Integration of Opal with the Ruby on Rails framework.

curious_fact: Opal is named after the precious gemstone, reflecting its valuable role in bridging Ruby and JavaScript.

name: OpenProgrammingLanguage
description: OpenProgrammingLanguage (OPL) is a modern, open-source programming language designed for flexibility and ease of use. It aims to provide a versatile environment for developers to create a wide range of applications. OPL is particularly noted for its innovative syntax and support for multiple programming paradigms.
connectionsExplanation: OpenProgrammingLanguage was developed as a successor to several popular languages, aiming to incorporate the best features from languages like Python, JavaScript, and Rust. Its design philosophy emphasizes simplicity and modularity, making it a promising choice for both beginners and experienced developers.

moons.name: Libraries
moons.description: A collection of reusable modules and functions for various programming tasks.

moons.name: Frameworks
moons.description: Structures for building web applications, data analysis tools, and more.

curious_fact: OPL was created by a community of developers who wanted a language that could adapt to future technological advancements.

name: OpenRoberta
description: OpenRoberta is an open-source platform for teaching programming to children using robots. It provides a visual programming environment and supports various robotic platforms, making it accessible for beginners.
connectionsExplanation: OpenRoberta was developed by the Fraunhofer Society in Germany and is inspired by the Blockly programming language, which is part of the Google Blockly project. It aims to democratize programming education by providing a user-friendly interface for learning through robotics.
curious_fact: OpenRoberta was created to encourage children's interest in programming and robotics, offering a gamified learning experience.

name: Open Shading Language
description: Open Shading Language (OSL) is a shading language designed for use in rendering systems. It allows artists and developers to write custom shaders that can be used to control the appearance of surfaces in 3D graphics. OSL is particularly popular in the film and animation industry for its flexibility and performance.
connectionsExplanation: Open Shading Language was developed by Sony Pictures Imageworks and is based on the shading language used in their internal rendering pipeline. It is designed to be a high-level language that can be compiled to run on various rendering backends, making it a versatile tool for shading in 3D graphics.
curious_fact: OSL was originally developed to address the need for more flexible and efficient shading in the production of the film 'Avatar'.
example: shader simple_diffuse(color baseColor = color(0.8)) { Ci = baseColor; }

name: OpenCL
description: OpenCL (Open Computing Language) is a framework for writing programs that execute across heterogeneous platforms consisting of CPUs, GPUs, and other processors or hardware accelerators. It is designed to enable developers to harness the parallel compute power of these devices.
connectionsExplanation: OpenCL was developed by the Khronos Group as a standard for parallel computing on heterogeneous systems. It builds upon earlier work in parallel computing languages and APIs, aiming to provide a unified programming model for diverse hardware architectures.
curious_fact: OpenCL was first introduced in 2009 and has since become a widely adopted standard for parallel computing across different hardware platforms.

name: OpenEdge Advanced Business Language
description: OpenEdge Advanced Business Language (ABL) is a high-level programming language developed by Progress Software Corporation. It is primarily used for building business applications, particularly in the financial and healthcare sectors. ABL is known for its ease of use and integration with Progress OpenEdge, a comprehensive application development platform.
connectionsExplanation: OpenEdge ABL evolved from the Progress 4GL (Fourth Generation Language) which was introduced in the 1980s. It is designed to simplify the development of business applications by providing a structured, English-like syntax. The language is closely tied to the Progress OpenEdge platform, which offers a range of tools and services for application development, deployment, and management.

moons.name: ABL Studio
moons.description: An integrated development environment (IDE) for writing, debugging, and deploying ABL applications.

moons.name: ABL Compiler
moons.description: Compiles ABL source code into executable programs for deployment.

curious_fact: ABL was originally developed to create a more accessible programming language for business users, reducing the need for extensive technical expertise.

name: OpenGL Shading Language
description: OpenGL Shading Language (GLSL) is a high-level shading language based on the C programming language. It is used for writing shaders in the OpenGL graphics API, allowing developers to create complex visual effects and graphics.
connectionsExplanation: GLSL was developed by the Khronos Group as an extension to the OpenGL API. It is designed to be a C-like language, making it familiar to C and C++ programmers. GLSL shaders are written in source code and compiled at runtime by the OpenGL driver.
curious_fact: GLSL introduced the concept of 'uniforms' and 'varyings', which are essential for passing data between different stages of the graphics pipeline.

name: OpenGL Shading Language
description: OpenGL Shading Language (GLSL) is a high-level shading language based on the C programming language. It is used for writing shaders in the OpenGL graphics API, allowing developers to control the rendering pipeline for real-time graphics.
connectionsExplanation: GLSL was developed by the Khronos Group as part of the OpenGL specification. It is designed to be a C-like language to facilitate the transition for C/C++ programmers. GLSL is essential for modern graphics programming, enabling complex visual effects and real-time rendering.
curious_fact: GLSL introduced the concept of 'uniforms' and 'varyings' to manage data flow between shader stages.

name: OpenLisp
description: OpenLisp is a dialect of the Lisp programming language, developed in the 1980s. It is known for its extensibility and support for object-oriented programming. OpenLisp was designed to be a more accessible and user-friendly version of Lisp, aiming to bridge the gap between Lisp and other programming paradigms.
connectionsExplanation: OpenLisp evolved from Common Lisp, which itself is a descendant of the original Lisp language. The development of OpenLisp was influenced by the need for a more modern and accessible Lisp dialect, reflecting the changing needs of the programming community in the 1980s.
curious_fact: OpenLisp introduced a unique feature called 'dynamic typing with static type checking', which aimed to improve performance and safety.

name: OpenMusic
description: OpenMusic is a visual programming environment for music composition and sound synthesis. It is designed to be user-friendly and flexible, allowing users to create complex musical structures through a graphical interface.
connectionsExplanation: OpenMusic was developed at the IRCAM (Institut de Recherche et Coordination Acoustique/Musique) in Paris, France. It is inspired by the concept of visual programming and is part of the Max/MSP ecosystem, which emphasizes graphical programming for multimedia applications.
curious_fact: OpenMusic uses a unique visual language to represent musical processes, making it accessible to both musicians and non-programmers.
example: OpenMusic uses a visual interface for programming, so there is no traditional 'Hello World' example. Instead, users create patches to generate sound.

name: OpenMx
description: OpenMx is an open-source, full-featured structural equation modeling (SEM) package for R. It provides a flexible and powerful framework for fitting a wide range of SEM models.
connectionsExplanation: OpenMx is built on top of the R programming language, which is widely used in statistical computing and data analysis. It leverages R's extensive ecosystem and capabilities to provide advanced SEM functionalities.
curious_fact: OpenMx was developed at the Pennsylvania State University and is named after the Open Matrix project.
example:
library(OpenMx)
model <- mxModel("OneFactor",
  mxMatrix(type="Full", nrow=1, ncol=5, free=TRUE, values=1, name="A"),
  mxMatrix(type="Full", nrow=5, ncol=1, free=TRUE, values=1, name="L"),
  mxMatrix(type="Diag", nrow=5, ncol=5, free=TRUE, values=1, name="U"),
  mxMatrix(type="Diag", nrow=1, ncol=1, free=TRUE, values=1, name="x"),
  mxAlgebra(A %*% L, name="S"),
  mxData(cov(data), type="cov", numObs=100),
  mxFitFunctionML(),
  mxExpectationNormal(covariance="S", means="x"))
fit <- mxRun(model)

name: OpenQASM
description: OpenQASM (Open Quantum Assembly Language) is an open-source assembly language for programming quantum computers. It provides a low-level interface for specifying quantum operations and is designed to be hardware-agnostic, allowing quantum algorithms to be written in a way that can be executed on various quantum processors.
connectionsExplanation: OpenQASM was developed by IBM as part of their efforts to standardize quantum computing languages. It builds upon the principles of classical assembly languages but is tailored to the unique requirements of quantum computing, such as qubit manipulation and entanglement. Its development was influenced by the need for a language that could be easily translated into the specific gate sets of different quantum hardware.
curious_fact: OpenQASM is one of the first languages designed specifically for programming quantum computers, reflecting the rapid advancement in quantum technology.
example: OPENQASM 2.0; qreg q[2]; creg c[2]; H q[0]; CX q[0],q[1]; measure q -> c;

name: OpenROAD
description: OpenROAD is an open-source, high-performance physical design tool for integrated circuits. It is designed to automate the layout and routing of complex chips, making it essential for modern semiconductor manufacturing.
connectionsExplanation: OpenROAD is part of the OpenROAD project, which aims to provide a comprehensive, open-source toolset for physical design. It builds upon the principles of previous proprietary tools and integrates various open-source components to offer a robust solution for chip design.
curious_fact: OpenROAD was developed to address the need for open-source tools in the semiconductor industry, aiming to reduce dependency on proprietary software.

name: OpenModelica
description: OpenModelica is an open-source Modelica-based modeling and simulation environment. It supports a wide range of modeling languages and is used in academia and industry for system modeling and simulation.
connectionsExplanation: OpenModelica is a direct descendant of Modelica, which was developed in the 1990s. It extends the capabilities of Modelica by providing a more open and extensible platform, allowing for easier integration with other tools and languages.

moons.name: OMEdit
moons.description: A graphical user interface for OpenModelica.

moons.name: OMSimulator
moons.description: A high-performance simulation engine for OpenModelica.

curious_fact: OpenModelica supports parallel simulation, which can significantly speed up the simulation of large models.

name: OptimJ
description: OptimJ is a Java-based programming language designed for real-time systems and embedded applications. It extends Java with features for real-time programming, such as deterministic scheduling and bounded execution time.
connectionsExplanation: OptimJ evolved from Java, specifically targeting real-time systems where predictable execution behavior is crucial. It builds upon Java's robust ecosystem while adding specialized constructs for real-time programming.
curious_fact: OptimJ was developed by Real-Time Innovations (RTI) to address the need for deterministic behavior in Java applications.
example: public class HelloWorld { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hello, World!"); } }

name: OptimizationProgrammingLanguage
description: OptimizationProgrammingLanguage (OPL) is a specialized programming language designed to optimize performance-critical applications. It focuses on minimizing resource usage and maximizing execution speed, making it ideal for high-performance computing environments.
connectionsExplanation: OPL was developed as an evolution of modern high-performance languages like C++ and Rust, incorporating advanced optimization techniques and paradigms to address the growing demand for efficient code in data-intensive applications.
curious_fact: OPL introduced a novel Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation technique that significantly reduces runtime overhead.

name: Orc
description: Orc is a programming language designed for parallel and distributed computing. It is known for its simplicity and efficiency in handling large-scale data processing tasks.
connectionsExplanation: Orc was developed at the University of California, Berkeley, as part of the Berkeley Data Systems Lab. It is inspired by functional programming paradigms and is designed to be highly parallel, making it suitable for distributed computing environments.
curious_fact: Orc's name is derived from the orc-like creatures in fantasy literature, symbolizing its strength and efficiency in handling complex tasks.

name: Orca
description: Orca is a modern, high-level programming language designed for cloud-native applications. It emphasizes simplicity and performance, making it suitable for developing scalable and efficient software in distributed environments.
connectionsExplanation: Orca was developed as a successor to the popular language 'Kotlin', aiming to address some of the limitations and complexities found in its predecessor. It inherits Kotlin's focus on interoperability with Java and its strong type system, while introducing new features to enhance developer productivity and application performance.

moons.name: Orca-Web
moons.description: A framework for building web applications in Orca.

moons.name: Orca-Data
moons.description: A library for handling data processing and storage in Orca.

curious_fact: Orca was named after the orca whale, symbolizing its strength and adaptability in the tech ecosystem.

name: Oriel
description: Oriel is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and readability. It is inspired by modern scripting languages but with a unique syntax that aims to make programming accessible to beginners.
connectionsExplanation: Oriel was created as an educational tool, drawing inspiration from popular scripting languages like Python and JavaScript. Its design philosophy focuses on simplicity and ease of learning, making it a unique addition to the programming landscape.
curious_fact: Oriel's syntax is designed to be intuitive, with a focus on natural language constructs.

name: Orwell
description: Orwell is a fictional programming language created by the author George Orwell in his dystopian novel '1984'. It is designed to be a totalitarian language, reflecting the oppressive nature of the regime in the novel. The language is highly controlled and limited in its expressive power, embodying the themes of surveillance and control.
connectionsExplanation: Orwell is a fictional language inspired by the themes of George Orwell's '1984'. It is not based on any real programming language but rather a conceptual creation reflecting the novel's dystopian society. The language's design is intended to symbolize the oppressive control and limited freedom of expression in the novel.
curious_fact: Orwell is a fictional language created to reflect the themes of George Orwell's '1984'.

name: OtusLisp
description: OtusLisp is a modern Common Lisp implementation designed for educational purposes and performance. It is written in C and aims to provide a clear and efficient way to learn Lisp.
connectionsExplanation: OtusLisp is a modern implementation of Common Lisp, which evolved from the original Lisp language. Common Lisp, in turn, is a direct descendant of Lisp, one of the earliest programming languages, developed in the late 1950s. Thus, OtusLisp has a lineage that traces back to the pioneering work of John McCarthy and his team at MIT.
curious_fact: OtusLisp is known for its minimalistic design, which makes it easier to understand and modify.

name: OverpassQL
description: OverpassQL is a query language for the OpenStreetMap database, designed to efficiently retrieve and analyze geospatial data. It is part of the Overpass API, which allows users to query the OpenStreetMap database in a structured way.
connectionsExplanation: OverpassQL is derived from the need to query and analyze the vast amounts of data in the OpenStreetMap database. It builds upon the capabilities of SQL, adapting it to the specific requirements of geospatial data retrieval.
curious_fact: OverpassQL is named after the Overpass API, which it powers, and it is specifically designed to handle the complex queries needed for OpenStreetMap data.

name: OverpassXML
description: OverpassXML is a query language used to interact with the OpenStreetMap database. It is designed to extract specific data from the map, such as roads, buildings, and other geographic features. While primarily used in conjunction with the Overpass API, it can also be used independently to query and manipulate OpenStreetMap data.
connectionsExplanation: OverpassXML is a specialized language designed to interact with the OpenStreetMap database. It evolved from the need to efficiently query and extract specific geographic data from the vast dataset of OpenStreetMap. While not directly derived from traditional programming languages, it shares some similarities with XML-based query languages and is influenced by the data structure and querying paradigms of OpenStreetMap.
curious_fact: OverpassXML queries can be quite complex, allowing for detailed and specific data extraction from OpenStreetMap.

name: Ox
description: Ox is a modern, statically-typed programming language designed for high-performance computing and systems programming. It emphasizes safety, efficiency, and simplicity, making it suitable for developing robust and scalable applications.
connectionsExplanation: Ox was developed as a successor to C++, aiming to address some of the language's limitations while retaining its performance characteristics. It draws inspiration from modern paradigms and incorporates features from languages like Rust and Go to enhance safety and concurrency.

moons.name: Ox Lib
moons.description: A standard library for Ox, providing essential utilities and abstractions for common programming tasks.

curious_fact: Ox was created by a team of former C++ developers who sought to improve upon the language's design without sacrificing its performance.

name: Oxygene
description: Oxygene is a modern, object-oriented programming language developed by RemObjects Software. It is designed to be easy to read and write, with a syntax that is similar to Pascal and Object Pascal. Oxygene is used primarily for developing applications for Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android.
connectionsExplanation: Oxygene is a descendant of Object Pascal, which itself evolved from Pascal. It shares many syntactical similarities with Pascal, making it easier for developers familiar with Pascal to transition to Oxygene. The language is designed to be cross-platform, allowing developers to write code that can run on multiple operating systems.

moons.name: RemObjects Elements
moons.description: A framework for building cross-platform applications using Oxygene.

curious_fact: Oxygene was named after the oxygen molecule, symbolizing its ability to 'oxygenate' or improve the development experience.

name: Oz
description: Oz is a declarative, logic-based programming language developed at the University of New South Wales in Australia. It is known for its unique approach to concurrency and constraint programming.
connectionsExplanation: Oz is a descendant of Prolog, inheriting its logic programming paradigm. It was designed to address the limitations of Prolog in handling concurrency and constraint solving, making it a significant advancement in the field of declarative programming.

moons.name: Mozart
moons.description: Mozart is an implementation of the Oz programming language, known for its efficiency and support for distributed computing.

curious_fact: Oz was named after the character from the fairy tale 'The Emperor's New Clothes' to symbolize its innovative approach to programming.

name: P
description: P is a programming language developed by John McCarthy and his team at Stanford University in the early 1970s. It was designed as a teaching language for introductory programming courses and is known for its simplicity and clarity. P is not widely used today but has influenced the development of other languages.
connectionsExplanation: P was developed as a teaching language at Stanford University in the early 1970s. It was influenced by earlier Lisp dialects and was designed to be simple and easy to understand, making it suitable for introductory programming courses. While P itself is not widely used, its principles and design have contributed to the evolution of modern programming languages.
curious_fact: P was one of the first programming languages to introduce the concept of structured programming.

name: P#
description: P# is a domain-specific programming language designed for creating games and simulations in the Unity game engine. It is a variant of C# tailored to simplify game development.
connectionsExplanation: P# is a specialized language that evolved from C# to cater to the specific needs of game development within the Unity engine. It inherits the syntax and many features of C# but adds syntactic sugar and abstractions to streamline game programming tasks.
curious_fact: P# was created to make game development more accessible to beginners by reducing boilerplate code.

name: P4
description: P4 is a domain-specific programming language (DSL) designed for programming packet processors in network switches and routers. It is widely used in the development of high-performance network devices.
connectionsExplanation: P4 was developed by the P4.org consortium, which includes major technology companies like Google, Microsoft, and Intel. It is designed to address the complexity and variability of modern network hardware, making it a crucial tool in the field of network programming.

moons.name: P4Runtime
moons.description: A protocol for controlling and managing P4 programs.

moons.name: BMv2
moons.description: A behavioral model for P4 programs, used for simulation and testing.

curious_fact: P4 was named after the P4 programming language, which was used in the early days of computer graphics.
example: control MyIngress(inout headers hdr, inout metadata meta) { apply { hdr.ipv4.ttl = hdr.ipv4.ttl - 1; } }

name: PACT
description: PACT is a domain-specific language (DSL) designed for blockchain smart contracts, focusing on security, efficiency, and ease of use. It is particularly suited for financial applications and aims to provide a more intuitive and secure way to write smart contracts compared to general-purpose languages.
connectionsExplanation: PACT was developed by Pact Foundation, which is a spin-off from R3, a global blockchain technology company. It is inspired by Haskell's functional programming paradigm, aiming to bring the benefits of functional programming to blockchain smart contract development.

moons.name: Pact Safe Language
moons.description: A subset of PACT designed for safer smart contract development.

moons.name: Pact Ledger API
moons.description: APIs for interacting with blockchain ledgers using PACT.

curious_fact: PACT uses a unique approach to smart contract verification, incorporating formal verification techniques to ensure contract correctness.

name: PAL
description: PAL (Programming Abstraction Language) is a procedural programming language developed in the early 1980s. It was designed to simplify the process of programming for personal computers and was notable for its user-friendly syntax and ease of learning. PAL was particularly popular in educational settings due to its simplicity and the availability of teaching materials.
connectionsExplanation: PAL was developed as a response to the growing demand for programming languages that were accessible to beginners. It was influenced by the procedural paradigm and aimed to provide a straightforward syntax that could be easily understood by those new to programming. PAL's design was inspired by the BASIC language, which was widely used in educational environments at the time.
curious_fact: PAL was one of the first languages to introduce a graphical user interface for programming, making it more accessible to users without a strong background in computer science.

name: PANORAMIC
description: PANORAMIC is a lesser-known programming language developed in the 1970s. It was designed for educational purposes and to explore alternative programming paradigms. Despite its innovative approach, it never gained widespread adoption.
connectionsExplanation: PANORAMIC was developed during a period of exploration in programming paradigms. It was influenced by the need for more intuitive and accessible programming languages for educational use. While it did not directly evolve from any specific language, it shares conceptual similarities with early educational languages like BASIC.
curious_fact: PANORAMIC was one of the first languages to incorporate visual programming elements into its syntax.

name: PARI/GP
description: PARI/GP is a computer algebra system designed for fast computations in number theory. It is particularly well-suited for operations involving large integers, rational numbers, polynomials, and algebraic numbers.
connectionsExplanation: PARI/GP was developed by Henri Cohen and his team at the University of Bordeaux. It is based on the PARI library, which was originally written in C, and the GP calculator, which provides a high-level interface for interactive use. PARI/GP is widely used in academic and research environments for number-theoretic computations.
curious_fact: PARI/GP is named after the French acronym for 'PARI' (Polynôme Arithmétique et Rationnel Itératif), reflecting its focus on polynomial arithmetic.

name: PATH
description: PATH is a programming language developed by John McCarthy and his team at Stanford University. It is a dialect of Lisp and was designed for symbolic computation and artificial intelligence research. PATH introduced several innovations, including a garbage collector and a more efficient interpreter.
connectionsExplanation: PATH is a direct descendant of Lisp, one of the pioneering programming languages developed in the 1950s. It inherits Lisp's symbolic computation capabilities and extends them with improvements in memory management and performance, making it a significant step in the evolution of Lisp-based languages.
curious_fact: PATH was one of the first languages to implement automatic garbage collection in a Lisp dialect.

name: PCASTL
description: PCASTL is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is not intended for production use but serves as a tool for beginners to understand basic programming concepts.
connectionsExplanation: PCASTL was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming. It draws inspiration from various paradigms, aiming to provide a gentle learning curve without the complexities of existing languages.
curious_fact: PCASTL was developed by a team of educators to simplify the learning process for new programmers.

name: PEARL
description: PEARL is a programming language designed for scientific and engineering applications. It was developed at the University of California, Berkeley, as part of the PDP-11 operating system. PEARL is known for its simplicity and ease of use, making it popular among researchers and engineers in the 1970s.
connectionsExplanation: PEARL was developed at the University of California, Berkeley, and was influenced by earlier languages like FORTRAN and ALGOL. It was designed to be a high-level language for scientific and engineering applications, making it a direct descendant of these pioneering languages.
curious_fact: PEARL was one of the first languages to support structured programming concepts like loops and conditionals.

name: PHP
description: PHP is a widely-used open source scripting language that is especially suited for web development and can be embedded into HTML. It was originally created by Rasmus Lerdorf in 1994 and has since evolved into a robust framework for building dynamic websites and web applications.
connectionsExplanation: PHP evolved from the Common Gateway Interface (CGI) scripts written in Perl and C. It was designed to be easy to learn and use, making it a popular choice for web developers. PHP's syntax is similar to that of C, which makes it accessible to developers familiar with C-based languages.

moons.name: Laravel
moons.description: A popular PHP framework known for its elegant syntax and robust features.

moons.name: Symfony
moons.description: A set of PHP components and a full-stack framework for web development.

moons.name: CodeIgniter
moons.description: A lightweight PHP framework that emphasizes simplicity and ease of use.

curious_fact: PHP stands for 'PHP: Hypertext Preprocessor', but the 'P' in PHP originally stood for 'Personal Home Page'.

name: PIC
description: PIC (Peripheral Interface Controller) is a microcontroller architecture developed by Microchip Technology. It is widely used in embedded systems for controlling various electronic devices.
connectionsExplanation: PIC was developed as a microcontroller architecture to provide a cost-effective solution for embedded control systems. It evolved from earlier microcontroller designs and has influenced many subsequent microcontroller architectures.
curious_fact: PIC microcontrollers are known for their low power consumption and high reliability, making them popular in automotive and industrial applications.

name: PILOT
description: PILOT (Programming Language for Instructional Text) is a programming language designed for creating instructional materials. It was developed by IBM in the late 1960s to facilitate the creation of educational software. PILOT is notable for its simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible to non-programmers.
connectionsExplanation: PILOT was developed as part of IBM's efforts to create educational software. It was influenced by the need to simplify programming for the creation of instructional materials, making it a direct descendant of early educational computing efforts.
curious_fact: PILOT was one of the first programming languages designed specifically for educational purposes.

name: PL-11
description: PL-11, also known as PL/11, is a programming language developed by Burroughs Corporation in the 1960s. It was designed to be a high-level language suitable for systems programming and was influenced by ALGOL and COBOL. Despite its early development, PL-11 did not gain widespread adoption and is now considered a historical curiosity.
connectionsExplanation: PL-11 was developed in the 1960s as part of the Burroughs Corporation's efforts to create a versatile high-level programming language for systems programming. It was influenced by the structured programming paradigms emerging at the time, particularly from languages like ALGOL and COBOL. While it did not achieve the same level of popularity as some of its contemporaries, PL-11 remains an interesting chapter in the history of programming languages.
curious_fact: PL-11 was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of nested functions, which allowed for more modular and reusable code.

name: PL-6
description: PL-6, also known as Programming Language 6, is a procedural programming language developed by IBM in the early 1960s. It was designed to be a more advanced version of its predecessor, PL-5, with improvements in syntax and features. PL-6 was part of IBM's efforts to standardize and improve programming languages for business and scientific applications.
connectionsExplanation: PL-6 was developed as an evolution of earlier IBM programming languages, particularly PL-5. It aimed to provide a more structured and efficient way of writing programs, which influenced subsequent languages in the procedural paradigm. The language was part of IBM's broader efforts to standardize programming practices and tools in the 1960s.
curious_fact: PL-6 was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of structured programming, which later became a fundamental aspect of many modern programming languages.

name: PL/0
description: PL/0 is a simple procedural programming language designed by Niklaus Wirth as a pedagogical tool to teach compiler construction. It is a subset of Pascal and is often used in computer science education to introduce concepts of programming languages and compilers.
connectionsExplanation: PL/0 was developed by Niklaus Wirth in 1964 as a simplified version of ALGOL 60. It was created to serve as a teaching language for compiler construction, making it a direct descendant of ALGOL 60 in the context of educational programming languages.
curious_fact: PL/0 was one of the first languages to introduce structured programming concepts, influencing the development of Pascal and other modern programming languages.

name: PL/8
description: PL/8 is a procedural programming language developed by IBM in the 1970s. It was designed for scientific and engineering applications, offering features like structured programming and support for complex data types.
connectionsExplanation: PL/8 evolved from earlier IBM programming languages like PL/I and was part of IBM's efforts to standardize and improve their programming environment for scientific computing.
curious_fact: PL/8 was one of the first languages to support complex data types, including arrays and structures.

name: PL/C
description: PL/C is a programming language developed by IBM in the early 1970s as an extension of the C programming language. It was designed to support the development of operating systems and other system software. PL/C introduced several extensions to C, including support for complex data types and additional control structures.
connectionsExplanation: PL/C evolved from C, which itself was inspired by the B programming language and influenced by BCPL. IBM developed PL/C to enhance C's capabilities for system programming, particularly for operating system development.
curious_fact: PL/C was one of the first languages to introduce complex data types to C, which influenced later versions of C and C++.

name: PL/I
description: PL/I (Programming Language One) is a high-level programming language developed by IBM in the early 1960s. It was designed to be a general-purpose language suitable for scientific, engineering, and business applications. PL/I aimed to combine the ease of use of high-level languages with the efficiency of assembly language.
connectionsExplanation: PL/I was developed as part of IBM's System/360 project, which aimed to create a family of compatible computers. It was influenced by earlier languages like COBOL and FORTRAN, but introduced several innovative features such as block structure and support for both procedural and structured programming paradigms.
curious_fact: PL/I was one of the first languages to support both fixed-point and floating-point arithmetic in a unified manner.

name: PL/Java
description: PL/Java is a procedural language for the Java platform that allows Java code to be executed within the Oracle Database environment. It enables developers to leverage Java's extensive libraries and capabilities directly within database applications.
connectionsExplanation: PL/Java was developed to integrate Java with Oracle databases, allowing Java developers to write stored procedures, functions, and triggers in Java. It builds on the existing Java ecosystem, making it easier for Java developers to work with Oracle databases without needing to learn PL/SQL.
curious_fact: PL/Java was one of the first languages to allow Java to be used directly within Oracle databases, facilitating the use of Java's rich libraries in database applications.

name: PL/Lua
description: PL/Lua is a procedural programming language designed for use in Oracle databases. It is an extension of the Lua programming language, allowing Lua scripts to be executed within Oracle environments. PL/Lua is used for database stored procedures, functions, and triggers, providing a flexible way to integrate Lua's scripting capabilities with Oracle's database management system.
connectionsExplanation: PL/Lua is an extension of Lua, which was developed by Roberto Ierusalimschy, Luiz Henrique de Figueiredo, and Waldemar Celes at the Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro in Brazil. The integration of Lua into Oracle databases as PL/Lua was a strategic move by Oracle to enhance its scripting capabilities, leveraging Lua's lightweight and efficient nature.
curious_fact: PL/Lua was introduced in Oracle 9i and has since been used in various Oracle applications to enhance database functionality with Lua scripting.

name: PL/M
description: PL/M (Programming Language for Microcomputers) is a high-level programming language developed by Burroughs Corporation in 1968. It was designed for writing operating systems and other system software for microcomputers. PL/M is a dialect of PL/I, tailored for the limited resources of early microprocessors.
connectionsExplanation: PL/M evolved from PL/I, a general-purpose programming language developed in the early 1960s. It was specifically designed to address the needs of microcomputer systems, which had limited memory and processing power compared to mainframe computers. The development of PL/M was a significant step in the evolution of programming languages for microcomputers, paving the way for the proliferation of personal computers in the 1980s.
curious_fact: PL/M was used to write the operating system for the Burroughs B5000, one of the first commercial computers to use a high-level language for its operating system.

name: PL/P
description: PL/P is a procedural programming language developed by IBM for use in the System/370 mainframe environment. It was designed to be a high-level language for database applications, providing a way to interact with IBM's DB2 database system.
connectionsExplanation: PL/P was developed as an extension to IBM's PL/I language, specifically tailored for database programming. It was introduced to simplify the process of writing applications that interact with DB2, leveraging the procedural capabilities of PL/I while adding database-specific features.
curious_fact: PL/P was one of the first languages to integrate database operations directly into the language syntax, influencing later database-centric languages.

name: PL/Perl
description: PL/Perl is a procedural language for the PostgreSQL database server that embeds the Perl interpreter. It allows users to write database functions and triggers in Perl, leveraging Perl's extensive libraries and capabilities.
connectionsExplanation: PL/Perl was developed to integrate Perl scripting capabilities directly into PostgreSQL, allowing for more flexible and powerful database operations. It builds on the existing PostgreSQL procedural language framework and Perl's scripting language features.
curious_fact: PL/Perl was one of the first procedural languages to be integrated into PostgreSQL, enabling Perl scripts to be executed within the database environment.

name: PL/Python
description: PL/Python is a procedural language for the PostgreSQL database server that allows users to write database functions and triggers in Python. It integrates Python with PostgreSQL, enabling developers to leverage Python's extensive libraries and capabilities within the database environment.
connectionsExplanation: PL/Python was developed to extend PostgreSQL's functionality by allowing Python scripts to be executed within the database. It builds upon PostgreSQL's extensibility framework, which supports the creation of custom procedural languages. The development of PL/Python was driven by the need to integrate Python's powerful scripting capabilities with PostgreSQL's robust database management features.
curious_fact: PL/Python was one of the first procedural languages to be integrated with PostgreSQL, demonstrating the database's openness to language extensions.

name: PL/R
description: PL/R is a procedural language for the PostgreSQL database that allows users to write functions and triggers in the R programming language. It integrates R's statistical capabilities directly into PostgreSQL, making it a powerful tool for data analysis within the database environment.
connectionsExplanation: PL/R was developed to extend PostgreSQL's functionality by integrating the R programming language, which is widely used for statistical computing and graphics. This integration allows database users to leverage R's powerful data analysis and visualization tools directly within their database operations, enhancing the capabilities of PostgreSQL for data-intensive applications.
curious_fact: PL/R was created by Joe Conway and is one of several extensions that allow PostgreSQL to support other programming languages.

name: PL/SQL
description: PL/SQL (Procedural Language for SQL) is an extension of SQL used for writing stored procedures, functions, packages, and triggers in Oracle Database. It allows for the creation of complex database applications with procedural logic.
connectionsExplanation: PL/SQL was developed by Oracle Corporation as an extension of SQL to include procedural features. It was first released in 1986 and has since become a core component of Oracle's database platform, enabling developers to write complex business logic directly within the database.

moons.name: Oracle Forms
moons.description: A development tool for building graphical user interfaces for Oracle databases.

moons.name: Oracle Reports
moons.description: A tool for generating reports from Oracle databases.

curious_fact: PL/SQL was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of packages, which encapsulate related procedures, functions, and variables.

name: PL/Scheme
description: PL/Scheme is a dialect of the Scheme programming language, designed for educational purposes and as a teaching language for computer science. It is known for its simplicity and clarity, making it a popular choice for introductory programming courses.
connectionsExplanation: PL/Scheme evolved from the original Scheme language, which was developed at MIT in the late 1970s. It inherits the functional programming paradigm and Lisp-like syntax, making it a direct descendant of Scheme.
curious_fact: PL/Scheme was one of the first languages to introduce the concept of tail call optimization, which helps in managing recursion efficiently.

name: PL/Tcl
description: PL/Tcl is a procedural language for the PostgreSQL database server that allows users to write database functions and triggers in Tcl. It provides a way to integrate Tcl scripting capabilities directly into PostgreSQL.
connectionsExplanation: PL/Tcl was developed to extend PostgreSQL's functionality by allowing Tcl scripts to be executed within the database environment. It was introduced in the mid-1990s as part of PostgreSQL's support for multiple procedural languages, which aimed to provide flexibility in database scripting.
curious_fact: PL/Tcl was one of the first procedural languages supported by PostgreSQL, enabling Tcl scripts to be executed directly within the database.

name: PL/X
description: PL/X is a procedural programming language developed by IBM in the 1970s. It was designed to be a high-level language for scientific and engineering applications, offering features like structured programming and support for complex data types.
connectionsExplanation: PL/X was developed as part of IBM's System/370 family of mainframe computers. It was influenced by earlier IBM languages like RPG and COBOL, aiming to provide a more modern and flexible programming environment for scientific computing.
curious_fact: PL/X was one of the first languages to support complex data types and structured programming constructs, making it a significant step forward in the evolution of high-level programming languages.

name: PL/pgSQL
description: PL/pgSQL is a procedural language for the PostgreSQL database system. It allows users to write database functions and triggers in a structured way, enhancing the capabilities of SQL for complex database operations.
connectionsExplanation: PL/pgSQL was developed by the PostgreSQL Global Development Group in 1993. It is an extension of SQL and is tightly integrated with the PostgreSQL database system, allowing for the creation of complex database functions and triggers.
curious_fact: PL/pgSQL is one of the oldest procedural languages for SQL, developed alongside the first versions of PostgreSQL.

name: PL360
description: PL360 is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes, emphasizing simplicity and ease of learning. It is inspired by modern scripting languages but with a unique syntax and structure.
connectionsExplanation: PL360 was created as an educational tool to introduce beginners to programming concepts without overwhelming them with complex syntax. It draws inspiration from the simplicity of BASIC and the flexibility of Python, aiming to bridge the gap between these two paradigms.
curious_fact: PL360 was developed by a team of educators who wanted to create a language that could be learned in a single semester.

name: PLANC
description: PLANC is a modern, high-level programming language designed for rapid application development. It emphasizes simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible to beginners while still offering powerful features for experienced developers.
connectionsExplanation: PLANC was developed as a successor to the popular PLANG language, aiming to address some of the limitations and inefficiencies found in its predecessor. It draws inspiration from modern paradigms such as functional programming and object-oriented design, while also incorporating elements from the imperative style.

moons.name: PLANC-Web
moons.description: A framework for building web applications with PLANC.

moons.name: PLANC-Data
moons.description: A library for data manipulation and analysis in PLANC.

curious_fact: PLANC was created by a team of developers who wanted to simplify the process of writing complex software without sacrificing performance.

name: PLASMA
description: PLASMA is a fictional programming language designed for educational purposes and as a thought experiment in language design. It features a unique syntax and semantics that challenge conventional programming paradigms.
connectionsExplanation: PLASMA was created as an academic exercise to explore alternative programming paradigms and language design principles. It has no direct historical predecessors but draws inspiration from various programming languages of the 1970s, including ALGOL and Lisp.
curious_fact: PLASMA was designed to be as different as possible from existing languages to encourage new ways of thinking about programming.

name: PLEX
description: PLEX is a domain-specific language (DSL) designed for rapid development of data processing applications. It is particularly suited for tasks involving complex data transformations and analysis.

curious_fact: PLEX was one of the first DSLs to support real-time data processing, making it highly valuable in financial and scientific applications.

name: PLEXIL

connectionsExplanation: PLEXIL was developed at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) to address the need for a robust and flexible language for planning and executing complex robotic tasks. It is inspired by the Prolog programming language and is used in conjunction with the PLEXIL Integrated Control System (PICS) to manage the execution of space missions.
curious_fact: PLEXIL was used in the Mars Exploration Rovers mission to plan and execute tasks on the Martian surface.

name: PONIE

It was inspired by the need for a more efficient and standards-compliant implementation of Perl 6, drawing from the experiences and lessons learned from the Perl 5 interpreter.

example:
:- program.
:- modeh((parent(X,Y)),[person(X),person(Y)]).
:- modeb((mother(X,Y)),[person(X),person(Y)]).
:- modeb((father(X,Y)),[person(X),person(Y)]).
:- input(mother(jane,john)).
:- input(father(john,jane)).
:- input(mother(jane,jane)).
:- input(father(john,john)).
:- output(parent(jane,john)).
:- output(parent(john,jane)).

p1 :- write('Hello from p1!').

moons.name: rhdfs
moons.description: rhdfs is an R package that provides an interface to Hadoop's HDFS file system.

moons.name: rmr2
moons.description: rmr2 is an R package that allows R users to write MapReduce jobs in R and run them on Hadoop.

curious_fact: The book 'Programming with Big Data in R' was written by Revolution Analytics, which was later acquired by Microsoft.
example:
library(rmr2)
library(rhdfs)

hdfs.init()

# Example MapReduce job to count words in a text file
word_count <- function(key, val) {
  words <- unlist(strsplit(tolower(val), "\W+"))
  return(keyval(words, rep(1, length(words))))
}

# Run the MapReduce job
results <- mapreduce(input = "hdfs:///path/to/input.txt", map = word_count, reduce = function(key, val) { keyval(key, sum(val)) })

print(results)

name: Pure
description: Pure is a modern, functional programming language designed for simplicity and efficiency. It emphasizes immutability and lazy evaluation, making it suitable for both scripting and larger applications.
connectionsExplanation: Pure is inspired by Haskell and ML, drawing from their functional paradigms. It aims to provide a clean and concise syntax while maintaining performance and ease of use.

moons.name: Pure Libraries
moons.description: A collection of libraries for various domains, including mathematics, graphics, and networking.

curious_fact: Pure is known for its minimalistic approach, with a small core language that can be extended through libraries.

name: PureBasic
description: PureBasic is a high-level programming language designed for Windows, Linux, and macOS. It focuses on simplicity and ease of use, making it accessible for beginners while still offering powerful features for experienced developers.
connectionsExplanation: PureBasic evolved from earlier Basic dialects, particularly emphasizing simplicity and cross-platform capabilities. It was developed by Fantaisie Software in Switzerland, aiming to provide a modern alternative to traditional Basic languages.

moons.name: PureWin
moons.description: A library for Windows API access.

moons.name: Pure3D
moons.description: A library for 3D graphics.

curious_fact: PureBasic is known for its fast compilation times and minimal runtime dependencies.

name: PureScript
description: PureScript is a purely functional programming language that compiles to JavaScript. It aims to provide a type-safe, expressive, and efficient way to write web applications.
connectionsExplanation: PureScript is inspired by Haskell and is part of the ML family of languages. It was created to bring the benefits of functional programming to JavaScript developers, offering a statically typed language that compiles to clean, readable JavaScript.

moons.name: Halogen
moons.description: A virtual DOM library for PureScript, inspired by Elm.

moons.name: purescript-react
moons.description: A library for using React with PureScript.

curious_fact: PureScript's type system is based on Haskell's, but it compiles to JavaScript, making it accessible to web developers.

name: Pxem
description: Pxem is an esoteric programming language created by user3253493 on the Esolangs wiki. It is designed to be minimalistic and uses a unique approach to programming where the code itself is a part of the data.
connectionsExplanation: Pxem is an esoteric language that does not have a direct historical predecessor. It is inspired by the minimalist philosophy of other esoteric languages like Brainfuck and Malbolge, but it introduces its own unique syntax and execution model.
